topics.title: Definitions - Robots, Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET831 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions - Robots, Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Robot

The most widely accepted and academically rigorous definitions of a robot come from international standards bodies. The **ISO 8373:2021** standard (Manipulating industrial robots — Vocabulary) defines a robot as:

> An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications.

The **Robotic Industries Association (RIA)** provides a more concise engineering definition:

> A robot is a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks.

The **Oxford Dictionary** offers a generalized definition:

> A machine capable of carrying out a complex series of actions automatically, especially one programmable by a computer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must remember the **three essential characteristics** that distinguish a robot from a regular machine:
> 1. **Sensing** — Ability to perceive its environment.
> 2. **Decision Making / Intelligence** — Ability to process information and decide.
> 3. **Actuation** — Ability to physically act upon the environment.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Robotics

**Robotics** is the interdisciplinary branch of engineering and science that deals with the design, construction, operation, and application of robots. It integrates:

- **Mechanical Engineering** (mechanical structure, kinematics)
- **Electrical Engineering** (actuators, sensors, circuits)
- **Computer Science** (algorithms, AI, control software)
- **Electronics** (microcontrollers, embedded systems)
- **Cognitive Science** (perception, learning)

> [!NOTE]
> **Academic Definition (Spong et al.):** *"Robotics is the science of designing, implementing, and using robots. It is a highly interdisciplinary field that combines mechanics, electronics, control engineering, and computer science."*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Think of a robot as a "smart mechanical arm with a brain and senses."**

Imagine a human worker in a factory:
- **Eyes** → Robot's **Sensors** (cameras, force sensors, encoders)
- **Brain** → Robot's **Controller / Computer** (processes data, makes decisions)
- **Muscles** → Robot's **Actuators** (motors, hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders)
- **Skeleton** → Robot's **Mechanical Structure** (links, joints, end-effector)
- **Nerves** → Robot's **Wiring & Communication Bus** (signal pathways)

Just as a human combines these subsystems to perform meaningful work, a robot integrates sensors, controllers, and actuators to autonomously execute tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Word Origin:** The term *"robot"* was coined by Czech playwright **Karel Čapek** in his 1920 play *R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)*, derived from the Czech word *"robota"* meaning **forced labor** or **drudgery**.

### 1.4 Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics (Historical Foundation)

Although fictional, Isaac Asimov's 1942 laws form the ethical foundation of modern robotics:

1. A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
2. A robot must obey orders given by human beings except where such orders conflict with the First Law.
3. A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Robot Definition

Let us break down the ISO/RIA definition into structured, logical components:

- **Automatically Controlled:** The robot operates without continuous human intervention once programmed. The control loop is closed internally.
- **Reprogrammable:** The task sequence can be modified without physical alteration — only software changes are needed.
- **Multipurpose:** A single robot can perform multiple tasks (unlike a single-purpose machine like a dedicated screw-tightening rig).
- **Manipulator:** The robot possesses a mechanical arm-like structure (or mobile platform) that interacts with the physical world.
- **Three or More Axes:** Refers to the **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — a robot must have a minimum of 3 DOF to position in 3D space.
- **Variable Programmed Motions:** The motion path is not fixed; it is defined by software.

### 2.2 Structural Components of Any Robot

Every robot, regardless of type, consists of five fundamental subsystems:

| Subsystem | Function | Examples |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Mechanical Structure** | Physical body, links, joints | Aluminum arm, carbon-fiber chassis |
| **Actuators** | Convert energy to motion | Servo motors, stepper motors, hydraulic pistons |
| **Sensors** | Perceive internal/external state | Encoders, LIDAR, cameras, force/torque sensors |
| **Controller** | Brain — processes data, generates commands | PLC, microcontroller, industrial PC |
| **Power Supply** | Energy source | Battery, mains electricity, pneumatic compressor |

### 2.3 The Sense-Plan-Act (SPA) Paradigm

Modern robotics follows the **SPA paradigm**, a conceptual model for robot intelligence:

- **Sense:** Acquire data from sensors (vision, touch, proprioception).
- **Plan:** Process the data, build a world model, and decide on an action sequence.
- **Act:** Execute the planned motion via actuators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The transition from rigid automation to intelligent robotics is measured by the **Level of Autonomy**, typically classified into **6 levels (0 to 5)** similar to autonomous vehicles.

### 2.4 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — The Key Metric

The **Degrees of Freedom** of a robot is the number of independent parameters that define its configuration in space.

- **Position in 3D space:** 3 DOF (x, y, z)
- **Orientation in 3D space:** 3 DOF (roll, pitch, yaw)
- **Total for a free-flying body:** **6 DOF** (3 translation + 3 rotation)

A robot manipulator typically has **6 DOF** to reach any point with any orientation.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Expression | Description | Unit |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|------|
| Degrees of Freedom | $n$ | Number of independent motions | dimensionless |
| Total DOF (spatial) | $DOF = 3T + 3R$ | T = translations, R = rotations | dimensionless |
| Gruebler's Formula (planar) | $DOF = 3(N-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$ | N = links, $J_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $J_2$ = 2-DOF joints | dimensionless |
| Gruebler's Formula (spatial) | $DOF = 6(N-1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5$ | General spatial mechanism | dimensionless |
| Workspace Volume | $V_w$ | Total reachable space by end-effector | $\text{m}^3$ |
| Repeatability | $R_p$ | Precision of returning to a point | $\text{mm}$ or $\mu\text{m}$ |
| Accuracy | $A_c$ | Closeness to commanded position | $\text{mm}$ |
| Payload | $P$ | Maximum load the robot can carry | $\text{kg}$ or $\text{N}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The DOF formula is used in KTU problems to determine if a robot arm is capable of performing a required task (e.g., welding a 3D seam requires ≥ 5 DOF).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The definitions form the foundation for:
- **Industrial Automation:** Selecting robots for assembly lines (payload, reach, DOF).
- **Medical Robotics:** Surgical robots (e.g., Da Vinci) require 6–7 DOF.
- **Space Exploration:** NASA's Robonaut and Mars rovers use SPA paradigm.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Direct application of sensing-planning-acting with 6 DOF body.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Standards-Based Definitions

This matrix is a high-yield KTU analysis tool for comparing how different bodies define a robot:

| Standards Body | Year | Key Terms Used | Scope | KTU Significance |
|----------------|------|----------------|-------|-------------------|
| **ISO 8373** | 2021 | Automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose, 3+ axes | Industrial robots | Most cited in KTU exams |
| **RIA** | 1980s | Reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator | Industrial robots | Foundation of ISO 8373 |
| **JIRA (Japan)** | 1970s | All-purpose, autonomous, intelligent | Industrial robots | Historical context |
| **Oxford Dictionary** | Generic | Complex series of actions automatically | All robots | Broad/general |
| **Asimov (Fictional)** | 1942 | Law-abiding, intelligent machine | Ethical/AI robots | Ethics question |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Degrees of Freedom of a Spatial Manipulator

A spatial robot arm with $N$ links connected by joints of varying types has a DOF given by **Kutzbach-Gruebler's equation** for spatial mechanisms:

$$
DOF = 6(N - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{5} (6 - f_i) J_i
$$

**Step 1:** Identify the number of links.

For a standard 6-DOF industrial arm (like the PUMA 560), $N = 7$ (6 links + 1 ground/base link).

**Step 2:** Identify joint types.

- 6 revolute joints, each with $f_i = 1$ DOF.
- So $J_1 = 6$ (since each revolute joint removes $6 - 1 = 5$ DOF).

**Step 3:** Substitute into the equation.

$$
DOF = 6(7 - 1) - (6 - 1)(6) - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0
$$

**Step 4:** Expand the bracket.

$$
DOF = 6(6) - 5(6)
$$

$$
DOF = 36 - 30
$$

$$
DOF = 6
$$

**Result:** The PUMA 560 has **6 DOF**, which matches its design intent of reaching any point in 3D space with any orientation.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Breakdown: How "Robotics" is Derived as a Discipline

**Step 1:** Identify the **root discipline** — Mechanical Engineering (mechanism design).

**Step 2:** Add **actuation** — Electrical Engineering (motors, drives).

**Step 3:** Add **perception** — Electronics & Instrumentation (sensors, signal conditioning).

**Step 4:** Add **intelligence** — Computer Science (algorithms, AI, control theory).

**Step 5:** Add **interaction** — Cognitive Science & HCI (human-robot interaction).

**Conclusion:** Robotics is the **convergence discipline** that emerges from the union of these five fields.

$$
\text{Robotics} = \text{Mechanical} \cup \text{Electrical} \cup \text{Electronics} \cup \text{CS} \cup \text{Cognitive}
$$

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Classification: Robot Categories

**Step 1:** Classify by **application domain** → Industrial vs. Service vs. Mobile vs. Humanoid.

**Step 2:** Classify by **kinematic structure** → Serial (open chain) vs. Parallel (closed chain).

**Step 3:** Classify by **mobility** → Fixed-base vs. Wheeled vs. Legged vs. Aerial vs. Underwater.

**Step 4:** Classify by **control method** → Servo-controlled vs. Non-servo (open-loop) vs. Hybrid.

**Step 5:** Classify by **intelligence level** → Manipulator (no intelligence) → Programmed → Adaptive → Intelligent (AI-driven).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robot

The following Mermaid flowchart shows how a robot's subsystems interact in the **Sense-Plan-Act (SPA)** paradigm:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Environment] -->|Stimuli| B[Sensors]
    B -->|Raw Data| C[Controller]
    C -->|Processed Info| D[Planner]
    D -->|Action Commands| E[Actuators]
    E -->|Physical Motion| F[Mechanical Structure]
    F -->|Interaction| A
    C -.->|Feedback Loop| E
    G[Power Supply] -.->|Energy| E
    G -.->|Energy| B
    G -.->|Energy| C
```

### 4.2 Robot Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Robots] --> A[By Application]
    R --> B[By Kinematics]
    R --> C[By Mobility]
    A --> A1[Industrial]
    A --> A2[Service]
    A --> A3[Medical]
    A --> A4[Space]
    B --> B1[Serial Manipulator]
    B --> B2[Parallel Manipulator]
    B --> B3[Hybrid]
    C --> C1[Fixed Base]
    C --> C2[Wheeled Mobile]
    C --> C3[Legged]
    C --> C4[Aerial Drone]
    C --> C5[Underwater AUV]
```

### 4.3 SPA Paradigm Detailed Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sense["SENSE Phase"]
        S1[Camera Vision]
        S2[Force Torque Sensor]
        S3[Encoder Position]
        S4[LIDAR Proximity]
    end
    subgraph Plan["PLAN Phase"]
        P1[Data Fusion]
        P2[World Model]
        P3[Path Planning]
        P4[Decision Algorithm]
    end
    subgraph Act["ACT Phase"]
        AC1[Motor Commands]
        AC2[Servo Drive]
        AC3[Hydraulic Valve]
    end
    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P1
    S3 --> P1
    S4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> AC1
    P4 --> AC2
    P4 --> AC3
```

### 4.4 Robotics as a Convergence Discipline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Mechanical Engineering] --> RB[Robotics]
    E[Electrical Engineering] --> RB
    EL[Electronics] --> RB
    CS[Computer Science] --> RB
    CG[Cognitive Science] --> RB
    RB --> APP[Real World Applications]
    APP --> IND[Industrial Automation]
    APP --> MED[Medical Surgery]
    APP --> SPACE[Space Exploration]
    APP --> DEF[Defense]
    APP --> AGR[Agriculture]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every robot, no matter how simple or complex, can be mapped onto the Sense-Plan-Act architecture. Even a basic pick-and-place arm has sensing (limit switches), planning (microcontroller logic), and actuation (motors).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a robot as per ISO 8373. List the three essential characteristics of a robot.*

**Model Answer:**

As per **ISO 8373:2021**, a robot is defined as:

> "An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."

**Three essential characteristics:**
1. **Sensing** — Perceives the environment via sensors.
2. **Decision Making** — Processes information and decides actions.
3. **Actuation** — Executes physical actions through motors/actuators.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between a robot and robotics with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Robot | Robotics |
|--------|-------|----------|
| Definition | A physical machine capable of performing tasks autonomously | The science/field of designing, building, and using robots |
| Nature | Tangible (hardware) | Intangible (discipline) |
| Example | ABB IRB 120 industrial arm | The study of kinematics, dynamics, AI applied to robots |
| Scope | Single machine | Multi-disciplinary field |

A **robot** is the *product*; **robotics** is the *engineering discipline* that creates the product.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the various components/subsystems of an industrial robot. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An industrial robot consists of **five major subsystems**:

1. **Mechanical Structure (Manipulator):** Comprises links, joints, and the end-effector. Links are rigid bodies connected by joints (revolute or prismatic). The end-effector (gripper, welding torch) interacts with the workpiece.

2. **Actuators:** Devices that convert energy into motion. Common types:
   - **Electric motors** (servo, stepper) — precise, clean, used in most modern robots.
   - **Hydraulic actuators** — high payload, used in heavy industry.
   - **Pneumatic actuators** — fast, low cost, used in simple pick-and-place.

3. **Sensors:** Two categories:
   - *Internal (proprioceptive):* Encoders, resolvers — measure joint angles/speeds.
   - *External (exteroceptive):* Cameras, force/torque sensors, proximity sensors — perceive environment.

4. **Controller:** The "brain" of the robot. Contains:
   - CPU/Microprocessor
   - Memory (RAM/ROM)
   - Communication interfaces
   - Servo drives
   It executes the program, processes sensor data, and generates control signals.

5. **Power Supply Unit:** Provides regulated power (electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic) to all subsystems.

**(b)** Explain the Sense-Plan-Act (SPA) paradigm in robotics with a block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **SPA paradigm** is the foundational model of robot intelligence, dividing robot operation into three phases:

**1. Sense Phase:**
The robot acquires data from its sensors. Example: A vision camera captures an image of an object on a conveyor; an encoder reports the current arm position.

**2. Plan Phase:**
The controller processes the sensor data. It:
- Fuses data from multiple sensors.
- Builds a world model.
- Plans a collision-free trajectory.
- Decides the next action (e.g., "move arm to object's coordinates").

**3. Act Phase:**
The controller sends command signals to the actuators. The motors move the joints, the end-effector grasps the object, and the task is executed.

**Feedback Loop:** Continuous sensing during action allows the robot to correct errors in real-time (closed-loop control).

**Block Diagram (in words):**

$$
\text{Environment} \rightarrow \text{Sensors} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Actuators} \rightarrow \text{Mechanical Structure} \rightarrow \text{Environment}
$$

A reference to the Mermaid SPA diagram in Section 4.3 is recommended for full marks.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Define Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a robot. A 6-DOF industrial manipulator has 7 links. Calculate its DOF using Kutzbach-Gruebler's equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot is the minimum number of independent parameters required to completely specify the configuration of the robot in space.

**Calculation:**

Given: $N = 7$ links, all joints are revolute ($f_i = 1$, $J_1 = 6$).

Kutzbach-Gruebler spatial equation:

$$
DOF = 6(N - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{5} (6 - f_i) J_i
$$

Substitute:

$$
DOF = 6(7 - 1) - (6 - 1)(6)
$$

$$
DOF = 6 \times 6 - 5 \times 6
$$

$$
DOF = 36 - 30 = 6
$$

**Result:** The robot has **6 DOF**, which is the standard for full spatial positioning and orientation.

**(b)** Discuss in detail Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics and their relevance in modern AI-driven robotics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Isaac Asimov introduced his **Three Laws of Robotics** in his 1942 short story *"Runaround"*. They are:

**Law 1 (Non-Harm):** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.

**Law 2 (Obedience):** A robot must obey orders given by human beings, except where such orders conflict with the First Law.

**Law 3 (Self-Preservation):** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.

**Relevance in Modern AI-Driven Robotics:**

1. **Ethical Foundation:** Asimov's laws remain the cornerstone of **robot ethics** (roboethics) and influence contemporary frameworks like the EU's AI Act and ISO 13482 (personal care robots).

2. **Autonomous Vehicles:** Self-driving cars directly apply Law 1 — algorithms must prioritize human safety in collision scenarios (e.g., pedestrian vs. passenger).

3. **Medical Robotics:** Surgical robots (Da Vinci) must obey surgeon commands (Law 2) while ensuring no harm to the patient (Law 1).

4. **Limitations in Modern AI:** Modern AI systems (LLMs, generative models) do not inherently follow these laws — they require explicit programming, RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback), or regulatory oversight.

5. **Industrial Robots:** Factory robots use **safety cages, light curtains, and emergency stops** — practical implementations of Law 1.

6. **Future of Human-Robot Interaction:** As humanoid and social robots become common, Asimov's laws guide design of **trust, transparency, and accountability** systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing "Robot" with "Robotics":** Many students use the terms interchangeably. Always clarify: robot = machine; robotics = field of study.
> 2. **Forgetting the year of ISO 8373:** Examiners award half-marks if the standard year (2021) is missing.
> 3. **Skipping DOF units:** DOF is dimensionless — do not write "meters" or "radians."
> 4. **Omitting Asimov's year (1942):** Frequently asked in Part A. Memorize: *1942 — "Runaround".*
> 5. **In SPA diagrams, missing the feedback loop:** A one-way arrow loses 2 marks. Always show the **closed-loop** from actuators back to sensors.
> 6. **Conflating "accuracy" and "repeatability":** Accuracy is closeness to *commanded* position; repeatability is closeness to *previously taught* position. Industrial robots are highly repeatable but only moderately accurate.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Robot (ISO 8373):** *Automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator with 3+ axes.*
- **Robotics:** The interdisciplinary field of designing, building, and using robots.
- **Three Characteristics:** Sense, Plan, Act.
- **Five Subsystems:** Mechanical structure, Actuators, Sensors, Controller, Power Supply.
- **SPA Paradigm:** Sense → Plan → Act (with continuous feedback loop).
- **DOF (Spatial):** $DOF = 6(N-1) - \sum (6-f_i)J_i$
- **Standard industrial arm:** 6 DOF (3 position + 3 orientation).
- **Asimov's Laws (1942):** Non-harm → Obedience → Self-preservation (in priority order).
- **First robot:** Čapek's *R.U.R.* (1920); term *robota* = forced labor.
- **Accuracy vs. Repeatability:** Different metrics; both expressed in mm.
- **Robot classification axes:** Application, Kinematics, Mobility, Control, Intelligence.
- **Convergence disciplines:** Mechanical + Electrical + Electronics + CS + Cognitive.
- **Level of Autonomy:** Ranges from 0 (manual) to 5 (fully autonomous).
- **Workspace:** Defined by reach and joint limits; can be *reachable* or *dexterous*.
- **End-effector:** The tool at the robot's tip (gripper, welder, sprayer).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Robots - Manipulators, Mobile Robots - Wheeled & Legged Robots, Aerial Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET831 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Robots - Manipulators, Mobile Robots - Wheeled & Legged Robots, Aerial Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Robot? (Foundation)

A **robot** is a reprogrammable, multifunctional, electromechanical device designed to perform tasks autonomously or semi-autonomously through programmed commands, sensors, actuators, and a control system. The word originates from the Czech *robota*, meaning "forced labor."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (ISO 8373:2021 Alignment):**
> An **industrial robot** is an *automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes*, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Robots (Big Picture)

Robots are broadly classified based on their **operational environment**, **mechanical structure**, and **degrees of freedom (DOF)**. The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) categorizes them primarily into:

1. **Manipulators** (stationary robotic arms)
2. **Mobile Robots** — sub-divided into:
   - **Wheeled Robots**
   - **Legged Robots**
3. **Aerial Robots** (Unmanned Aerial Vehicles — UAVs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Robot Family Tree:**
> Think of robots like vehicles:
> - A **Manipulator** is like a *human arm bolted to a workbench* — strong, precise, but cannot move its base.
> - A **Wheeled Robot** is like a *car* — fast and efficient on smooth terrain.
> - A **Legged Robot** is like a *human or animal* — slow but capable of traversing rough terrain.
> - An **Aerial Robot** is like a *bird or helicopter* — operates in 3D airspace.

---

## 1.3 Manipulators (Robotic Arms)

A **manipulator** is a mechanically constructed arm-like structure consisting of a series of **rigid links** connected by **revolute (R)** or **prismatic (P)** joints, anchored to a fixed base. It is the most common type of robot used in manufacturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):**
> The **DOF** of a manipulator is the **number of independent positional variables** required to completely specify the configuration of the mechanism in space.
> - In 3D Cartesian space, a rigid body has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
> - Industrial manipulators typically have **4 to 7 DOF**.

### Common Manipulator Configurations (KTU High-Yield)

| Configuration | Joint Sequence | Workspace Shape | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian (PPP) | 3 Prismatic | Rectangular cuboid | Pick-and-place, CNC loading |
| Cylindrical (RPP) | 1 Revolute + 2 Prismatic | Hollow cylinder | Assembly, machine tending |
| Spherical/Polar (RRP) | 2 Revolute + 1 Prismatic | Sphere sector | Welding, painting |
| Articulated (RRR) | 3 Revolute | Spherical | Welding, complex assembly |
| SCARA (RRP) | 2 Revolute + 1 Prismatic | Flat disc | PCB assembly, packaging |
| Parallel (e.g., Delta) | Multiple closed-loop | Dome / flat | High-speed pick-and-place |

---

## 1.4 Mobile Robots — Wheeled Robots

A **wheeled mobile robot (WMR)** is a robot whose locomotion is achieved through motorized wheels in contact with the ground. It is characterized by high efficiency on smooth, flat terrain and simple mechanical design.

### Wheel Types (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

- **Standard wheel** — fixed orientation, rotation about axle
- **Caster wheel** — free pivot + free rotation (passive support)
- **Swedish wheel** — passive rotational rollers at 45°/90° to main axis (omnidirectional)
- **Ball/Spherical wheel** — omnidirectional point contact

### Wheel Arrangement / Drive Types

| Drive Type | Wheels | Maneuverability | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Drive | 2 driven + 1 caster | Zero turning radius | Pioneer, Roomba |
| Ackermann Steering | 2 fixed + 2 steered | Car-like (minimum turning radius) | Autonomous cars |
| Synchronous Drive | All wheels driven & steered | Same orientation always | Some indoor robots |
| Omni-directional | 3 or 4 Swedish/Ball wheels | Move in any planar direction | KUKA youBot |
| Tracked (Sub-type) | Continuous tracks | High traction, rough terrain | Military/tank robots |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — Why Different Drives?**
> - **Differential drive** is like a *wheelchair* — easy to spin in place but cannot go sideways.
> - **Ackermann** is like a *car* — fast and stable on roads, but needs space to turn.
> - **Omnidirectional** is like a *hovercraft* — can glide in any direction without rotating.

---

## 1.5 Mobile Robots — Legged Robots

A **legged mobile robot** uses articulated limbs (legs) for locomotion. Each leg consists of a chain of links and powered joints (typically revolute), mimicking biological walkers.

### Classification by Number of Legs

| # Legs | Name | Static Stability? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Monopod | No (dynamic only) | Hopping robots (CMU 1983) |
| 2 | Biped | No (dynamic) | Honda ASIMO, Boston Dynamics Atlas |
| 4 | Quadruped | Possible (3 legs on ground) | Boston Dynamics Spot, ANYmal |
| 6 | Hexapod | Yes (always) | Most insects (e.g., crawling beetle) |
| 8 | Octopod | Yes (always) | Some research spiders |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept — Static vs Dynamic Stability:**
> - **Static stability**: Robot does not fall even when motion is paused. Requires ≥3 legs on the ground AND the **center of gravity (CoG)** projection to lie within the **support polygon** formed by contact feet.
> - **Dynamic stability**: Robot can fall when paused but is balanced during motion (e.g., human walking, hopping).

---

## 1.6 Aerial Robots (UAVs / Drones)

An **aerial robot**, commonly known as an **Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV)** or **drone**, is a flying machine that operates without an onboard human pilot. It carries its own flight controller, sensors, and (often) a payload.

### Classification by Wing/Rotor Type

| Type | Lift Mechanism | Hover? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fixed-wing | Forward airspeed over wings | No | Survey drones, military UAVs |
| Single-rotor (Helicopter) | 1 main + 1 tail rotor | Yes | Yamaha RMAX |
| Multi-rotor | 3, 4, 6, or 8 rotors | Yes | Quadcopter (DJI Phantom), Hexacopter |
| Flapping-wing (Ornithopter) | Flapping motion | Limited | RoboBee, DelFly |
| Lighter-than-air | Buoyancy (helium) | Yes (passive) | Surveillance blimps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — Lift Mechanisms:**
> - **Fixed-wing** = *airplane* — efficient over long distances but needs a runway or launch.
> - **Multi-rotor** = *helicopter* — can hover but consumes more energy.
> - **Flapping-wing** = *bird* — bio-inspired; currently limited to micro-scale.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Section 1 — Key Takeaways for KTU:**
> - Robots are classified by **environment** (industrial, ground, aerial, underwater).
> - **Manipulators** are stationary; **mobile robots** have a moving base; **aerial robots** fly.
> - Each type has **trade-offs** in mobility, stability, energy, and workspace.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manipulators — Degrees of Freedom (Gruebler's Formula)

The **degrees of mobility** of a planar or spatial mechanism can be calculated using **Gruebler's equation**. For a manipulator:

### Gruebler's Formula (Planar):

$$
\text{DOF} = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

### Gruebler's Formula (Spatial):

$$
\text{DOF} = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

Where:
- $n$ = total number of links (including ground)
- $j_1$ = number of lower-pair joints (1 DOF each — revolute, prismatic)
- $j_2$ = number of joints with 2 DOF
- $j_3, j_4, j_5$ = joints with 3, 4, 5 DOF respectively

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Gruebler's formula lets you predict how many independent inputs (actuators) are needed to fully control a manipulator. For a 6-DOF industrial arm, you need exactly 6 actuators.

### Workspace Definitions

- **Dexterous workspace**: The set of points the end-effector can reach with **all orientations**.
- **Reachable workspace**: The set of points the end-effector can reach with **at least one orientation**.
- Always: **Dexterous ⊆ Reachable**.

---

## 2.2 Wheeled Mobile Robots — Kinematic Analysis

### 2.2.1 Differential Drive Robot

Two independently driven wheels on a common axis. Robot state: $(x, y, \theta)$.

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

Where:
- $v$ = linear velocity of robot center
- $\omega$ = angular velocity
- $r$ = wheel radius
- $L$ = distance between wheels (wheelbase)
- $\omega_R, \omega_L$ = angular velocities of right and left wheels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it works:** When both wheels spin the same way → robot moves straight. When wheels spin opposite ways → robot rotates about its center (zero-radius turn).

### 2.2.2 Turning Radius (Ackermann)

For a car-like (Ackermann) robot:

$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan(\phi)}
$$

Where:
- $R$ = turning radius
- $L$ = wheelbase (distance between front and rear axles)
- $\phi$ = steering angle of the front wheels

> [!NOTE]
> **Special cases:**
> - $\phi = 0°$ → $R = \infty$ (straight line motion)
> - $\phi = 90°$ → $R = 0$ (pivot turn — but mechanically impossible for Ackermann)
> - Larger $L$ → larger minimum turning radius

### 2.2.3 Static Stability for Wheeled Robots

To prevent tipping, the **center of gravity (CoG)** must lie inside the **support polygon** (formed by the wheel-ground contact points).

For a 4-wheeled rectangular robot:

$$
\frac{w}{2h} > \tan(\alpha_{max})
$$

Where:
- $w$ = width between left and right wheels
- $h$ = height of CoG above ground
- $\alpha_{max}$ = maximum tilt angle before tip-over

---

## 2.3 Legged Robots — Gait and Stability Analysis

### 2.3.1 Static Stability Margin (SSM)

The **static stability margin** is the **shortest distance** from the vertical projection of the CoG to any edge of the support polygon.

$$
\text{SSM} = \min_{i} d(\text{CoG}_{\text{proj}}, \text{edge}_i)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For static stability:** SSM must be **> 0** at all times during the gait.

### 2.3.2 Number of Legs for Static Stability

Let:
- $n$ = total number of legs
- $k$ = number of legs simultaneously lifted during one step

Condition for static stability with all combinations:

$$
n - k \geq 3
$$

For **maximum gait efficiency**, lift only **1 leg at a time** ($k = 1$):

$$
n \geq 4
$$

So **quadrupeds, hexapods, and octopods** can use statically stable gaits; **bipeds cannot** (they are dynamically stable).

### 2.3.3 Common Gaits

| Gait | Sequence (for 6-legged) | Speed | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tripod | L1-R2-L3 → R1-L2-R3 | Fast | Stable |
| Wave | Lift one leg at a time | Slow | Very stable |
| Ripple | Two-tripod variations | Medium | Stable |

For a quadruped:
- **Walk**: Lift one leg at a time (LF → RF → RH → LH)
- **Trot**: Diagonal pairs move together (LF+RH, RF+LH)
- **Pace**: Lateral pairs (LF+RF, LH+RH)
- **Gallop/Bound**: Both front or both hind legs together

### 2.3.4 Stride Length and Duty Factor

$$
\beta = \frac{\text{time foot on ground}}{\text{cycle time}}
$$

For static walking: $\beta > 0.5$ (always more than half the legs touching).

---

## 2.4 Aerial Robots — Fundamentals of Flight

### 2.4.1 Quadcopter Dynamics

A quadcopter has **4 motors** arranged in a plus (×) configuration. Vertical motion is controlled by **collective thrust**:

$$
T = \sum_{i=1}^{4} T_i
$$

Rotation about each axis by **differential thrust**:
- **Roll** ($\phi$): difference between left and right motor pairs
- **Pitch** ($\theta$): difference between front and rear motor pairs
- **Yaw** ($\psi$): difference between CW and CCW rotating rotors

### 2.4.2 Hover Condition

For vertical hover, thrust must equal weight:

$$
T = mg
$$

Where:
- $m$ = total mass (kg)
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ (gravitational acceleration)

### 2.4.3 Endurance vs. Payload Trade-off

$$
E_{hover} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot t_{hover}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{prop}}
$$

Where:
- $E_{hover}$ = battery energy required
- $t_{hover}$ = desired hover time
- $\eta_{motor}, \eta_{prop}$ = motor and propeller efficiencies

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This relation explains why **quadcopters have low endurance (~25 min)** compared to **fixed-wing UAVs (~2 hours)** — fixed wings generate lift passively (aerodynamic), while multi-rotors must continuously spin rotors (energy-intensive).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Topic | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Gruebler (planar) | $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | DOF of planar mechanism |
| Gruebler (spatial) | $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | DOF of spatial mechanism |
| Diff. drive linear vel. | $v = \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ | Forward speed |
| Diff. drive angular vel. | $\omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ | Rotation rate |
| Ackermann radius | $R = \dfrac{L}{\tan(\phi)}$ | Turning radius |
| Static stability | $\dfrac{w}{2h} > \tan(\alpha_{max})$ | No tip-over condition |
| Legged stability | $n - k \geq 3$ | Legs on ground ≥ 3 |
| Duty factor | $\beta = \dfrac{t_{ground}}{t_{cycle}}$ | Fraction of contact time |
| Hover thrust | $T = mg$ | Lift = weight |
| Hover energy | $E_{hover} = \dfrac{mgt}{\eta_{motor}\eta_{prop}}$ | Battery energy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants (must memorize for KTU):**
> - $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
> - 1 revolution $= 2\pi$ rad
> - Air density at sea level $\rho \approx 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^3$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Robot Type | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| Manipulators | Automotive welding (e.g., FANUC in Tesla plant), surgical robots (Da Vinci), PCB assembly |
| Wheeled Mobile | Warehouse automation (Amazon Kiva), planetary rovers (NASA Curiosity), AGVs |
| Legged Mobile | Search & rescue, forest inspection, last-mile delivery, stair-climbing service robots |
| Aerial | Aerial photography, precision agriculture, defense reconnaissance, package delivery (Amazon Prime Air) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential Drive Kinematics

### Step 1 — Define the Robot Configuration

Let the robot center be at point $(x, y)$ with orientation angle $\theta$ (measured from x-axis). The wheel separation (track width) is $L$, and each wheel has radius $r$.

The instantaneous velocities of the left and right wheels along the ground are:

$$
v_L = r\omega_L \qquad v_R = r\omega_R
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Linear Velocity of the Robot Center

The robot center lies midway between the two wheels. Its linear velocity is the **average** of the two wheel linear velocities:

$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2}
$$

$$
v = \frac{r\omega_L + r\omega_R}{2}
$$

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}
$$

> **Logic:** The center is geometrically the midpoint, so its velocity is the arithmetic mean of the two wheel velocities.

### Step 3 — Compute Angular Velocity

The robot rotates about the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**. For a differential drive, when $\omega_L = -\omega_R$, the ICR lies at the robot center (zero-radius turn). In general, the angular velocity is derived from the **difference** in wheel velocities divided by the wheel separation:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{r\omega_R - r\omega_L}{L}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

### Step 4 — Full Kinematic Model

Combining linear and angular velocities into the pose update equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v\cos\theta = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\cos\theta \\[4pt]
\dot{y} &= v\sin\theta = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\sin\theta \\[4pt]
\dot{\theta} &= \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The x and y velocities are projections of the linear velocity $v$ onto the global axes; the heading rate $\dot{\theta}$ is independent of orientation.

### Step 5 — Special Case Verification

- **Straight motion** ($\omega_R = \omega_L = \omega_0$): $v = r\omega_0$, $\omega = 0$ → Robot moves in a straight line. ✅
- **Pure rotation** ($\omega_R = -\omega_L = \omega_0$): $v = 0$, $\omega = \dfrac{2r\omega_0}{L}$ → Robot spins in place. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ackermann Turning Radius

### Step 1 — Geometry of a Car-Like Robot

Consider a 4-wheeled vehicle with wheelbase $L$ (distance between front and rear axles) and steering angle $\phi$ applied to the front wheels. As the vehicle turns, all wheels must rotate about a **common Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** to avoid skidding (Ackermann condition).

### Step 2 — Triangle of Motion

From the ICR to the rear axle, the distance is $R_{rear}$. From the ICR to the front axle, the distance is $R_{front}$. These differ by the wheelbase:

$$
R_{front}^2 = R_{rear}^2 + L^2
$$

But for small angles, $R_{front} \approx R_{rear} \approx R$ (approximately equal at low speeds). The perpendicular from the ICR to the vehicle's longitudinal axis gives the geometric relation:

$$
\tan(\phi) = \frac{L}{R}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Turning Radius

$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan(\phi)}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify Limiting Cases

- $\phi \to 0$: $R \to \infty$ → Straight line. ✅
- $\phi = 45°$: $R = L$ → Tight but realistic turn. ✅
- $\phi \to 90°$: $R \to 0$ → Theoretically pivot, but mechanical limits prevent this. ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Static Stability of a 4-Wheeled Robot

### Step 1 — Setup

Place a 4-wheeled robot of width $w$ (left-right wheel distance) on a tilted surface inclined at angle $\alpha$. The CoG is at height $h$ above the ground, centered horizontally.

### Step 2 — Condition for No Tipping

When tilted by $\alpha$, the CoG shifts horizontally by $h \sin(\alpha)$. The maximum allowable shift before tipping over the outer wheel edge is $w/2$ (half the wheelbase width).

For stability, the horizontal CoG shift must be **less than** the half-width:

$$
h \sin(\alpha) < \frac{w}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Maximum Safe Tilt Angle

$$
\sin(\alpha_{max}) < \frac{w}{2h}
$$

For small angles, $\sin(\alpha) \approx \tan(\alpha)$:

$$
\tan(\alpha_{max}) < \frac{w}{2h}
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
\alpha_{max} = \arctan\left(\frac{w}{2h}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example

If $w = 0.6 \, \text{m}$ and $h = 0.3 \, \text{m}$:

$$
\alpha_{max} = \arctan\left(\frac{0.6}{2 \times 0.3}\right) = \arctan(1) = 45°
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A robot with a **wide, low** base is far more stable. This is why Mars rovers (Curiosity) have a low, wide wheelbase compared to a tall humanoid robot.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Minimum Legs for Static Stability

### Step 1 — Problem Setup

A legged robot must remain stable when transitioning legs. Suppose the robot has $n$ legs and during one step, $k$ legs are simultaneously lifted (in the air).

### Step 2 — Requirement

For the robot to remain **statically stable**, the remaining legs on the ground must form a non-degenerate support polygon, which requires at least **3 non-collinear points**:

$$
n - k \geq 3
$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Efficiency

For smooth motion, we want to lift as few legs as possible. The minimum value of $k$ is **1** (lift one leg at a time).

Substituting $k = 1$:

$$
n - 1 \geq 3 \implies n \geq 4
$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion

A statically stable walking robot must have **at least 4 legs**.

> **Why bipeds need dynamic stability:** Humans have only 2 legs, so $n - k = 1 < 3$ — we would fall over if we stopped mid-stride. Instead, we use **zero-moment point (ZMP)** control to maintain dynamic balance during walking.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Quadcopter Hover Thrust and Motor Sizing

### Step 1 — Force Balance at Hover

For vertical hover with no vertical acceleration:

$$
\sum F_z = 0
$$

The four motor thrusts (each $T_i$) acting upward must equal the gravitational force (weight) acting downward:

$$
T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 = mg
$$

For symmetric design where all motors produce equal thrust $T$:

$$
4T = mg \implies T = \frac{mg}{4}
$$

### Step 2 — Numerical Example

A quadcopter of mass $m = 1.5 \, \text{kg}$:

$$
T = \frac{1.5 \times 9.81}{4} = \frac{14.715}{4} = 3.679 \, \text{N}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Each motor must be capable of producing at least **3.68 N** of thrust. With a 50% safety margin, designers specify motors rated for ≥ 5.5 N.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Manipulator | Wheeled Mobile | Legged Mobile | Aerial (Multi-rotor) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Base mobility | None (fixed) | High (planar) | High (rough terrain) | Full 3D |
| Typical DOF | 4–7 | 3 (x, y, θ) | 12–18 (per leg) | 6 (x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw) |
| Energy efficiency | Moderate | High | Low | Lowest |
| Stability | Static (inherent) | Static (geometric) | Static/Dynamic | Dynamic (active control) |
| Main sensors | Encoders, vision | Encoders, LIDAR, IMU | Force sensors, IMU | IMU, GPS, barometer |
| Cost | Medium–High | Low–Medium | High | Medium |
| Terrain | Structured factory | Smooth/flat | Any (stairs, rocks) | Air (no terrain) |
| Example | KUKA KR 16 | Clearpath Husky | Boston Dynamics Spot | DJI Matrice 350 |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Degree of Freedom (DOF)" of a robotic manipulator. State the DOF of any two common industrial robot configurations.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a robotic manipulator is defined as the **number of independent movements** (rotational or translational) that the end-effector can perform in three-dimensional space. Each independent movement is typically powered by one actuator.

Mathematically, it can be calculated using **Gruebler's formula**:

$$
\text{DOF}_{spatial} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

**Two common configurations:**
1. **Cartesian Robot (PPP)** → 3 DOF (3 prismatic joints — X, Y, Z)
2. **Articulated Robot (RRR)** → 3 DOF (3 revolute joints — waist, shoulder, elbow); typically extended to 6 DOF by adding a spherical wrist.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between static stability and dynamic stability in legged robots. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Stability | Dynamic Stability |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Robot retains balance even when motion is paused | Robot requires continuous motion to remain upright |
| Condition | CoG projection always inside support polygon | CoG may exit polygon but inertia controls balance |
| Leg requirement | At least 4 legs (3 ground contact needed) | 2 legs possible |
| Control complexity | Low | High (requires active balancing) |
| Example | Hexapod walking on flat ground | Human walking; Honda ASIMO |

---

## PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing **DOF** of a *joint* (1) with DOF of an *end-effector* (typically 6 in 3D).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing Ackermann radius as $R = L \tan(\phi)$ instead of $R = L / \tan(\phi)$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to mention **support polygon** in static stability answers.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing **fixed-wing** (cannot hover) with **multi-rotor** (can hover).
> - **Marks Deducted:** Typically **2 marks** for missing a labeled diagram in manipulator answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Classify the different types of robots based on their locomotion and operational environment. Explain in detail the various configurations of manipulators with neat sketches and their typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive mobile robot has two wheels of radius $r = 0.05 \, \text{m}$, separated by a distance $L = 0.4 \, \text{m}$. The robot is required to move straight at a linear velocity of $0.5 \, \text{m/s}$ for 4 seconds, and then rotate in place by 90° clockwise.
**(i)** Determine the angular velocities of both wheels during straight motion. **(3 Marks)**
**(ii)** Determine the angular velocities of both wheels during the in-place rotation, if the rotation must be completed in 2 seconds. **(4 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Robot Classification & Manipulator Configurations (7 Marks)**

**Classification (2 Marks):**
- **Manipulators** — fixed-base, arm-like
- **Mobile Robots** — Wheeled, Legged, Tracked
- **Aerial Robots** — Fixed-wing, Rotary-wing (multi-rotor)
- **Other** — Underwater (AUVs), Swarm, Humanoid

**Manipulator Configurations (5 Marks):**

1. **Cartesian (PPP)** — Three perpendicular prismatic joints forming a rectangular workspace.
   - Applications: Pick-and-place, CNC machine loading, 3D printing.

2. **Cylindrical (RPP)** — One revolute base + two prismatic joints; cylindrical workspace.
   - Applications: Machine tending, die-casting, assembly.

3. **Spherical/Polar (RRP)** — Two revolute + one prismatic; spherical sector workspace.
   - Applications: Welding, painting, material handling.

4. **Articulated (RRR)** — Three revolute joints in series; closest to human arm.
   - Applications: Complex assembly, automotive welding (most popular industrial type).

5. **SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)** — Two revolute + one prismatic; rigid in Z, compliant in XY.
   - Applications: PCB assembly, high-speed packaging.

6. **Parallel (e.g., Delta)** — Multiple arms connecting base to end-effector.
   - Applications: High-speed pick-and-place in food/pharma industry.

*Each configuration must include: joint type, workspace shape, application, and labeled diagram.*

---

#### **Part (b) — Differential Drive Kinematics (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Wheel radius: $r = 0.05 \, \text{m}$
- Wheel separation: $L = 0.4 \, \text{m}$
- Linear velocity (straight): $v = 0.5 \, \text{m/s}$
- Straight motion duration: $t_1 = 4 \, \text{s}$
- Rotation: 90° in $t_2 = 2 \, \text{s}$ (in-place)

**Step 1 — Straight Motion (3 Marks):**

For straight-line motion, both wheels must rotate at the same angular velocity:

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

Since $\omega_R = \omega_L = \omega_0$:

$$
v = r\omega_0 \implies \omega_0 = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{0.5}{0.05} = 10 \, \text{rad/s}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\omega_R = \omega_L = 10 \, \text{rad/s} \approx 95.49 \, \text{rpm}}
$$

**Step 2 — In-place Rotation (4 Marks):**

For a 90° (= $\pi/2$ rad) rotation in $t_2 = 2 \, \text{s}$:

$$
\omega_{robot} = \frac{\pi/2}{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785 \, \text{rad/s}
$$

For in-place rotation, $v = 0$, so:

$$
\omega_{robot} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

With $\omega_R = -\omega_L$ (opposite directions), let $\omega_R = \omega_{wheel}$:

$$
\omega_{robot} = \frac{r(2\omega_{wheel})}{L} = \frac{2r\omega_{wheel}}{L}
$$

Solving:

$$
\omega_{wheel} = \frac{\omega_{robot} \cdot L}{2r} = \frac{(\pi/4)(0.4)}{2 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.1\pi}{0.1} = \pi \approx 3.14 \, \text{rad/s}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\omega_R = +3.14 \, \text{rad/s} \; (\text{forward}) \qquad \omega_L = -3.14 \, \text{rad/s} \; (\text{backward})}
$$

The wheels rotate at **30 rpm** in opposite directions to achieve a 90° pivot turn in 2 seconds.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the various types of wheel configurations used in wheeled mobile robots. Compare differential drive with Ackermann steering in terms of maneuverability and applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A quadcopter weighs $2.5 \, \text{kg}$ (including battery and payload). Each of its four motors can produce a maximum thrust of $10 \, \text{N}$. For hovering at sea level:
**(i)** Calculate the total thrust required for hover. **(2 Marks)**
**(ii)** Determine whether the quadcopter has sufficient thrust margin (compute thrust-to-weight ratio). **(3 Marks)**
**(iii)** If one motor fails, can the quadcopter still maintain controlled flight? Justify with calculations. **(2 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Wheel Configurations & Drive Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Wheel Configurations (4 Marks):**

1. **Standard wheel** — 1 DOF (rotation about axle); fixed direction. Used as primary drive wheel.
2. **Caster wheel** — 2 DOF (rotation + pivot); passive support. Used for balance (e.g., office chair wheel).
3. **Swedish (Mecanum) wheel** — passive rollers at 45° to the main axle; gives omnidirectional motion without steering.
4. **Ball/Spherical wheel** — 3 DOF point contact; full omnidirectional but mechanically complex.

**Drive Type Comparison (3 Marks):**

| Property | Differential Drive | Ackermann Steering |
|---|---|---|
| Turning radius | **Zero** (can rotate in place) | Non-zero, $R = L/\tan(\phi)$ |
| Maneuverability | Excellent in tight spaces | Limited by mechanical constraints |
| Control simplicity | Easy (2 motor speeds) | Complex (steering + throttle) |
| Speed | Limited (small wheels) | High speed capable |
| Application | Indoor robots (Roomba, warehouse AGV) | Autonomous cars, outdoor robots |
| Stability | Less stable at high speed | Inherently stable |

---

#### **Part (b) — Quadcopter Hover Analysis (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 2.5 \, \text{kg}$
- Number of motors = 4
- Maximum thrust per motor $T_{max} = 10 \, \text{N}$

**Step 1 — Total Thrust for Hover (2 Marks):**

$$
T_{hover} = mg = 2.5 \times 9.81 = 24.525 \, \text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Thrust Margin and TWR (3 Marks):**

Total maximum thrust available:

$$
T_{max,total} = 4 \times 10 = 40 \, \text{N}
$$

Thrust-to-Weight Ratio (TWR):

$$
\text{TWR} = \frac{T_{max,total}}{mg} = \frac{40}{24.525} \approx 1.631
$$

Since TWR > 1.2 (minimum for agile flight) and < 4.0 (efficient), the quadcopter has a **good thrust margin** and is capable of hover + payload + moderate maneuvering.

**Margin in Newtons:**

$$
\text{Margin} = 40 - 24.525 = 15.475 \, \text{N} \approx 63.1\% \text{ of hover thrust}
$$

**Step 3 — Single Motor Failure (2 Marks):**

If one motor fails, remaining thrust available:

$$
T_{remaining} = 3 \times 10 = 30 \, \text{N}
$$

This still exceeds the hover requirement of $24.525 \, \text{N}$.

$$
\text{Remaining TWR} = \frac{30}{24.525} \approx 1.223
$$

**Conclusion:**
A **standard quadcopter CANNOT maintain controlled flight** with one motor failed. Although thrust (30 N) exceeds weight (24.5 N), the loss of thrust on one side creates an **asymmetric torque**, causing uncontrolled roll and yaw rotation. The center of thrust shifts, and the resulting moment cannot be balanced by the remaining 3 motors.

> **Engineering Note:** This is why **hexacopters and octocopters** are preferred for mission-critical applications (e.g., cinematography, surveying) — they can lose 1 or 2 motors and still maintain controlled flight.

---

## 📝 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

### Manipulators
- ✅ DOF = number of independent movements of the **end-effector** in space.
- ✅ 6 configurations: Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, Articulated, SCARA, Parallel.
- ✅ Gruebler's formula: $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- ✅ Articulated (6-axis) is the **most popular** industrial robot.
- ✅ SCARA = Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm — rigid in Z, compliant in XY.

### Wheeled Robots
- ✅ Differential drive gives **zero turning radius** (rotates in place).
- ✅ Ackermann radius: $R = L/\tan(\phi)$ — cannot be zero (mechanically).
- ✅ **Mecanum / Swedish wheels** enable omnidirectional motion.
- ✅ Static stability: $\tan(\alpha_{max}) < w/(2h)$.
- ✅ Tracked robots offer high traction but are slower and less efficient.

### Legged Robots
- ✅ Static stability requires **≥3 legs on the ground** and CoG inside support polygon.
- ✅ Minimum legs for static walking = **4** (since $n - 1 \geq 3$).
- ✅ **Hexapod tripod gait** = 3 legs up + 3 legs down alternating.
- ✅ Bipeds use **Zero-Moment Point (ZMP)** control for dynamic balance.
- ✅ Higher leg count = better stability but more actuators and control complexity.

### Aerial Robots
- ✅ Multi-rotors (quad, hexa, octa) **can hover**; fixed-wing **cannot**.
- ✅ Hover condition: $T = mg$.
- ✅ TWR (thrust-to-weight ratio) must be ≥ 1.2 for agile flight.
- ✅ Differential thrust controls roll, pitch, yaw.
- ✅ Quadcopters have **low endurance** (~25 min) due to continuous rotor power.
- ✅ Hexacopters/Octocopters offer **redundancy** (motor-out capability).

### Universal Constants & Units
- ✅ $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ (gravity)
- ✅ 1 rad = $180°/\pi \approx 57.3°$
- ✅ Thrust in Newtons (N), mass in kg, velocity in m/s, angles in rad.

### Common Exam Traps
- ❌ Confusing *DOF of joint* (1) with *DOF of mechanism*.
- ❌ Using $R = L\tan(\phi)$ instead of $R = L/\tan(\phi)$ for Ackermann.
- ❌ Saying "fixed-wing drones can hover" — they CANNOT.
- ❌ Forgetting to draw a **labeled diagram** for full marks in manipulator questions.
- ❌ Mixing up **tripod gait** with **wave gait** in hexapod questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For KTU 2024 Scheme, questions on this topic frequently appear in **Module 1** of the ESE (End Semester Exam) carrying 14 marks. Always include:
> 1. A labeled sketch (2–3 marks reserved for diagrams)
> 2. Tabulated comparisons (manipulator configurations, drive types)
> 3. Numerical solution with proper **Given → Formula → Substitution → Answer** format.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anatomy of a Robotic Manipulator - Links, Joints, Actuators, Sensors, Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET831 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Anatomy of a Robotic Manipulator - Links, Joints, Actuators, Sensors, Controller ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Robotic Manipulator — Formal Definition A **robotic manipulator** is a mechanically constructed, programmable, multi-degree-of-freedom (DOF) kinematic chain composed of **rigid links** interconnected by **joints**, driven by **actuators**, supervised by a **controller**, and informed by **sensors** that allow it to perform desired tasks within a defined workspace. According to the **ISO 8373:2021** standard (industrial robotics vocabulary) and the KTU 2024 syllabus framing, every manipulator can be decomposed into **five functional subsystems** that mirror the human body: | Subsystem | Human Analogy | Industrial Equivalent | |---|---|---| | **Links** | Bones | Aluminum / steel arm segments | | **Joints** | Elbow, shoulder, wrist | Revolute / Prismatic couplings | | **Actuators** | Muscles | Servo motors / hydraulic cylinders | | **Sensors** | Skin, eyes, proprioception | Encoders, vision cameras, F/T sensors | | **Controller** | Brain + nervous system | Microcontroller / Industrial PC + drives | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim for 3-mark answers):** > *"A robotic manipulator is a serial or parallel chain of rigid links connected by joints, where motion is generated by actuators, regulated by sensors, and coordinated by a controller to achieve a programmed end-effector trajectory in Cartesian space."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm Imagine a human arm performing the task of picking up a cup: - **Bones (Humerus, Radius, Ulna)** → These are the *links*. They are rigid, have a specific length, and connect at two ends. - **Joints (Shoulder, Elbow, Wrist)** → These allow rotation or sliding. Your shoulder rotates like a **revolute joint**, and if your shoulder had a sliding collar, that would be a **prismatic joint**. - **Muscles (Biceps, Triceps)** → These are the *actuators*. They provide the **torque** that causes joints to move. Your brain commands them via electrical signals (analogous to PWM/servo signals). - **Skin, Eyes, Inner Ear (Vestibular System)** → These are the *sensors*. Proprioception tells you where your hand is *without* looking — this is exactly what an **encoder** does for a motor. - **Cerebellum + Motor Cortex** → This is the *controller*. It continuously compares *where the arm is* with *where it should be* and corrects the muscle commands — this is **closed-loop feedback control**. > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 module stresses that students must be able to *identify* and *functionally classify* each component in a real industrial arm (e.g., **KUKA KR 16**, **ABB IRB 1200**, or **Fanuc LR-Mate**). ### 1.3 The Five Subsystems at a Glance **1. Links** — Rigid structural members, usually **aluminum alloy (Al 6061)**, **carbon fiber**, or **steel**. Length denoted as $a_i$ in DH parameters. **2. Joints** — Kinematic pairs classified by **Gruebler's mobility criterion**. The most common in serial manipulators are **Revolute (R)** and **Prismatic (P)**. **3. Actuators** — Energy conversion devices. Three principal families: **Electric** (DC servo, BLDC, stepper), **Hydraulic** (high force), and **Pneumatic** (low cost, compliant). **4. Sensors** — Two main classes: - **Internal / Proprioceptive**: encoders, resolvers, tachometers, IMU. - **External / Exteroceptive**: cameras (vision), force-torque, proximity, tactile. **5. Controller** — Hierarchical computing architecture: **Servo-level (lowest)** → **Joint-level** → **Task-level (highest)**. Implements real-time feedback loops (typically running at **1–10 kHz** on the servo loop). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Links — The Rigid Skeleton A **link** is a *kinematically rigid body* — meaning under applied forces it deforms negligibly. The KTU board expects the following classifications: - **Base link (link 0)**: Fixed to the ground / mounting platform. - **Intermediate links**: Connect two non-adjacent joints. - **End-effector link (link n)**: Hosts the tool (gripper, welding torch, suction cup). Each link is described by: - **Length** $a_i$ (DH parameter) - **Twist angle** $\alpha_i$ (DH parameter) - **Mass** $m_i$, **Center of mass** location, **Inertia tensor** $\mathbf{I}_i$. **Why this matters:** Link mass and inertia directly influence **payload capacity**, **dynamic response**, and **motor torque sizing**. A board question may ask: *"Why are links often made hollow?"* — Answer: To **reduce mass and moment of inertia** ($I \propto mr^2$) without sacrificing stiffness. ### 2.2 Joints — The Kinematic Pair A **kinematic pair** is the contact between two links allowing relative motion. The KTU 2024 syllabus lists the following joint types: | Joint Type | Symbol | DOF | Motion | Real-world Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Revolute (R)** | R | 1 | Pure rotation about an axis | Elbow, shoulder | | **Prismatic (P)** | P | 1 | Pure translation along an axis | Telescopic arm | | **Cylindrical** | C | 2 | Rotation + translation | Hydraulic piston-rod | | **Spherical / Ball** | S | 3 | 3-axis rotation | Hip joint, ball-socket | | **Universal / Hooke** | U | 2 | 2 orthogonal rotations | Drive shaft coupling | | **Planar** | E | 3 | 2 translations + 1 rotation | XY-theta stage | | **Screw / Helical** | H | 1 | Coupled rotation + translation | Lead screw, ball-screw | **Joints are classified (Gruebler / Kutzbach criteria):** - **Lower pair**: Surface contact (revolute, prismatic) — *low wear, high load capacity*. - **Higher pair**: Point/line contact (gear, cam) — *higher wear*. **Serial chain notation:** A 6-DOF arm is described as a string of joint types, e.g., **"RRR-RRP"** or **"RRR-RRR"**. The KTU board often gives this as a 3-mark short answer. ### 2.3 Actuators — The Muscle System An **actuator** converts stored energy (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or chemical) into mechanical motion. #### 2.3.1 Electric Actuators (Most Common in Modern Robotics) - **DC Servo Motors**: High torque-to-inertia ratio, easy to control. Used with **PWM drives** and **PID controllers**. - **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors**: Higher efficiency, longer life, used in modern collaborative robots (cobots). - **Stepper Motors**: Open-loop position control; used in low-cost applications (3D printers, small pick-and-place). - **Harmonic Drive / Cycloidal Reducers**: Inline gear reducers with **reduction ratios of 50:1 to 320:1** for high-torque, zero-backlash output. #### 2.3.2 Hydraulic Actuators - **Working fluid**: Mineral oil at **70–210 bar**. - **Force output**: $F = P \times A$ where $P$ is pressure and $A$ is piston area. - **Use case**: Heavy-payload arms (e.g., mining, large part handling). - **Disadvantage**: Leakage, maintenance, noise. #### 2.3.3 Pneumatic Actuators - **Working fluid**: Compressed air at **4–8 bar**. - **Compliance**: Naturally compliant — useful for safe human interaction. - **Disadvantage**: Hard to control precisely; typically open-loop (on/off) or low-precision servo-pneumatic. #### 2.3.4 Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for 7-Mark Questions) | Property | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | |---|---|---|---| | **Power-to-weight** | High | Very High | Low | | **Precision** | Excellent | Good | Poor–Fair | | **Stiffness** | High | Very High | Low | | **Maintenance** | Low | High | Medium | | **Cost** | Medium–High | High | Low | | **Speed** | Medium–High | High | Very High | | **Cleanroom OK** | Yes | No | Yes | ### 2.4 Sensors — The Nervous System #### 2.4.1 Internal / Proprioceptive Sensors (Measure robot's own state) - **Potentiometer**: Resistive, gives absolute position; resolution limited by noise. - **Optical / Magnetic Encoder**: Incremental or absolute. **Resolution** = $360°/N$ where $N$ = pulses per revolution. - **Resolver**: Electromagnetic, robust in harsh environments. - **Tachometer**: Measures rotational velocity. - **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**: Combines gyroscopes and accelerometers for orientation. **Encoder resolution formula:** $$ \theta_{\text{res}} = \frac{360°}{N \times 4} \quad \text{(with quadrature decoding, ×4)} $$ #### 2.4.2 External / Exteroceptive Sensors (Perceive environment) - **Vision (CCD / CMOS cameras)**: 2D or 3D (stereo, structured light, ToF). - **Force/Torque Sensor**: Mounted at the wrist; measures wrench $(F_x, F_y, F_z, M_x, M_y, M_z)$ along all 6 Cartesian axes. - **Proximity Sensor**: Inductive, capacitive, ultrasonic, or IR-based. - **Tactile Sensor Array**: Distributed pressure sensors on gripper surface. - **LIDAR / Time-of-Flight**: For mobile robots, but also used at end-effector for scanning. ### 2.5 Controller — The Brain The **controller** is the real-time computing layer that: 1. Reads sensor values. 2. Compares with desired trajectory. 3. Computes actuator commands via a control law (typically **PID**). 4. Sends PWM / current commands to motor drivers. **The PID Control Law** (essential for KTU derivations): $$ u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} $$ where: - $e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$ is the **joint error** (desired − actual). - $K_p$ = proportional gain (immediate response). - $K_i$ = integral gain (eliminates steady-state error). - $K_d$ = derivative gain (damps overshoot). #### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables | Units | |---|---|---|---| | **Gruebler DOF (planar)** | $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$=links, $j_1$=1-DOF lower pairs, $j_2$=2-DOF pairs | — | | **Gruebler DOF (spatial)** | $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | as above with 1- to 5-DOF pairs | — | | **Hydraulic force** | $F = P \cdot A$ | $P$ pressure, $A$ piston area | $P$ in Pa, $A$ in m², $F$ in N | | **Encoder resolution** | $\theta_{\text{res}} = \frac{360°}{4N}$ | $N$ = lines per revolution | degrees / pulse | | **PID control law** | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i\!\int e\,dt + K_d \dot e$ | $K_p, K_i, K_d$ gains; $e$ error | $u$ in V or torque units | | **Velocity from encoder** | $\omega = \frac{\Delta\theta}{\Delta t}$ | $\Delta\theta$ in rad, $\Delta t$ in s | rad/s | | **Power transmission** | $\tau_{\text{out}} = \tau_{\text{motor}} \cdot \eta \cdot N$ | $\eta$ efficiency, $N$ gear ratio | N·m | | **Inertia (cylinder)** | $I_z = \frac{1}{2}mr^2$ | $m$ mass, $r$ radius | kg·m² | | **Workspace volume (cyl.)** | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | $r$ reach, $h$ vertical travel | m³ | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** Every KTU-listed formula appears in industrial arm design — encoder resolution determines pick-and-place repeatability (±0.02 mm for ABB IRB series), hydraulic force sizing determines gripper clamping force, and PID gains are tuned on commissioning day for each joint. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Gruebler's Formula for a Planar 4-Bar Manipulator **Problem context:** A planar robotic arm has 4 links connected by 4 revolute joints. Compute its **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**. **Step 1: Identify parameters** - Number of links (including ground): $n = 4$ - Number of 1-DOF lower pairs (revolute joints): $j_1 = 4$ - Number of 2-DOF lower pairs: $j_2 = 0$ - Number of higher pairs: $j_3 = 0$ **Step 2: Recall the Gruebler (planar) formula** $$ \text{DOF} = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 $$ **Step 3: Substitute values** $$ \text{DOF} = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 $$ **Step 4: Simplify** $$ \text{DOF} = 3 \times 3 - 8 = 9 - 8 = 1 $$ **Step 5: Physical interpretation** — The planar 4-bar mechanism has **1 DOF** (you fix one input crank angle, the entire mechanism is determined). This matches the famous planar four-bar linkage topology used in low-cost pick-and-place arms. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Encoder Resolution for a Servo Joint **Problem context:** A robot joint uses an **optical encoder** with **N = 2500 lines per revolution**, decoded in **quadrature (×4)** mode. Compute the **angular resolution** in degrees and in radians. **Step 1: Recall formula** $$ \theta_{\text{res}} = \frac{360°}{4N} $$ **Step 2: Substitute N = 2500** $$ \theta_{\text{res}} = \frac{360°}{4 \times 2500} $$ **Step 3: Compute numerator and denominator** $$ \theta_{\text{res}} = \frac{360°}{10000} = 0.036° $$ **Step 4: Convert to radians** $$ \theta_{\text{res}} = 0.036° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad} $$ **Step 5: Compute linear resolution at a 0.5 m reach** $$ \Delta s = r \cdot \theta_{\text{res}} = 0.5 \times 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ \Delta s = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.314 \text{ mm} $$ **Interpretation:** A 0.036° resolution at 0.5 m reach gives a **linear positioning resolution of ~0.31 mm** — well within typical industrial pick-and-place tolerances of **±0.1 mm** (after gearing). --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Hydraulic Gripper Force Sizing **Problem context:** A robotic gripper uses a double-acting hydraulic cylinder. The piston diameter is **50 mm**, the operating pressure is **100 bar**, and the mechanical advantage of the gripper linkage is **2:1**. Compute the **clamping force** at the fingertips. **Step 1: Convert units to SI** - Diameter: $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ - Pressure: $P = 100 \text{ bar} = 100 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 10^7 \text{ Pa}$ **Step 2: Compute piston area** $$ A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} $$ $$ A = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 3: Compute raw piston force** $$ F_{\text{piston}} = P \cdot A = 10^7 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ F_{\text{piston}} = 1.9635 \times 10^4 \text{ N} \approx 19{,}635 \text{ N} $$ **Step 4: Apply mechanical advantage of 2:1** $$ F_{\text{clamp}} = 2 \times F_{\text{piston}} = 2 \times 19{,}635 $$ $$ F_{\text{clamp}} = 39{,}270 \text{ N} \approx 39.27 \text{ kN} $$ **Interpretation:** This corresponds to a payload capacity of approximately **4 tonnes** (using a friction coefficient of 0.3 between gripper and object). This is the principle behind heavy-payload industrial manipulators used in **foundries and forging**. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Joint Sensor Pairings (High-Yield Board Table) | Joint Type | Typical Sensor Pairing | Typical Actuator | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Revolute (R)** | Optical encoder + tachogenerator | BLDC + harmonic drive | Wrist rotation | | **Prismatic (P)** | Linear encoder / LVDT | Ball-screw + servo | Cartesian gantry | | **Spherical (S)** | 3-axis IMU (rare) | 3 independent motors | Parallel wrist (Delta) | | **Cylindrical (C)** | Encoder + LVDT | Hydraulic piston | Heavy lifting arms | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Anatomical Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Robotic Manipulator ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph user_layer["USER LAYER"] opHMI["Operator HMI / Teach Pendant"] prog["Robot Program Offline"] end subgraph ctrl_layer["CONTROLLER LAYER"] taskCtl["Task Level Controller
Trajectory Planner"] jointCtl["Joint Level Controller
Inverse Dynamics"] servoCtl["Servo Level Controller
PID at 1 to 10 kHz"] end subgraph sensor_layer["SENSOR LAYER"] enc["Encoders
Position and Velocity"] ft["Force / Torque Sensor
at Wrist"] vis["Vision System
Cameras and ToF"] prox["Proximity and
Tactile Sensors"] end subgraph act_layer["ACTUATOR LAYER"] servo["Servo Drives
PWM Current"] hyd["Hydraulic Valve
Servo Proportional"] pneu["Pneumatic Valve
5 by 3 Directional"] end subgraph mech_layer["MECHANICAL LAYER"] link1["Link 1 Base"] link2["Link 2 Shoulder"] link3["Link 3 Elbow"] link4["Link 4 Wrist"] ee["End Effector
Gripper or Tool"] end opHMI --> taskCtl prog --> taskCtl taskCtl --> jointCtl jointCtl --> servoCtl servoCtl --> servo servoCtl --> hyd servoCtl --> pneu servo --> link1 link1 --> link2 link2 --> link3 link3 --> link4 link4 --> ee enc -. feedback .-> servoCtl ft -. feedback .-> jointCtl vis -. feedback .-> taskCtl prox -. feedback .-> jointCtl link1 -. measured by .-> enc ee -. measures force .-> ft ee -. scans .-> vis ee -. detects contact .-> prox ``` ### 4.2 Joint Classification Tree (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB jt["Kinematic Joint Pair"] jt --> lp["Lower Pair
Surface Contact"] jt --> hp["Higher Pair
Point Contact"] lp --> r["Revolute R
1 DOF Rotation"] lp --> p["Prismatic P
1 DOF Translation"] lp --> s["Spherical S
3 DOF Rotation"] lp --> u["Universal U
2 DOF Rotation"] lp --> c["Cylindrical C
2 DOF R and T"] lp --> h["Screw H
1 DOF Coupled"] hp --> gear["Gear Pair"] hp --> cam["Cam Pair"] hp --> ballB["Ball Bearing"] ``` ### 4.3 Closed-Loop Feedback Control Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR ref["Reference
q desired"] sum["Summing Junction
Error e"] pid["PID Controller
Kp Ki Kd"] act["Actuator
Motor and Drive"] plant["Plant
Robot Joint"] sensor["Encoder
Sensor"] ref --> sum sum -- "e = q desired minus q actual" --> pid pid -- "u of t" --> act act --> plant plant -- "q actual" --> sensor sensor -- "feedback" --> sum ``` ### 4.4 Link-and-Joint Schematic of a 6-DOF Articulated Arm ```mermaid flowchart LR base["Base Link
Link 0"] j1["J1 Revolute
Waist"] l1["Link 1
Upper Arm"] j2["J2 Revolute
Shoulder"] l2["Link 2
Forearm"] j3["J3 Revolute
Elbow"] l3["Link 3
Wrist 1"] j4["J4 Revolute
Wrist Roll"] l4["Link 4
Wrist 2"] j5["J5 Revolute
Wrist Pitch"] l5["Link 5
Wrist 3"] j6["J6 Revolute
Flange"] endf["End Effector
Gripper"] base --> j1 --> l1 --> j2 --> l2 --> j3 --> l3 --> j4 --> l4 --> j5 --> l5 --> j6 --> endf ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The horizontal flow is a **serial chain** of 6 revolute joints — this is the topology of a **6R articulated arm** (e.g., KUKA KR 6, ABB IRB 1200). The first three joints (J1, J2, J3) are the **positioning** joints, and the last three (J4, J5, J6) are the **orientation / wrist** joints. This separation is the **RPP-RRR split** that simplifies inverse kinematics for spherical-wrist designs. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define a "kinematic joint" in a robotic manipulator. List any four common types of joints used in industrial robots.* **Model Answer (verbatim board style):** A **kinematic joint** is a constrained connection between two adjacent links that allows specific relative motion while restricting others. It is characterized by its **degrees of freedom (DOF)** and the **type of contact** (lower or higher pair). Four common joint types in industrial robots: 1. **Revolute (R)** — single-axis rotation (e.g., shoulder of a 6R arm). 2. **Prismatic (P)** — single-axis translation (e.g., linear gantry axis). 3. **Spherical (S)** — three-axis rotation (used in wrists and parallel manipulators). 4. **Cylindrical (C)** — coupled rotation and translation along the same axis. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (3-mark trap):** Students often list *only* revolute and prismatic. To get full 3 marks, the KTU 2024 board expects **at least 4 types with one-line descriptions** of each. Merely naming them earns only 1 mark. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Differentiate between a proprioceptive sensor and an exteroceptive sensor. Give one example of each used in robotics.* **Model Answer:** | Property | **Proprioceptive Sensor** | **Exteroceptive Sensor** | |---|---|---| | **Function** | Measures the *internal* state of the robot (joint angle, velocity, current) | Measures *external* state (objects, environment, forces) | | **Loop placement** | Inside the servo feedback loop | In higher-level (task) control loop | | **Example** | Optical encoder on a motor shaft | CCD/CMOS camera in the vision system | A **potentiometer** is another example of a proprioceptive sensor, and a **force-torque sensor** at the wrist is an example of an exteroceptive sensor. --- ### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- #### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Joints + Actuators + Sensors **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the working of the following joint types used in robotic manipulators: **(7 marks)** - (i) Revolute joint - (ii) Prismatic joint - (iii) Spherical joint **(b)** Compare **electric**, **hydraulic**, and **pneumatic** actuators in industrial robots on the basis of any **seven** key parameters. State one typical application of each. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Working of joint types:** **(i) Revolute Joint (R):** A revolute joint allows **pure rotational motion** about a single fixed axis. Relative motion is described by a single joint variable $\theta_i$. The DOF is 1. It is constructed using a **bearing** (ball or roller) so that the two connected links can rotate freely with **low friction** and **high stiffness**. Examples: shoulder, elbow, and waist joints of a 6R industrial arm. **(ii) Prismatic Joint (P):** A prismatic joint allows **pure translational (sliding) motion** along a single fixed axis. The joint variable is a linear displacement $d_i$. It is built with a **linear bearing and a sliding carriage** (often driven by a **ball screw** or **linear motor**). The DOF is 1. Examples: gantry axes, telescoping arms, and the extending column of a cylindrical-coordinate robot. **(iii) Spherical Joint (S):** A spherical (ball-and-socket) joint allows **3 independent rotational DOFs** about three intersecting axes. The joint variable is a 3-vector $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$. Construction uses a **ball-and-socket** with low-friction liners. It is rarely used as an *active* joint in serial manipulators (because of actuation difficulty) but is the kinematic basis for the **human hip joint** and for **parallel wrist designs** (Delta robot). **(b) Comparison of Actuators:** | Parameter | **Electric** | **Hydraulic** | **Pneumatic** | |---|---|---|---| | **Energy source** | Electrical (AC/DC) | Pressurized oil | Compressed air | | **Power-to-weight** | High | Very high | Low | | **Precision** | Excellent (±0.01 mm) | Good | Poor–Fair | | **Stiffness** | High | Very high | Low (compliant) | | **Speed** | Medium–High | High | Very high | | **Maintenance** | Low | High (leakage, filters) | Medium | | **Cleanroom suitability** | Excellent | Poor | Good | | **Typical application** | KUKA KR 16, Fanuc welding | Forging, casting handling | Pick-and-place, food packaging | --- #### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: Sensors + Controller **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **(a)** Explain with a block diagram the **closed-loop PID control** of a single robotic joint. Define each term in the control law and explain the effect of increasing $K_p$, $K_i$, and $K_d$ on the step response. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the working of the following sensors used in robotic manipulators: **(7 marks)** - (i) Optical encoder (incremental type) - (ii) Force/torque sensor - (iii) Vision system (CCD camera) **Model Solution:** **(a) PID Closed-Loop Control of a Robotic Joint:** The closed-loop architecture is: **Reference → Comparator → PID Controller → Servo Drive → Motor → Joint (Plant) → Encoder → (back to Comparator)** The PID control law is: $$ u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} $$ **Effect of each gain:** - **$K_p$ (Proportional):** Provides *immediate* corrective action. Increasing $K_p$ makes the system respond faster but increases **overshoot** and may cause **oscillation** at high values. Too high → instability. - **$K_i$ (Integral):** Eliminates **steady-state error** by accumulating past errors.
