Increasing $K_i$ removes offset but slows response and can cause **integral windup** if the actuator saturates. - **$K_d$ (Derivative):** Acts on the *rate of change* of error. Provides a **damping** effect, reducing overshoot. Increasing $K_d$ improves stability but amplifies **sensor noise** (since derivative of a noisy signal is very noisy). **Typical tuning sequence:** Increase $K_p$ until slight oscillation → add $K_d$ to damp → add $K_i$ to remove steady-state error → re-tune. **(b) Sensor working:** **(i) Optical Encoder (Incremental):** A disk with **N radial lines** rotates between an **LED source** and a **photodetector**. As the disk rotates, light is interrupted, generating **N pulses per revolution**. Two channels (A and B) in quadrature give **direction sensing** and **4× resolution** after decoding. Used as the *primary position feedback* on virtually every servo-driven joint. **Angular resolution:** $$ \theta_{\text{res}} = \frac{360°}{4N} $$ **(ii) Force/Torque Sensor:** Mounted between the wrist and the end-effector, this sensor uses **strain gauges** arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration on a precisely machined elastic element. Deformation under load changes the gauge resistance, which is decoded to give a 6-axis wrench: $$ \mathbf{w} = [F_x,\; F_y,\; F_z,\; M_x,\; M_y,\; M_z]^T $$ Used in **assembly**, **deburring**, and **impedance control** applications. **(iii) Vision System (CCD Camera):** A 2D array of **charge-coupled devices (CCDs)** or **CMOS pixels** captures reflected light from the scene. Each pixel integrates incoming photons and outputs a digital intensity value, forming a **gray-scale or RGB image matrix**. In robotics, vision enables: - **Object recognition** (template matching, deep learning classifiers). - **Pose estimation** (using fiducial markers like ArUco). - **Visual servoing** (closing the control loop directly on image features). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (7-mark trap):** When asked to *"explain PID control"*, many students write only the formula and skip the **block diagram** and the **physical effect of each gain** on step response. The KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates **3 marks for the diagram**, **2 marks for the formula**, and **2 marks for the gain effects** — so omitting the diagram costs you half the marks. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this before the exam):** - ☐ **Manipulator = Links + Joints + Actuators + Sensors + Controller** — never omit any in a definition. - ☐ **Joint types**: R (rotational, 1 DOF), P (linear, 1 DOF), S (3 rotations), C (R+T), U (2 rotations), H (screw), E (planar). - ☐ **Gruebler's formula** (spatial): $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$. - ☐ **Gruebler's formula** (planar): $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$. - ☐ **Serial chain notation**: list joint types from base to end-effector, e.g., R-R-R-R-R-R. - ☐ **Actuator trade-off**: Electric = precision, Hydraulic = power, Pneumatic = simplicity. - ☐ **Encoder resolution**: $\theta_{\text{res}} = 360°/(4N)$ with quadrature decoding. - ☐ **Hydraulic force**: $F = P \cdot A$ — remember unit conversion: **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa**. - ☐ **Sensor classification**: Proprioceptive (internal state) vs. Exteroceptive (environment). - ☐ **Controller hierarchy**: Servo level (kHz) → Joint level (inverse dynamics) → Task level (trajectory). - ☐ **PID law**: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i\!\int e\,dt + K_d \dot e$ — know the role of each gain. - ☐ **Block diagram must** accompany any control question (3 marks reserved for the diagram alone). - ☐ **6R arm topology**: First 3 joints = *position*, last 3 = *orientation* (spherical wrist convention). - ☐ **Industrial examples**: KUKA, ABB, Fanuc, Yaskawa — recognize the manufacturer→application mapping. - ☐ **Forward kinematics** uses **DH parameters** $(a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i)$ — one per joint. - ☐ **Lower pair joints** have surface contact → low wear, high load capacity → preferred in industrial arms. > ✅ **End of Module 1 Notes — Anatomy of a Robotic Manipulator**

topics.title: Open Kinematic vs Closed Kinematic Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET831 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Open Kinematic vs Closed Kinematic Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematic Chain:** A kinematic chain is an assembly of rigid bodies (links) connected by joints (kinematic pairs) such that each link has a defined relative motion with respect to its neighbours. It models the structural skeleton of any robotic manipulator.

**Open Kinematic Chain (OKC):**
An open kinematic chain, also called a **serial manipulator** or **serial chain**, is a kinematic configuration in which the links are arranged sequentially end-to-end, with the **base** at one end and the **end-effector** at the other. There exists **only one single, unconstrained path** of motion from the base to the end-effector — no loop is formed.

**Closed Kinematic Chain (CKC):**
A closed kinematic chain, also called a **parallel manipulator** or **parallel chain**, is a kinematic configuration in which the **end-effector is simultaneously connected to the base through two or more independent serial chains (limbs)**. The closed loops constrain the motion of the end-effector, providing multiple load-bearing paths.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid Kinematic Chain:** Many modern industrial robots (e.g., the **Delta robot**, **Stewart platform**, and **SCARA**) combine both open and closed sub-chains. KTU examiners frequently pose questions contrasting hybrid structures.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

| Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---------|--------------------|
| **Open Kinematic Chain** | Your **human arm**. Shoulder → upper arm → elbow → forearm → wrist → palm → fingers. Each joint moves independently, and your fingertips are free in space with only one path from shoulder to fingertip. |
| **Closed Kinematic Chain** | A **steering linkage** in a car, or a **folding umbrella's ribs**, or the **legs of a four-legged stool**. The moving platform (steering knuckle) is connected to the base (chassis) via two arms, forming a four-bar closed loop. If you push one link, the other MUST move to preserve the loop closure. |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for exams:** *"Open = Open hand (free end). Closed = Closed loop (constrained end like a ring)."*

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — denoted as **n** or **F**
- **Number of links** — denoted as **l**
- **Number of joints** — denoted as **j**
- **Loop closure equations** — fundamental constraint count
- **Workspace volume** — **Reachable Workspace** vs **Dexterous Workspace**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Open Kinematic Chains

- **Structure:** Base → Link 1 → Joint 1 → Link 2 → Joint 2 → … → Joint n → Link n → End-effector.
- **Joint types typically used:** Revolute (R) and Prismatic (P). Common notations: **RRR** (articulated), **RRP**, **PPP** (Cartesian).
- **Motion accumulation:** Each joint's motion is **added** to the cumulative pose of the end-effector.
- **Forward kinematics:** Easy — multiply successive homogeneous transformation matrices $A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdots A_n$.
- **Inverse kinematics:** **Hard** — multiple solutions, singularities, non-linear transcendental equations.
- **Error accumulation:** Joint errors stack multiplicatively along the chain — distal links amplify errors from proximal joints.
- **Workspace:** **Large, dexterous, and irregular** (often spherical or toroidal).

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown of Closed Kinematic Chains

- **Structure:** Base → Limb 1 → End-effector ← Limb 2 ← Base (and possibly more limbs).
- **Loop closure constraint:** Sum of joint displacements around every closed loop must equal **zero** (or $2\pi$ for revolute loops).
- **Joint types typically used:** Prismatic actuators are preferred for high stiffness (e.g., Stewart platform uses 6 prismatic legs).
- **Forward kinematics:** **Hard** — non-linear coupled equations, multiple solutions.
- **Inverse kinematics:** **Easy** — given desired end-effector pose, each limb length is computed directly.
- **Error averaging:** Manufacturing tolerances are averaged across limbs — error does **not** propagate monotonically.
- **Workspace:** **Smaller, regular (often cylindrical or cubic), with high stiffness** near the centre.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kutzbach–Grübler DOF Criterion (Spatial) | $\displaystyle F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | DOF of planar/spatial mechanisms |
| 2 | Loop-Closure Equation | $\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_i = 0 \pmod{2\pi}$ (closed revolute loop) | Constraint for CKC kinematics |
| 3 | Forward Kinematics (Denavit–Hartenberg) | $\displaystyle {}^{0}T_{n} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} {}^{i-1}A_i(\theta_i, d_i, a_i, \alpha_i)$ | End-effector pose for OKC |
| 4 | Parallel Manipulator Force Balance | $\displaystyle \vec{F}_{ee} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} J_k^{-T} \vec{\tau}_k$ | Static force mapping for CKC |
| 5 | Effective Stiffness Ratio | $\displaystyle k_{eff} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} k_k$ (parallel) vs $\displaystyle k_{eff} = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{1}{k_k}\right)^{-1}$ (serial) | Stiffness comparison |
| 6 | Workspace Dexterity Index | $\displaystyle \nu = \frac{V_{dexterous}}{V_{reachable}} \le 1$ | Quality of workspace |
| 7 | Singular Configuration Condition | $\displaystyle \det(J(\theta)) = 0$ | Loss of DOF; boundary of workspace |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stiffness rule of thumb:** Springs (or limbs) in **parallel add stiffness**, springs (or limbs) in **series reduce stiffness**. This is the single most cited KTU exam point differentiating OKC and CKC.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OKC dominates** heavy-payload industrial welding, painting, assembly (e.g., **KUKA KR 1000**, **ABB IRB series**).
- **CKC dominates** high-precision tasks: flight simulators (**Stewart platform**), high-speed pick-and-place (**Delta robot**), machine tool hexapods, and surgical robotic platforms.
- **Hybrid chains** dominate medical robotics where stiffness *and* dexterity are both critical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Kutzbach–Grübler Criterion for a Planar 4-Bar Closed Chain

The 4-bar linkage is the canonical example of a **planar closed kinematic chain**. We derive its DOF using the Kutzbach criterion.

**Step 1 — Identify the components.**
- Number of rigid links (excluding ground): $n = 3$ (the coupler, the input crank, the output rocker).
- Number of full joints (revolute, 1 DOF each): $j_1 = 4$ (all four pin joints are R-pairs).

**Step 2 — Apply the planar Kutzbach formula.**
For a planar mechanism, the formula reduces to:

$$
F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
F = 3(3 - 1) - 2(4) - 0
$$

**Step 3 — Expand and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 3 \cdot 2 - 8 \\
  &= 6 - 8 \\
  &= -2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Grübler's correction for dependent loops.**
A planar 4-bar has **1 dependent loop**. Grübler's correction adds back the number of redundant constraints:

$$
F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 + \lambda
$$

where $\lambda$ is the number of independent redundant loops. For 4-bar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(3 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 + 1 \\
  &= 6 - 8 + 1 \\
  &= -1 \quad \text{(still incorrect — meaning a single 4-bar is over-constrained?)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Correct counting.** For the classic planar 4-bar linkage:
- Links = 4 (including ground)
- Moving links $n = 3$
- 1-DOF revolute joints $j_1 = 4$

Using the **corrected planar mobility** with one redundant loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n) - 2j_1 - j_2 + F_{red} \\
  &= 3(3) - 2(4) - 0 \\
  &= 9 - 8 \\
  &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives **1 DOF** as expected for a planar 4-bar — the driver crank controls the entire mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 examiners accept the simplified **Grübler's formula** $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 + \lambda$ for planar problems and $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - \cdots$ for spatial problems.

### 3.2 Derivation: Loop-Closure Equation for a Simple Closed Chain

Consider a planar 4-bar with link lengths $L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4$ and angles $\theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4$ (measured from the X-axis of the ground link).

**Step 1 — Vector loop equation.**

$$
\vec{L}_1 + \vec{L}_2 - \vec{L}_3 - \vec{L}_4 = \vec{0}
$$

**Step 2 — Resolve into X and Y components.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_2\cos\theta_2 - L_3\cos\theta_3 - L_4\cos\theta_4 &= 0 \\
L_2\sin\theta_2 - L_3\sin\theta_3 - L_4\sin\theta_4 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Squaring and adding eliminates the joint variable** $\theta_4$ via $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$:

$$
L_1^2 + L_2^2 - L_3^2 - L_4^2 - 2L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2 + 2L_3 L_4 \cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $\theta_3$** yields Freudenstein's equation:

$$
K_1\cos\theta_3 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3)
$$

where:

$$
K_1 = \frac{L_4}{L_2}, \quad K_2 = \frac{L_4}{L_1}, \quad K_3 = \frac{L_1^2 - L_2^2 + L_3^2 + L_4^2}{2L_2 L_4}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.** This non-linear equation is what makes the **inverse kinematics of CKC non-trivial**, justifying the existence of numerical solvers (Newton–Raphson) in robotic simulation software.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: OKC vs CKC

| Parameter | Open Kinematic Chain | Closed Kinematic Chain |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Serial, single path | Parallel, multi-path loop |
| **Mechanical Stiffness** | **Low** (springs in series) | **Very High** (springs in parallel) |
| **Payload Capacity** | Lower per unit mass | Much higher per unit mass |
| **Workspace Volume** | **Large**, often irregular | **Smaller**, regular shape |
| **Dexterity** | High (e.g., human-arm-like) | Moderate |
| **Positioning Accuracy** | Degrades with reach | Constant, high |
| **Forward Kinematics** | Closed-form, easy | Non-linear, hard |
| **Inverse Kinematics** | Multiple solutions, hard | Direct, easy |
| **Error Accumulation** | Multiplicative (cumulative) | Averaging (self-correcting) |
| **Singularities** | At full reach (boundary) | Inside workspace (more dangerous) |
| **Manufacturing Cost** | Moderate | High (precision limbs required) |
| **Typical Examples** | Articulated robot arm, SCARA | Stewart platform, Delta robot |
| **Force Transmission** | Through each joint sequentially | Distributed across limbs |
| **Mass-to-Payload Ratio** | High (heavy actuators at base) | Low (motors near base) |
| **Backlash Accumulation** | Severe (sum of all joints) | Minimal (averaged) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Topology — Open vs Closed Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OKC["OPEN KINEMATIC CHAIN"]
        B1[BASE] --> J1A[JOINT 1 Revolute] --> L1A[LINK 1] --> J2A[JOINT 2 Revolute] --> L2A[LINK 2] --> J3A[JOINT 3 Revolute] --> L3A[LINK 3] --> J4A[JOINT 4 Revolute] --> EE1[END EFFECTOR Free]
    end
    subgraph CKC["CLOSED KINEMATIC CHAIN"]
        B2[BASE] --> J1B[JOINT A] --> LB1[LIMB 1] --> JMB[JOINT M] --> EE2[END EFFECTOR]
        B2 --> J2B[JOINT B] --> LB2[LIMB 2] --> JMB
        B2 --> J3B[JOINT C] --> LB3[LIMB 3] --> JMB
    end
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Industrial Robot Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TASK DEFINITION] --> B{PRIMARY REQUIREMENT}
    B -- LARGE WORKSPACE / DEXTERITY --> C[OPEN KINEMATIC CHAIN SERIAL ARM]
    B -- HIGH PRECISION / HIGH STIFFNESS --> D[CLOSED KINEMATIC CHAIN PARALLEL MANIPULATOR]
    B -- MIXED REQUIREMENT --> E[HYBRID KINEMATIC CHAIN]
    C --> F[EXAMPLES KUKA ABB IRB FANUC]
    D --> G[EXAMPLES DELTA STEWART HEXAPOD]
    E --> H[EXAMPLES SCARA TRICEPT]
    C --> I[DOMINANT INDUSTRIES WELDING PAINTING ASSEMBLY]
    D --> J[DOMINANT INDUSTRIES AEROSPACE MEDICAL SURGERY FLIGHT SIM]
    E --> K[DOMINANT INDUSTRIES PCB ASSEMBLY PACKAGING]
```

### 4.3 DOF Flow Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SERIAL["SERIAL CHAIN DOF FLOW"]
        S1[BASE 0 DOF] --> S2[J1 1 DOF] --> S3[J2 1 DOF] --> S4[J3 1 DOF] --> S5[END EFFECTOR 6 DOF TOTAL]
    end
    subgraph PARALLEL["PARALLEL CHAIN DOF FLOW"]
        P1[BASE] --> P2[LIMB 1 ACTUATED 1 DOF]
        P1 --> P3[LIMB 2 ACTUATED 1 DOF]
        P1 --> P4[LIMB 3 ACTUATED 1 DOF]
        P2 --> P5[END EFFECTOR 6 DOF CONSTRAINED]
        P3 --> P5
        P4 --> P5
    end
```

### 4.4 Workspace Geometry Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WS["WORKSPACE SHAPES"]
        W1[OPEN CHAIN WORKSPACE] -->|Shape| WSH1[IRREGULAR SPHERICAL OR TOROIDAL]
        W2[CLOSED CHAIN WORKSPACE] -->|Shape| WSH2[CYLINDRICAL OR CUBIC WITH HOLES NEAR SINGULARITIES]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between an open kinematic chain and a closed kinematic chain with one suitable example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Open Kinematic Chain | Closed Kinematic Chain |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Links arranged in series with a free end-effector | End-effector connected to base via multiple limbs forming loops |
| Example | 6-DOF articulated robot arm (e.g., ABB IRB) | Delta robot / Stewart platform |
| Load path | Single path | Multiple paths |

**(1 mark per correct row, 3 marks total.)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Kutzbach–Grübler equation for spatial mechanisms and define all terms.*

**Model Answer:**

$$
F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

- **F** = Degrees of freedom of the mechanism. **(1 mark)**
- **n** = Number of links (excluding ground). **(0.5 mark)**
- **j₁, j₂, j₃, j₄, j₅** = Number of joints with 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 DOF respectively. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **6-DOF closed kinematic chain manipulator (Stewart platform)**. List its **industrial applications**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Kutzbach–Grübler DOF** for a spatial 6-DOF Stewart platform with 6 prismatic limbs and 12 spherical joints. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

A Stewart platform consists of:
- A **fixed base platform** (lower).
- A **moving end-effector platform** (upper).
- **6 prismatic actuators (limbs)** connecting base to end-effector via **12 spherical joints** (2 per limb).

**(3 marks for diagram description)** — Reference Figure: Base plate, 6 linear actuators arranged symmetrically at 60° intervals, upper mobile plate, spherical joints at both ends of each limb.

**Applications (4 marks, 1 each):**
1. **Flight simulators** — high-fidelity 6-DOF motion cueing.
2. **Machine tool hexapods** — high-precision milling (e.g., Hexel Hexapod).
3. **Telescope optics** — adaptive mirror positioning.
4. **Medical surgery platforms** — minimally invasive robotic surgery.
5. **Off-shore vessel motion compensation**.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Count components.**
- Number of moving links: Base (ground) is fixed. End-effector = 1 moving link. Each limb = 1 moving link.
- Total moving links: $n = 6 + 1 = 7$.
- Spherical joints (3-DOF each): $j_3 = 12$.
- Prismatic joints (1-DOF each): $j_1 = 6$.

**Step 2 — Apply Kutzbach–Grübler spatial formula.**

$$
F = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(12) - 2(0) - 0 \\
  &= 6 \cdot 6 - 30 - 0 - 36 - 0 \\
  &= 36 - 30 - 36 \\
  &= -30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply loop correction.** A 6-DOF Stewart platform has **6 independent closed loops**. Adding $\lambda = 6$ to the equation (using the alternate mobility form $M = 6(n-j-1) + \sum f_i$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(13 - 12 - 1) + (6 \cdot 1 + 12 \cdot 3) \\
  &= 6(0) + (6 + 36) \\
  &= 42 \quad \text{(raw)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the correct mobility formula:**

$$
M = 6(n - 1 - j) + \sum_{i} f_i
$$

Substituting $n = 13$ (all links), $j = 18$ (all joints):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(13 - 1 - 18) + (6 \cdot 1 + 12 \cdot 3) \\
  &= 6(-6) + 42 \\
  &= -36 + 42 \\
  &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hence F = 6 DOF, as expected for a Stewart platform.** ✅ **(7 marks — full breakdown: 2 marks setup, 3 marks substitution, 1 mark loop correction concept, 1 mark final answer.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **forward and inverse kinematics** of an open kinematic chain robot. Why is forward kinematics trivial but inverse kinematics complex? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A planar 4-bar linkage has the following parameters: $L_1 = 100$ mm, $L_2 = 50$ mm, $L_3 = 80$ mm, $L_4 = 70$ mm, input angle $\theta_2 = 60°$. Compute the output angle $\theta_4$ using the **loop-closure equation**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Forward Kinematics (FK):** Given joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n$, compute the end-effector pose $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$. **(2 marks)**

Using the Denavit–Hartenberg homogeneous transformation:

$$
{}^{0}T_n = A_1(\theta_1) \cdot A_2(\theta_2) \cdots A_n(\theta_n)
$$

This is a **deterministic matrix multiplication** — uniquely solvable. **(2 marks)**

**Inverse Kinematics (IK):** Given a desired end-effector pose, compute the joint angles. **(1 mark)**

The system becomes **non-linear**, multi-valued, and may have **no solution** (out of workspace) or **infinite solutions** (redundant robots). Closed-form solutions exist only for specific geometries (spherical wrist, decoupled structures). Numerical methods (Jacobian pseudo-inverse, iterative solvers) are typically used. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Vector loop closure (X-component):**

$$
L_2\cos\theta_2 - L_3\cos\theta_3 - L_4\cos\theta_4 + L_1 = 0
$$

Wait — for the standard 4-bar with input crank $L_2$, coupler $L_3$, output rocker $L_4$, ground $L_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_2\cos\theta_2 + L_3\cos\theta_3 - L_4\cos\theta_4 - L_1 &= 0 \\
L_2\sin\theta_2 + L_3\sin\theta_3 - L_4\sin\theta_4 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $L_1 = 100, L_2 = 50, L_3 = 80, L_4 = 70, \theta_2 = 60°$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
50\cos 60° + 80\cos\theta_3 - 70\cos\theta_4 - 100 &= 0 \\
50\sin 60° + 80\sin\theta_3 - 70\sin\theta_4 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
25 + 80\cos\theta_3 - 70\cos\theta_4 - 100 &= 0 \\
43.30 + 80\sin\theta_3 - 70\sin\theta_4 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
80\cos\theta_3 - 70\cos\theta_4 &= 75 \\
80\sin\theta_3 - 70\sin\theta_4 &= -43.30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve simultaneously.** Squaring and adding:

$$
(80)^2 + (70)^2 - 2(80)(70)\cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4) = (75)^2 + (-43.30)^2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
6400 + 4900 - 11200\cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4) &= 5625 + 1875 \\
11300 - 11200\cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4) &= 7500 \\
\cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4) &= \frac{11300 - 7500}{11200} = \frac{3800}{11200} = 0.3393
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\theta_3 - \theta_4 = \pm 70.17°
$$

**Step 4 — Branch selection (assembly mode).** Taking the open configuration:

$$
\theta_4 = \theta_3 - 70.17°
$$

Substituting back and solving via elimination yields:

$$
\theta_4 \approx -38.5° \quad \text{or} \quad \theta_4 \approx 78.4°
$$

The valid assembly configuration (Grashof condition check: $L_s + L_l \le L_p + L_q \Rightarrow 50 + 100 \le 80 + 70 \Rightarrow 150 \le 150$, **Grashof critical**) gives:

$$
\boxed{\theta_4 \approx -38.5° \text{ (open configuration)}}
$$

**(Mark distribution: 2 marks setup, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks elimination, 1 mark final numerical answer.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **DO NOT confuse Grübler's formula for planar vs spatial.** Planar uses multiplier **3**, spatial uses multiplier **6**. Common 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Always count `n` excluding the ground link** when using the standard form $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1$. Many students include ground by mistake.
> 3. **For loop-closure problems**, students forget that **two assembly modes** (open and crossed) exist — KTU expects both to be acknowledged or the correct one explicitly justified.
> 4. **CKC singularities occur INSIDE the workspace** — this is the opposite of OKC. Examiners love this trick question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **open kinematic chain** has **one single path** from base to end-effector; **large workspace**, **low stiffness**, **easy FK / hard IK**.
- ✅ A **closed kinematic chain** has **two or more limbs** forming **loops**; **small workspace**, **high stiffness**, **hard FK / easy IK**.
- ✅ **Stiffness rule:** Parallel limbs **add stiffness**; serial limbs **reduce stiffness**. Use it in any KTU comparison.
- ✅ **Error propagation:** Cumulative in OKC, averaging in CKC.
- ✅ **Singularities:** At workspace boundary for OKC; **inside** workspace for CKC — much more dangerous.
- ✅ **Grübler's formula** — Planar: $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 + \lambda$; Spatial: $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5 + \lambda$.
- ✅ **Loop-closure equations** are the defining mathematical feature of CKC and lead to non-linear IK.
- ✅ **Hybrid chains** (e.g., SCARA with a closed-loop planar section + open wrist) are common in industry and frequently tested.
- ✅ **Force transmission:** OKC transmits force sequentially through each joint; CKC distributes force across all limbs simultaneously.
- ✅ **Examples to remember:** OKC → ABB IRB 120, KUKA KR 16; CKC → Delta robot, Stewart platform; Hybrid → SCARA, Tricept.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degrees of Freedom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET831 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Degrees of Freedom

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of robotics, **Degrees of Freedom (DoF)** is formally defined as the *number of independent parameters (or coordinates) required to completely specify the configuration (position and orientation) of a robotic mechanism in three-dimensional space*. For a free, unconstrained rigid body in 3D space, the maximum possible degrees of freedom is **6**, comprising 3 translational motions (surge, sway, heave) and 3 rotational motions (roll, pitch, yaw).

For a robotic manipulator, the total degrees of freedom of the system is the sum of the degrees of freedom contributed by each individual joint, minus any constraints imposed by the end-effector's task requirements or the robot's mechanical structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A robot manipulator with **6 DoF** is kinematically sufficient to position its end-effector at any arbitrary point $(x, y, z)$ with any arbitrary orientation (roll, pitch, yaw) within its workspace. Robots with fewer than 6 DoF are kinematically deficient (cannot achieve arbitrary orientation), and robots with more than 6 DoF are kinematically redundant (multiple joint configurations yield the same end-effector pose).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a human arm:
- **Your shoulder** allows your upper arm to rotate forward/backward, side-to-side, and twist (3 rotational DoF).
- **Your elbow** allows your forearm to bend (1 rotational DoF).
- **Your wrist** allows twisting, bending, and side-bending (3 rotational DoF).

Total: 3 + 1 + 3 = **7 DoF** (humans are kinematically redundant for simple positioning tasks — you can still touch your nose with your eyes closed using many different arm configurations).

In robotics, we carefully design mechanical joints to mimic this flexibility, but typically for industrial tasks we settle on 6 DoF as the engineering sweet spot — enough dexterity to reach any position with any tool orientation, but not so much that control becomes unnecessarily complex.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant / Standard Metric:** A rigid body in 3D Euclidean space has exactly **6** independent degrees of freedom. This is a fundamental result of classical mechanics and forms the basis for all robotic kinematic analysis.

### 1.3 Why DoF Matters in Robot Design

The number of degrees of freedom directly determines:
1. **Workspace size and shape** — the set of all reachable points and orientations.
2. **Dexterity** — the ability to orient the end-effector arbitrarily.
3. **Controllability** — more DoF means more complex inverse kinematics and trajectory planning.
4. **Mechanical complexity and cost** — each DoF typically requires one actuator, one joint, and feedback sensors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breakdown of the 6 DoF of a Free Rigid Body

A free rigid body in 3D space possesses exactly **6 independent motions**:

**Translational Components (3 DoF):**
- Translation along the **X-axis** (longitudinal / surge)
- Translation along the **Y-axis** (lateral / sway)
- Translation along the **Z-axis** (vertical / heave)

**Rotational Components (3 DoF):**
- Rotation about the **X-axis** (roll)
- Rotation about the **Y-axis** (pitch)
- Rotation about the **Z-axis** (yaw)

### 2.2 Joint Types and Their DoF Contributions

| Joint Type | Symbol | DoF | Motion Description | Real-World Example |
|------------|--------|-----|--------------------|---------------------|
| Revolute    | R      | 1   | Rotation about a single axis | Elbow joint, motor shaft |
| Prismatic   | P      | 1   | Linear translation along a single axis | Linear actuator, piston |
| Cylindrical | C      | 2   | Rotation + translation (coaxial) | Screw-in cylinder mechanism |
| Universal   | U      | 2   | Rotation about two perpendicular intersecting axes | Car universal joint |
| Spherical (Ball) | S  | 3   | Rotation about three concurrent axes | Human shoulder, ball-and-socket |
| Screw / Helical | H  | 1   | Combined rotation and translation (coupled) | Lead screw, bolt-and-nut |
| Planar      | E      | 3   | Translation in plane + rotation normal to plane | Floating platform |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always remember that **revolute (R) and prismatic (P) joints each contribute exactly 1 DoF**. These are the building blocks of nearly all industrial robot configurations. Most industrial manipulators use only R and P joints.

### 2.3 Standard Robot Configurations and Their DoF

| Configuration | Joint Sequence | Arm DoF | With 3-DoF Wrist | Typical Application |
|---------------|----------------|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| Cartesian / Gantry | P-P-P | 3 | 6 | Pick-and-place, CNC |
| Cylindrical       | R-P-P | 3 | 6 | Assembly, machine loading |
| Spherical / Polar | R-R-P | 3 | 6 | Welding, painting |
| SCARA             | R-R-P | 3 | 4 (or 6) | PCB assembly, packaging |
| Articulated / Anthropomorphic | R-R-R | 3 | 6 | Welding, material handling |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description |
|-------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| Free rigid body in 3D | $\text{DoF} = 6$ | Maximum independent motions |
| Free rigid body in 2D (planar) | $\text{DoF} = 3$ | Two translations + one rotation |
| Grübler-Kutzbach (Spatial) | $\text{DoF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | n = links, $j_i$ = joints with $i$ DoF |
| Grübler-Kutzbach (Planar) | $\text{DoF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | n = links, $j_i$ = joints with $i$ DoF |
| Total manipulator DoF | $\text{DoF}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$ | Sum of all joint freedoms |
| Position vector dimension | $\vec{p} = [x, y, z]^T$ | 3 components |
| Orientation representation | $\mathbf{R} = [R_{ij}]_{3 \times 3}$ or $[ \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ | 3 independent parameters |
| Full pose vector | $\vec{\xi} = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ | 6-DoF pose |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition for Grübler-Kutzbach:** If the computed DoF is negative, the structure is a *pre-loaded structure* (statically indeterminate). If DoF = 0, it is a *structure* (no mobility). If DoF > 0, it is a *mechanism* with that many independent inputs.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Degrees of freedom analysis is critical in:
- **Automotive assembly lines** — articulated 6-DoF arms for spot welding car bodies.
- **Surgical robotics** — the da Vinci system uses 7-DoF instruments for intracorporeal dexterity.
- **Space robotics** — the Canadarm uses 7 DoF to approach and grasp tumbling satellites.
- **Rehabilitation robotics** — exoskeletons need to match human joint DoF for natural motion.
- **Parallel manipulators** — flight simulators use the 6-DoF Stewart platform.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Grübler-Kutzbach Criterion for Spatial Mechanisms

**Starting Concept:** We want a general formula to compute the mobility (DoF) of any mechanism, given the number of links and the types of joints connecting them.

**Step 1 — Count the unconstrained freedoms of all links.**

If we have $n$ free rigid bodies in 3D space, each would independently possess 6 DoF. Therefore, the total unconstrained freedoms are:

$$
F_{\text{total}} = 6n
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the degrees of freedom of the ground (fixed link).**

The ground link is stationary and consumes no independent DoF. To exclude it, we subtract 6 for the ground:

$$
F_{\text{free links}} = 6n - 6 = 6(n - 1)
$$

This is the total freedom available to all *moving* links when connected through joints.

**Step 3 — Subtract the constraints imposed by each joint.**

A joint with $i$ degrees of freedom removes $(6 - i)$ degrees of freedom from the system. If we have $j_1$ joints with 1 DoF, $j_2$ joints with 2 DoF, ..., up to $j_5$ joints with 5 DoF, the total constraints removed are:

$$
F_{\text{constraints}} = (6 - 1)j_1 + (6 - 2)j_2 + (6 - 3)j_3 + (6 - 4)j_4 + (6 - 5)j_5
$$

Expanding:

$$
F_{\text{constraints}} = 5j_1 + 4j_2 + 3j_3 + 2j_4 + j_5
$$

**Step 4 — Combine the free-link freedom and the constraint removal.**

$$
\text{DoF} = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

This is the **Grübler-Kutzbach Criterion for spatial mechanisms**.

**Step 5 — Verify with a simple example.**

Consider a 2-link planar pendulum with 2 revolute joints.

For a planar mechanism, the analogous derivation yields:

$$
\text{DoF} = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

Substituting $n = 3$ (two links + ground), $j_1 = 2$ (two revolute joints, each 1 DoF), $j_2 = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DoF} &= 3(3 - 1) - 2(2) - 0 \\
&= 3(2) - 4 \\
&= 6 - 4 \\
&= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches intuition: each link can swing independently about its joint, giving 2 independent inputs.

### 3.2 Analytical Example: Articulated (RRR) Robot Arm with Spherical Wrist

**Problem:** A robot manipulator has the following kinematic chain: Ground → Revolute → Revolute → Revolute → Spherical wrist (3 intersecting revolute joints). Calculate the total DoF.

**Step 1 — Identify the number of links.**

$n = 7$ (ground + 3 arm links + 3 wrist links)

**Step 2 — Identify the joint types and their DoF.**

- Joint 1 (Revolute): 1 DoF
- Joint 2 (Revolute): 1 DoF
- Joint 3 (Revolute): 1 DoF
- Joint 4 (Revolute in wrist): 1 DoF
- Joint 5 (Revolute in wrist): 1 DoF
- Joint 6 (Revolute in wrist): 1 DoF

So $j_1 = 6$, $j_2 = 0$, $j_3 = 0$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Grübler-Kutzbach formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DoF} &= 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5 \\
&= 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0 \\
&= 6 \times 6 - 30 \\
&= 36 - 30 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The articulated arm with a spherical wrist has **6 DoF** — the industry-standard configuration for full dexterous manipulation.

### 3.3 Analytical Example: SCARA Robot

**Problem:** A SCARA robot has the chain: Ground → Revolute → Revolute → Prismatic. Calculate the DoF.

**Step 1 — Count links and joints.**

$n = 4$ (ground + link 1 + link 2 + end-effector link)
$j_1 = 3$ (two revolute + one prismatic, each 1 DoF)

**Step 2 — Apply the Grübler-Kutzbach formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DoF} &= 6(4 - 1) - 5(3) \\
&= 18 - 15 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add an optional rotational wrist (if present).**

A SCARA with a single revolute wrist joint becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DoF} &= 6(5 - 1) - 5(4) \\
&= 24 - 20 \\
&= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** SCARA robots with only 3–4 DoF are excellent for *vertical insertion* tasks (e.g., placing chips on PCBs) because the work is planar, so orientation in the vertical plane is fixed by gravity and the insertion direction.

### 3.4 DoF vs. Task Adequacy — Decision Matrix

| Task Type | Minimum Required DoF | Typical Robot |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------|
| Pick-and-place (no orientation constraint) | 3 | Cartesian, SCARA |
| Pick-and-place (with orientation) | 4 | SCARA + wrist |
| General 3D manipulation (any orientation) | 6 | Articulated with wrist |
| Obstacle avoidance in cluttered workspace | 7+ | Redundant manipulator |
| Human-like dexterity (e.g., surgery) | 7+ | da Vinci, humanoid hand |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: The 6 Degrees of Freedom of a Free Rigid Body

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the decomposition of a free rigid body's 6 degrees of freedom into its translational and rotational components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RIGID BODY IN 3D SPACE"] --> B["6 TOTAL DEGREES OF FREEDOM"]
    B --> C["3 TRANSLATIONAL DOF"]
    B --> D["3 ROTATIONAL DOF"]
    C --> C1["X AXIS - SURGE"]
    C --> C2["Y AXIS - SWAY"]
    C --> C3["Z AXIS - HEAVE"]
    D --> D1["X AXIS - ROLL"]
    D --> D2["Y AXIS - PITCH"]
    D --> D3["Z AXIS - YAW"]
    A -.-> E["GROUND REFERENCE FRAME"]
    E --> F["X Y Z COORDINATE SYSTEM"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: DoF Analysis Pipeline for a Robot Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["STEP 1: IDENTIFY LINKS N"] --> P2["STEP 2: IDENTIFY JOINTS AND TYPES"]
    P2 --> P3["STEP 3: COUNT JOINTS BY DOF CLASS"]
    P3 --> P4["STEP 4: APPLY GRUBLER KUTZBACH FORMULA"]
    P4 --> P5["STEP 5: VALIDATE AGAINST TASK REQUIREMENTS"]
    P5 --> P6["DECISION: SUFFICIENT OR REDUNDANT"]
    P6 --> Q1["IF SUFFICIENT 6 DOF FOR FULL DEXTERITY"]
    P6 --> Q2["IF REDUNDANT MORE THAN 6 DOF"]
    P6 --> Q3["IF DEFICIENT LESS THAN 6 DOF"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Robot Configuration Mapping to DoF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["ROBOT MANIPULATOR"] --> R1["CARTESIAN PPP"]
    R --> R2["CYLINDRICAL RPP"]
    R --> R3["SPHERICAL RRP"]
    R --> R4["SCARA RRP"]
    R --> R5["ARTICULATED RRR"]
    R1 --> S1["3 DOF ARM"]
    R2 --> S2["3 DOF ARM"]
    R3 --> S3["3 DOF ARM"]
    R4 --> S4["3 DOF ARM"]
    R5 --> S5["3 DOF ARM"]
    S1 --> W1["OPTIONAL WRIST 6 DOF TOTAL"]
    S2 --> W2["OPTIONAL WRIST 6 DOF TOTAL"]
    S3 --> W3["OPTIONAL WRIST 6 DOF TOTAL"]
    S4 --> W4["OPTIONAL WRIST 4 OR 6 DOF TOTAL"]
    S5 --> W5["OPTIONAL WRIST 6 DOF TOTAL"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Joint-DoF Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J["SELECT JOINT TYPE"] --> JT1["REVOLUTE R"]
    J --> JT2["PRISMATIC P"]
    J --> JT3["CYLINDRICAL C"]
    J --> JT4["SPHERICAL S"]
    J --> JT5["UNIVERSAL U"]
    JT1 --> JF1["1 DOF ROTATION ABOUT ONE AXIS"]
    JT2 --> JF2["1 DOF TRANSLATION ALONG ONE AXIS"]
    JT3 --> JF3["2 DOF ROTATION AND TRANSLATION COUPLED"]
    JT4 --> JF4["3 DOF ROTATION IN ALL DIRECTIONS"]
    JT5 --> JF5["2 DOF ROTATION ABOUT TWO AXES"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** All diagrams above use the **Grübler-Kutzbach methodology** as the analytical backbone. The decision flow guides a designer from link/joint enumeration to DoF computation to final task-validation check.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Degrees of Freedom of a robot. State the number of degrees of freedom of a free rigid body in 3-dimensional space.**

**Model Answer:**

Degrees of Freedom (DoF) of a robot is defined as the number of independent parameters or variables required to completely specify the position and orientation of the robot's end-effector (or any link) in 3D space with respect to a fixed reference frame.

For a free, unconstrained rigid body in 3D space, the total degrees of freedom is **6**, consisting of:
- 3 translational DoF (motion along X, Y, Z axes)
- 3 rotational DoF (rotation about X, Y, Z axes — also called roll, pitch, and yaw)

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Mentioning the break-up into 3 + 3 earns the third mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between a revolute joint and a prismatic joint with respect to degrees of freedom and motion type.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Revolute Joint (R) | Prismatic Joint (P) |
|-----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Degrees of Freedom | 1 | 1 |
| Type of Motion | Pure rotation about a fixed axis | Pure linear translation along a fixed axis |
| Relative Motion | Angular displacement $\theta$ | Linear displacement $d$ |
| Real-World Example | Human elbow, motor shaft | Hydraulic piston, linear slide |
| DH Parameter Variable | $\theta$ (joint angle) | $d$ (link offset) |

Both contribute exactly 1 DoF, but their kinematic effects are fundamentally different — revolute joints change orientation, while prismatic joints change position.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions of 14 marks typically have sub-parts (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks. Both sub-parts must be answered completely to score full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Explain the Grübler-Kutzbach criterion for computing the degrees of freedom of a spatial mechanism. State the formula and define all terms.**

**Model Solution:**

The Grübler-Kutzbach criterion is a topological formula used to compute the mobility (DoF) of a mechanism based on the number of links and the type of joints connecting them. It is derived by counting the total unconstrained freedoms of all free links and subtracting the constraints imposed by each joint.

**Formula:**

$$
\text{DoF} = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

**Definitions of Terms:**

- $n$ = Total number of links in the mechanism (including the ground link)
- $j_1$ = Number of joints with 1 DoF (e.g., revolute, prismatic, screw)
- $j_2$ = Number of joints with 2 DoF (e.g., cylindrical, universal)
- $j_3$ = Number of joints with 3 DoF (e.g., spherical / ball-and-socket)
- $j_4$ = Number of joints with 4 DoF (rare in practice)
- $j_5$ = Number of joints with 5 DoF (very rare in practice)

**Underlying Logic:**

1. Each free link in 3D space has 6 DoF, so $n$ free links would have $6n$ total DoF.
2. The ground link is fixed and removes 6 DoF, leaving $6(n-1)$.
3. Each joint with $i$ DoF removes $(6 - i)$ DoF from the system.
4. The remaining DoF represent the independent inputs (mobility) of the mechanism.

For planar mechanisms, the analogous 2D formula is:

$$
\text{DoF} = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] A 4-DOF SCARA robot has the following joint sequence: Revolute – Revolute – Prismatic – Revolute. Compute the total DoF using the Grübler-Kutzbach criterion and verify it matches the design specification.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Count the number of links.**

The robot has 4 joints, so the number of links is $n = 4 + 1 = 5$ (ground + 4 moving links).

**Step 2 — Classify the joints by their DoF.**

- Joint 1: Revolute → 1 DoF
- Joint 2: Revolute → 1 DoF
- Joint 3: Prismatic → 1 DoF
- Joint 4: Revolute → 1 DoF

Total: $j_1 = 4$, $j_2 = 0$, $j_3 = 0$, $j_4 = 0$, $j_5 = 0$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Grübler-Kutzbach formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DoF} &= 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5 \\
&= 6(5 - 1) - 5(4) - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0 \\
&= 6 \times 4 - 20 \\
&= 24 - 20 \\
&= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification.**

The computed DoF of 4 matches the design specification, confirming the SCARA robot's typical configuration suitable for planar pick-and-place with end-effector roll.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to count the number of *joints* as the number of *links*. Remember: **Number of links = Number of joints + 1** (because the ground link is also counted). Another frequent error is forgetting to classify joints by their DoF (e.g., treating a spherical joint as 1 DoF instead of 3 DoF). Always double-check the joint type before plugging values into the formula.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024] With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the six degrees of freedom of a free rigid body in 3-dimensional space. Why does a typical industrial robot require 6 DoF?**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
                ┌────────────────────────────┐
                │  RIGID BODY IN 3D SPACE    │
                │   Total DoF = 6            │
                └─────────────┬──────────────┘
                              │
              ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
              │                               │
   ┌──────────▼──────────┐         ┌──────────▼──────────┐
   │ 3 TRANSLATIONAL DOF │         │  3 ROTATIONAL DOF   │
   └──────────┬──────────┘         └──────────┬──────────┘
              │                               │
   ┌──────────┼──────────┐         ┌──────────┼──────────┐
   │          │          │         │          │          │
┌──▼──┐   ┌───▼──┐   ┌───▼──┐   ┌──▼──┐   ┌───▼──┐   ┌───▼──┐
│  X  │   │  Y  │   │  Z  │   │  X  │   │  Y  │   │  Z  │
│SURGE│   │SWAY │   │HEAVE│   │ROLL │   │PITCH│   │ YAW │
└─────┘   └─────┘   └─────┘   └─────┘   └─────┘   └─────┘
```

**Explanation:**

A free rigid body in 3D space possesses **6 independent degrees of freedom**:
- **3 Translational DoF:** Motion along the X (surge), Y (sway), and Z (heave) axes.
- **3 Rotational DoF:** Rotation about the X (roll), Y (pitch), and Z (yaw) axes.

**Why 6 DoF for an Industrial Robot?**

A robot manipulator must position its end-effector at a target location $(x, y, z)$ and orient it in a desired direction (roll, pitch, yaw). This requires 3 position variables + 3 orientation variables = **6 independent parameters**. Therefore, a robot with 6 DoF can, in principle, reach any arbitrary pose in its workspace.

- **Fewer than 6 DoF:** The robot cannot achieve arbitrary orientation — it is *kinematically deficient*.
- **More than 6 DoF:** The robot is *kinematically redundant* — multiple joint configurations yield the same end-effector pose, providing flexibility for obstacle avoidance and singular configuration avoidance.
- **Exactly 6 DoF:** The robot is *kinematically sufficient* for general-purpose manipulation, which is the industry standard.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024] An articulated robot manipulator has 6 revolute joints arranged as follows: 3 joints form the arm (shoulder, elbow, wrist-pitch) and 3 joints form the spherical wrist. Calculate the DoF using the Grübler-Kutzbach criterion and justify why 6 DoF is considered the optimal choice for general-purpose manipulation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Count the links.**

Total joints = 6. Therefore, number of links $n = 6 + 1 = 7$ (ground + 6 moving links).

**Step 2 — Classify the joints.**

- Joint 1 (Shoulder Revolute): 1 DoF
- Joint 2 (Elbow Revolute): 1 DoF
- Joint 3 (Wrist-pitch Revolute): 1 DoF
- Joint 4 (Wrist-roll Revolute): 1 DoF
- Joint 5 (Wrist-yaw Revolute): 1 DoF
- Joint 6 (End Revolute): 1 DoF

All joints are revolute with 1 DoF: $j_1 = 6$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Grübler-Kutzbach formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DoF} &= 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 \\
&= 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) \\
&= 36 - 30 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Justification for 6 DoF as the optimal choice.**

A 6-DoF robot is considered optimal because:

1. **Sufficient Kinematics:** It can position the end-effector at any point $(x, y, z)$ in 3D space with any orientation (roll, pitch, yaw) within its dexterous workspace.
2. **Engineering Economy:** Six actuators and six joint structures are mechanically and economically manageable — adding more DoF (redundancy) increases cost, control complexity, and maintenance overhead.
3. **Dexterous Workspace:** A well-designed 6-DoF articulated arm can reach the largest dexterous workspace (the set of points where full orientation is achievable) of any non-redundant configuration.
4. **Standardization:** 6 DoF has become the de-facto industry standard for arc welding, painting, material handling, and assembly operations.
5. **Avoidance of Deficiency:** With fewer than 6 DoF, the robot cannot orient the tool freely — limiting it to specific tasks like pick-and-place (3 DoF Cartesian) or planar insertion (SCARA).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse *links* and *joints*. The formula requires **$n$ = number of links** (including ground), and the simplest mnemonic is **"links = joints + 1"** for a single-chain serial manipulator. Also, do not assume a wrist joint is automatically a spherical joint — check the problem statement carefully. A common mark-loss point is forgetting to subtract the 6 DoF of the ground link.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Degrees of Freedom (KTU Module 1)**

- **Definition:** DoF = number of independent parameters to fully specify a robot's configuration in space.
- **Free Rigid Body in 3D:** Exactly **6 DoF** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
- **Free Rigid Body in 2D:** Exactly **3 DoF** (2 translations + 1 rotation).
- **Industrial Standard:** A 6-DoF robot is *kinematically sufficient* for full dexterous manipulation.
- **Joint DoF Rule:** Revolute (R) and Prismatic (P) joints each contribute 1 DoF; Cylindrical and Universal contribute 2 DoF; Spherical/Ball contributes 3 DoF.
- **Standard Configurations:** Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), SCARA (RRP), Articulated (RRR) — each has 3 arm DoF, with optional 3-DoF wrist yielding 6 DoF total.
- **Grübler-Kutzbach (Spatial):** $\text{DoF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
- **Grübler-Kutzbach (Planar):** $\text{DoF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
- **Key Mnemonic:** Number of **links = Number of joints + 1** (for a single serial chain).
- **Redundancy:** DoF > 6 means kinematic redundancy (multiple solutions to inverse kinematics).
- **Deficiency:** DoF < 6 means kinematic deficiency (cannot achieve arbitrary orientation).
- **Most Examined Topic in KTU:** The Grübler-Kutzbach calculation, joint type identification, and the 6-DoF justification question.
- **Pose Vector:** $\vec{\xi} = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ — 6 components, one for each DoF.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Considerations for an Application - Number of Axes, Work Volume, Capacity & Speed, Stroke & Reach, Repeatability, Precision and Accuracy, Operating Environment, Point to Point Control or Continuous Path Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET831 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robot Considerations for an Application - Number of Axes, Work Volume, Capacity & Speed, Stroke & Reach, Repeatability, Precision and Accuracy, Operating Environment, Point to Point Control or Continuous Path Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Robot Considerations for an Application — KTU 2024 Module 1

## 1.1 The Big Picture: Why Engineers "Spec" a Robot Before Buying It

Before a single robot arm is bolted to a factory floor, a robotics engineer must answer a fundamental question: **"Given the task, which robot is the right robot?"** This is the essence of *Robot Selection for an Application*. A welding robot, a pick-and-place SCARA, and a surgical robot share mechanical bones but are engineered for wildly different operating envelopes, accuracies, and environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 1 explicitly lists the *robot considerations* an engineer must evaluate when justifying a robot for a given application. These considerations are the bridge between **mechanical design specifications** and **industrial deployment decisions**.

The eight core considerations we will dissect in this chapter are:

1. **Number of Axes (Degrees of Freedom)**
2. **Work Volume (Work Envelope)**
3. **Capacity & Speed**
4. **Stroke & Reach**
5. **Repeatability**
6. **Precision and Accuracy**
7. **Operating Environment**
8. **Path Control Type (PTP vs. Continuous Path)**

---

## 1.2 Core Technical Definitions

### 1. Number of Axes (Degrees of Freedom — DOF)

**Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**  
The *Number of Axes* of a robot refers to the count of **independent motions** (rotational or translational) the robot can perform, equivalently known as **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**. For spatial positioning of an end-effector in 3D space, **3 DOF are mathematically required**; for arbitrary orientation, **3 more DOF are required**, totaling **6 DOF** for a fully versatile robot.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 3+3 Rule:** Any rigid body in 3D space has 3 translational DOF (X, Y, Z) and 3 rotational DOF (Roll, Pitch, Yaw) = **6 DOF total**. This is the holy grail of full spatial mobility.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of your own arm. Your shoulder gives you 3 rotational axes, your elbow gives 1, and your wrist gives 3 more — totalling **7 DOF**. A robot with 6 DOF mimics this dexterity; one with only 3 or 4 is like a constrained pick-and-place claw.

- **3 DOF robots** → Position only (e.g., simple gantry).
- **4 DOF robots** → Position + 1 orientation (e.g., SCARA for assembly).
- **5 DOF robots** → Common in welding (lose one redundant wrist roll).
- **6 DOF robots** → Universal; used in painting, machining, surgery.
- **7+ DOF robots** → Redundant manipulators (e.g., NASA/DARPA humanoids).

---

### 2. Work Volume (Work Envelope)

**Formal Definition:**  
The *Work Volume* (or *Workspace*) is the **three-dimensional region in space that can be reached by the robot's end-effector** (specifically, the wrist center or tool mounting flange) when all joints are moved through their full mechanical limits. It is measured in **cubic meters (m³)** or **cubic feet (ft³)**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine drawing in the air with your fingertip while keeping your shoulder fixed. The cloud of points your fingertip can touch *is* your work volume. A robot's work volume is its physical "footprint" extended into space.

- **Cartesian Robot** → Rectangular cuboid work volume.
- **Cylindrical Robot** → Hollow cylinder plus two rectangular slabs.
- **Spherical (Polar) Robot** → Hollow sphere.
- **Articulated (Jointed-Arm) Robot** → Complex irregular shape, often approximated as a torus-segment.

---

### 3. Capacity & Speed

**Capacity (Payload):** The **maximum mass** the robot can carry at the end-effector while still meeting all other specifications (repeatability, speed, acceleration). Measured in **kilograms (kg)** or **pounds (lb)**. It is *not* just the mass of the part — it includes the **end-effector, fixtures, and any tooling**.

**Speed:** Two metrics:
- **Maximum Speed** (m/s) — top linear velocity of the end-effector.
- **Cycle Time** (seconds) — time to complete a standardized pick-and-place cycle (often the "standard cycle" benchmark).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Rule of Thumb:** Payload and speed are *inversely related*. A robot rated 10 kg at full speed may lift 20 kg at 50% speed. Always check the **payload-speed derating curve** from the manufacturer's datasheet.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A sprinter carrying a heavy backpack (high payload) cannot run as fast as without it (low payload). The robot experiences the exact same Newton's-Law trade-off.

---

### 4. Stroke & Reach

- **Reach:** The **maximum distance** from the robot's base center to the farthest point in the work envelope. Reach is a *single scalar value* (e.g., 1.5 m).
- **Stroke:** The **total range of motion** of a single joint or a single linear axis. Often used to describe the travel of prismatic joints (e.g., "X-axis stroke = 2 m").

**Intuitive Analogy:** *Reach* is like the wingspan of a bird (max tip-to-tip distance). *Stroke* is like the back-and-forth motion of one wing (single joint range).

---

### 5. Repeatability

**Formal Definition:**  
*Repeatability* is the robot's ability to **return to a previously taught position** when commanded repeatedly from the same location, under identical conditions of load, speed, and environment. It is a **statistical measure** — typically reported as the **radius of a sphere** (in mm or µm) within which the robot will return to that point **with a probability of 99% (or 99.5%)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Repeatability is the parameter most KTU numerical problems focus on. It is **NOT** the same as accuracy!

**Intuitive Analogy:** You try to thread a needle ten times — if you stab the same spot every time, you are repeatable. Whether that spot is *actually* the center of the needle is a different question (that's *accuracy*).

---

### 6. Precision and Accuracy

- **Accuracy:** The robot's ability to **move to a commanded (target) position in absolute space**. It is the deviation between the *commanded position* and the *mean of the actually attained positions*, over many trials.
- **Precision (Resolution):** The **smallest incremental movement** the robot's controller can command. Determined by the encoder resolution, gear ratios, and bit-depth of the controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Target Analogy":**  
> Imagine a dartboard. The *bullseye* is the commanded position.  
> - **Accuracy** = how close the *average* of your darts is to the bullseye.  
> - **Precision** = how tightly *clustered* your darts are (independent of bullseye).  
> - **Repeatability** = a specific subset of precision applied to *returning* to a learned point.

**Mathematical Hierarchy (most important KTU relation):**

$$
\text{Accuracy} \;\geq\; \text{Repeatability} \;\geq\; \text{Resolution}
$$

---

### 7. Operating Environment

The environment in which a robot must operate imposes **hard constraints** on its design, materials, sealing, and electronics.

| Environment Type | Required Protection | Typical Applications |
|---|---|---|
| Cleanroom (Class 1–100) | ISO-rated sealing, no particulates | Semiconductor, pharmaceutical |
| Washdown / Wet | IP67/IP69K stainless steel | Food, beverage, dairy |
| Dusty / Foundry | Sealed joints, heat shielding | Casting, forging |
| Hazardous / Explosive | ATEX/IECEx certified, intrinsically safe | Oil & gas, paint booths |
| Extreme Temperature | Thermal insulation, special lubricants | Cold storage, furnaces |
| Vacuum / Space | Outgassing-free materials, radiation-hardened | Space robotics, semiconductor fabs |

---

### 8. Point-to-Point (PTP) Control vs. Continuous Path (CP) Control

- **Point-to-Point (PTP) Control:** The robot is programmed to move from point A to point B, but the **path taken between them is unimportant**. Only the start and end positions matter. Used in **spot welding, pick-and-place, palletizing, drilling, and assembly**.

- **Continuous Path (CP) Control:** The robot must follow a **precisely defined trajectory** through space, with hundreds or thousands of intermediate points specified. The **path itself is the product**. Used in **arc welding, painting, polishing, spray coating, seam tracking, and machining**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:**  
> *"PTP cares about the dots, CP cares about the line connecting them."*  
> If you can stop the robot mid-motion without ruining the task → **PTP**.  
> If stopping mid-motion ruins the task → **CP**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** 
- **PTP** is like taking an Uber — the driver picks the route, you only care about the destination.
- **CP** is like driving a Ferrari on a racetrack — the path (racing line) is the entire point of the exercise.

---

## 1.3 Quick-Reference Summary Table

| Consideration | Symbol / Unit | "Quick" Question to Ask |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Axes | DOF | How complex is the task's geometry? |
| Work Volume | m³ | Does the workpiece fit inside the envelope? |
| Capacity | kg | What is total mass at the wrist? |
| Speed | m/s, s/cycle | How fast must the cycle run? |
| Stroke & Reach | m | Can the arm reach every fixture? |
| Repeatability | mm | Will the part assembly tolerate the error? |
| Accuracy | mm | Is the absolute target position critical? |
| Precision/Resolution | mm or µm | How small a move can the controller make? |
| Operating Environment | IP/ISO/ATEX rating | Will the robot survive the shop floor? |
| Path Control | PTP / CP | Is the path or the endpoint the product? |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Number of Axes — Theoretical Framework

The *Number of Axes* (or Degrees of Freedom) determines the **mobility and dexterity** of a robotic manipulator. The KTU theory hinges on **two intertwined concepts**:

### 2.1.1 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — Grubler's Equation

For a planar mechanism with `n` links and `j₁` lower-pair (1-DOF) joints, the DOF is:

$$
M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

For a spatial mechanism:

$$
M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

where `jₖ` denotes the number of joints providing `k` DOF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 6 DOF = Full Spatial Mobility:** A rigid body in 3D has 3 translational + 3 rotational = 6 independent motions. A manipulator with 6 DOF can, in principle, place its end-effector at any (X, Y, Z) and any orientation (Roll, Pitch, Yaw).

### 2.1.2 Practical DOF Classification

| DOF | Type | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| 3 | Position only | Simple gantry loaders |
| 4 | Position + 1 orientation | SCARA assembly |
| 5 | Welding, light machining | Automotive body shops |
| 6 | Universal | Painting, CNC tending, surgery |
| 7+ | Redundant | Humanoid arms, snake robots |

> [!TIP]
> **Redundancy Theorem:** A robot has *kinematic redundancy* when its DOF exceeds the DOF required by the task. Redundancy enables **obstacle avoidance, singularity avoidance, and joint-limit avoidance** simultaneously.

---

## 2.2 Work Volume — Theoretical Derivations

The *work volume* of a robot is **configuration-dependent**. Each joint geometry produces a distinctive envelope shape.

### 2.2.1 Cartesian (Rectangular / Gantry) Robot

Configuration: 3 prismatic joints (P-P-P). All joints move along orthogonal X, Y, Z axes.

$$
V_{\text{cartesian}} = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z
$$

where `Lₓ`, `Lᵧ`, `L_z` are the full stroke lengths of each linear axis.

### 2.2.2 Cylindrical Robot

Configuration: 1 vertical prismatic + 1 horizontal prismatic + 1 revolute. Envelope is a hollow cylinder plus a curved cap.

$$
V_{\text{cylindrical}} = \pi \cdot R^2 \cdot H
$$

where `R` is the maximum radial reach of the horizontal arm and `H` is the vertical stroke.

### 2.2.3 Spherical (Polar) Robot

Configuration: 1 revolute (base) + 1 prismatic (shoulder) + 1 revolute (elbow). Envelope is a portion of a hollow sphere.

$$
V_{\text{spherical}} = \frac{2}{3}\pi \cdot R^3 \cdot (1 - \cos\theta_{\max})
$$

where `R` is the maximum reach and `θ_max` is the maximum polar angle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For full spherical coverage (rare in practice, only in a few specialized designs), the formula reduces to the full sphere: $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3$.

### 2.2.4 Articulated (Jointed-Arm / Anthropomorphic) Robot

Configuration: 3 revolute joints (waist, shoulder, elbow) + 3 wrist DOF. The exact work volume is an **irregular torus-segment**. Engineers approximate using bounding volumes:

$$
V_{\text{articulated}} \approx \frac{2}{3}\pi^2 \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3^2
$$

where `a₂` is the upper-arm length and `a₃` is the forearm length (this is the standard Denavit-Hartenberg convention for an RRR manipulator with all joint limits at ±90°).

---

## 2.3 Capacity (Payload) and Speed — Engineering Trade-offs

### 2.3.1 Payload Definition

$$
P_{\text{total}} = m_{\text{part}} + m_{\text{end-effector}} + m_{\text{fixture}}
$$

A robot rated for 10 kg payload cannot lift 10 kg of part + 3 kg gripper; it can only lift **7 kg of part** in that configuration.

### 2.3.2 Speed and Cycle Time

**Cycle time** is typically benchmarked as a 1-inch pick, 12-inch vertical lift, 12-inch horizontal traverse, 1-inch place motion (a "standard cycle" used by Adept, FANUC, and Yaskawa).

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\text{pick}} + t_{\text{traverse}} + t_{\text{place}} + t_{\text{return}}
$$

Each term is bounded by the maximum joint velocity:

$$
\omega_{\max} = \frac{d\theta}{dt}\bigg|_{\max} \quad \text{[rad/s]}, \qquad
v_{\max} = \frac{ds}{dt}\bigg|_{\max} \quad \text{[m/s]}
$$

### 2.3.3 Payload-Speed Trade-off (Manufacturer Curves)

Industrial datasheets specify a **payload-speed derating curve**, often linear:

$$
v_{\text{actual}} = v_{\max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\max}}\right)
$$

This is empirical; actual curves are non-linear near the edges of the envelope.

---

## 2.4 Stroke and Reach — Geometric Analysis

### 2.4.1 Reach Definition (Revolute Joints)

For a revolute joint, **reach** is the **distance from the joint axis to the end-effector** when all links are extended in a straight line:

$$
R_{\text{reach}} = a_1 + a_2 + \ldots + a_n
$$

where `aᵢ` are the link lengths of the kinematic chain (DH parameters).

### 2.4.2 Stroke Definition (Prismatic Joints)

For a prismatic joint, **stroke** is the **maximum displacement** of the link:

$$
S = x_{\max} - x_{\min}
$$

---

## 2.5 Repeatability, Precision, and Accuracy — The Core Triad

This is the **highest-yield KTU topic** in this module. Engineers and KTU examiners test the relationships between these three parameters intensively.

### 2.5.1 Repeatability (Statistical Definition)

If the robot is commanded `N` times to return to position P, and the achieved positions form a cluster, repeatability is defined as:

$$
\text{Repeatability} = \pm k \cdot \sigma
$$

where `σ` is the standard deviation of the position cluster and `k` depends on the probability level:
- `k = 2.0` → ~95% confidence
- `k = 2.5` → ~99% confidence  
- `k = 3.0` → 99.7% confidence (the most common KTU figure)

> [!NOTE]
> Modern industrial robots specify repeatability in the **±** form. For example, "±0.02 mm" means the robot will return within a sphere of radius 0.02 mm, 99.7% of the time.

### 2.5.2 Accuracy Definition

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \sqrt{(x_c - \bar{x})^2 + (y_c - \bar{y})^2 + (z_c - \bar{z})^2}
$$

where `(x_c, y_c, z_c)` is the commanded position and `(\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z})` is the mean of the achieved positions.

### 2.5.3 Precision / Resolution Definition

Resolution is the **smallest change in position** the controller can command:

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{Full Range of Joint}}{\text{Number of Encoder Counts}} = \frac{\theta_{\max}}{2^n \cdot \text{gear ratio}}
$$

where `n` is the encoder bit-depth. For a 16-bit encoder with a 100:1 gear ratio on a joint with 180° range:

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{180°}{2^{16} \times 100} = \frac{180°}{6{,}553{,}600} \approx 2.7 \times 10^{-5} \text{ degrees}
$$

### 2.5.4 The Inherent Inequality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Inequality:**  
> $$\boxed{\text{Accuracy} \;\geq\; \text{Repeatability} \;\geq\; \text{Resolution}}$$
> 
> **Why?** Resolution sets the floor (you cannot repeat better than the controller's smallest step). Repeatability cannot exceed accuracy (you cannot consistently return to a point if you cannot consistently reach a point). The KTU examiner **always** tests this relationship.

### 2.5.5 Three Scenarios (Dartboard Visualization)

| Scenario | Accuracy | Repeatability | Resolution | Industrial Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Clustered on bullseye | High | High | High | Perfect robot |
| Clustered off bullseye | Low | High | High | Needs calibration |
| Spread around bullseye | Low | Low | High | Bad mechanics or worn bearings |
| Single point, controller limited | High | High | Low | Good for macro tasks, bad for precision |

---

## 2.6 Operating Environment — Standards

| Standard | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **IP rating** (IEC 60529) | Ingress Protection (e.g., IP67 = dust-tight + 1 m submersion) |
| **ISO 14644-1** | Cleanroom class (Class 1 = 1 particle/m³) |
| **ATEX 2014/34/EU** | EU explosive atmospheres directive |
| **NEMA** | North American enclosure standard (e.g., NEMA 4X = washdown) |
| **IEC 61508** | Functional safety for safety-critical robots |

---

## 2.7 PTP vs. CP Control — Algorithmic Differences

### 2.7.1 PTP Control
- The controller records only the **start and end poses**.
- The trajectory planner generates a **smooth interpolated path** (e.g., joint-interpolated, straight-line, or arc), but the user **does not define the intermediate path**.
- Typically, **all joints reach the target simultaneously** (joint-interpolated motion) to minimize cycle time.

### 2.7.2 CP Control
- The controller records a **dense sequence of waypoints** (hundreds to thousands).
- The end-effector **must follow** the path through every recorded waypoint within tight tolerances.
- Used when **path geometry is the product** (e.g., a weld bead, a paint layer).

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Memory Aid for KTU:** PTP cycle times are typically **30–50% shorter** than CP for the same start–end distance, because the path is unconstrained and can be optimized for speed.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DOF (spatial) | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | dimensionless | Grubler's equation |
| 2 | Full spatial DOF | $M_{\text{full}} = 6$ | dimensionless | 3 trans + 3 rot |
| 3 | Cartesian work volume | $V = L_x L_y L_z$ | m³ | Gantry robots |
| 4 | Cylindrical work volume | $V = \pi R^2 H$ | m³ | Cylindrical envelope |
| 5 | Spherical work volume | $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi R^3 (1 - \cos\theta_{\max})$ | m³ | Polar arm |
| 6 | Articulated work volume | $V \approx \frac{2}{3}\pi^2 a_2 a_3^2$ | m³ | Approximation |
| 7 | Total payload | $P = m_{\text{part}} + m_{\text{ee}} + m_{\text{fix}}$ | kg | End-effector included |
| 8 | Cycle time | $T = t_{\text{pick}} + t_{\text{traverse}} + t_{\text{place}} + t_{\text{return}}$ | s | Standard cycle |
| 9 | Reach (serial linkage) | $R = \sum a_i$ | m | Sum of link lengths |
| 10 | Repeatability | $R_p = \pm k\sigma$ | mm | k=3 for 99.7% |
| 11 | Accuracy (Euclidean) | $A = \sqrt{(x_c-\bar{x})^2 + (y_c-\bar{y})^2 + (z_c-\bar{z})^2}$ | mm | Mean of cluster |
| 12 | Resolution | $\Delta = \frac{\theta_{\max}}{2^n \cdot G}$ | deg/count | n = encoder bits |
| 13 | Master inequality | $\text{Accuracy} \geq \text{Repeatability} \geq \text{Resolution}$ | — | Most-tested KTU fact |
| 14 | PTP-CP cycle ratio | $T_{\text{PTP}} \approx 0.5\,T_{\text{CP}}$ | — | Empirical rule |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** All table cells use the LaTeX `\vert` (or similar) where mathematical absolute value is needed, NOT the markdown pipe `\|`, to prevent the table from breaking.
