# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Work Volume of a Cylindrical Robot

### Problem Statement
A cylindrical-coordinate robot has a horizontal arm with a maximum radial extension of `R = 1.2 m` and a vertical stroke of `H = 0.8 m`. Calculate the work volume.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the configuration. The robot is a cylindrical robot, so we apply the cylindrical work volume formula.

**Step 2:** Write down the governing equation from the formula sheet.

$$
V_{\text{cylindrical}} = \pi R^2 H
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$
V_{\text{cylindrical}} = \pi \times (1.2\,\text{m})^2 \times (0.8\,\text{m})
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the square of the radius.

$$
(1.2)^2 = 1.44\,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply through.

$$
V_{\text{cylindrical}} = \pi \times 1.44 \times 0.8
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the numerical product.

$$
V_{\text{cylindrical}} = \pi \times 1.152 = 3.619\,\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 7:** Final answer with units.

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{cylindrical}} \approx 3.62\,\text{m}^3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **reachable volume** of the wrist center. The actual **task volume** (where the end-effector can do work) is smaller due to the tool dimensions, joint limits, and singularities.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Repeatability vs. Accuracy Distinction (Classic KTU Trap)

### Problem Statement
A pick-and-place robot has the following measured deviations from the commanded position (in mm) over 10 trials:

| Trial | $\Delta x$ | $\Delta y$ | $\Delta z$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | +0.10 | -0.05 | +0.02 |
| 2 | +0.09 | -0.06 | +0.01 |
| 3 | +0.11 | -0.04 | +0.03 |
| 4 | +0.10 | -0.05 | +0.02 |
| 5 | +0.10 | -0.05 | +0.02 |
| 6 | +0.09 | -0.05 | +0.01 |
| 7 | +0.10 | -0.06 | +0.02 |
| 8 | +0.11 | -0.05 | +0.03 |
| 9 | +0.10 | -0.05 | +0.02 |
| 10 | +0.10 | -0.04 | +0.02 |

Compute (a) the **accuracy** and (b) the **repeatability** of the robot.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Mean Deviations:**

$$
\bar{\Delta x} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10}\Delta x_i = \frac{1.00}{10} = +0.10\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
\bar{\Delta y} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10}\Delta y_i = \frac{-0.50}{10} = -0.05\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
\bar{\Delta z} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10}\Delta z_i = \frac{0.20}{10} = +0.02\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Accuracy (Euclidean Norm of Mean Deviation Vector):**

$$
A = \sqrt{(\bar{\Delta x})^2 + (\bar{\Delta y})^2 + (\bar{\Delta z})^2}
$$

$$
A = \sqrt{(0.10)^2 + (-0.05)^2 + (0.02)^2}
$$

$$
A = \sqrt{0.0100 + 0.0025 + 0.0004}
$$

$$
A = \sqrt{0.0129} = 0.1136\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 0.114\,\text{mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Standard Deviations:**

For the X-axis:

$$
\sigma_x = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\Delta x_i - \bar{\Delta x})^2}
$$

Compute each $(\Delta x_i - \bar{\Delta x})^2$:

- (0.10 − 0.10)² = 0.0000
- (0.09 − 0.10)² = 0.0001
- (0.11 − 0.10)² = 0.0001
- (0.10 − 0.10)² = 0.0000
- (0.10 − 0.10)² = 0.0000
- (0.09 − 0.10)² = 0.0001
- (0.10 − 0.10)² = 0.0000
- (0.11 − 0.10)² = 0.0001
- (0.10 − 0.10)² = 0.0000
- (0.10 − 0.10)² = 0.0000

Sum = 0.0004

$$
\sigma_x = \sqrt{0.0004/10} = \sqrt{0.00004} = 0.0063\,\text{mm}
$$

By inspection, $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ are similarly small. Approximating the overall spatial standard deviation as the dominant component:

$$
\sigma \approx 0.0063\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Repeatability (3σ convention):**

$$
R_p = \pm 3\sigma = \pm 3 \times 0.0063 = \pm 0.019\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_p \approx \pm 0.019\,\text{mm}}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

- Accuracy (0.114 mm) is **poor** (the robot consistently misses the target by ~0.1 mm).
- Repeatability (0.019 mm) is **excellent** (the robot is very consistent).
- This is the classic **"high repeatability, low accuracy"** scenario → the robot needs **calibration**, not new mechanics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** A common exam question gives the same kind of data and asks "is the robot suitable for the application?" The answer is "**It depends on the task**: if absolute target position matters (e.g., hole drilling on a plate), the robot is unsuitable without calibration. If the task uses *taught positions* (e.g., spot welding against a fixture), the robot is perfectly fine."

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Resolution from Encoder Specifications

### Problem Statement
A robot joint has a total rotation range of $\theta_{\max} = 180°$, an encoder with $n = 16$ bits, and a gear reduction ratio of $G = 50$. Compute the controller resolution.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Recall the resolution formula.

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{\theta_{\max}}{2^n \cdot G}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $2^n$.

$$
2^{16} = 65{,}536
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{180°}{65{,}536 \times 50} = \frac{180°}{3{,}276{,}800}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute.

$$
\Delta\theta = 5.49 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{degrees}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to arcseconds (KTU examiners love this).

$$
1° = 3600'' \implies \Delta\theta = 5.49 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 \approx 0.198\,\text{arcseconds}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta\theta \approx 5.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{°} \approx 0.2''}
$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation.  
The controller can command joint increments of about **0.2 arc-seconds**. At a 1-meter reach, this corresponds to a linear resolution of:

$$
\Delta x = R \cdot \Delta\theta_{\text{rad}} = 1\,\text{m} \times \left(\frac{5.49 \times 10^{-5} \times \pi}{180}\right) \approx 9.6 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} \approx 1\,\mu\text{m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The resolution (≈1 µm at 1 m reach) is much smaller than the repeatability (typically 20–50 µm for industrial robots). This confirms the **Resolution < Repeatability** part of the master inequality.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Choosing Between PTP and CP Control

### Problem Statement
A manufacturing engineer must decide whether to use PTP or CP control for the following four tasks:

1. Pick-and-place of biscuits onto a conveyor.
2. Arc welding of a car chassis.
3. Spot welding of sheet metal.
4. Spray painting of a car body.

### Solution

For each task, we ask: **"If the robot were stopped mid-motion, would the task be ruined?"**

| Task | If stopped mid-motion... | Control Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Biscuit pick-and-place | Biscuit lands in air, robot continues; **task is OK** | **PTP** | Only endpoints matter |
| 2. Arc welding chassis | Weld bead breaks mid-seam; **task ruined** | **CP** | Seam continuity is the product |
| 3. Spot welding metal | Spot is complete before motion; **task OK** | **PTP** | Spot is a discrete point |
| 4. Spray painting body | Paint layer is uneven; **task ruined** | **CP** | Coating uniformity is the product |

$$
\boxed{\text{Tasks 1 and 3 → PTP; Tasks 2 and 4 → CP}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Multi-Constraint Robot Selection

### Problem Statement  
A factory is to be automated for the following operation:  
*"Pick a 5 kg part from a conveyor and place it onto a machining fixture located 1.3 m horizontally and 0.4 m vertically above the conveyor. The required cycle time is 6 seconds, and the positional repeatability must be better than 0.05 mm. The factory floor is wet and dusty."*

Select the appropriate robot configuration.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Evaluate the Work Volume Requirement:**

We need a robot that can reach 1.3 m horizontally and 0.4 m vertically. The required reach is:

$$
R = \sqrt{1.3^2 + 0.4^2} = \sqrt{1.69 + 0.16} = \sqrt{1.85} \approx 1.36\,\text{m}
$$

Therefore, the robot's reach must be at least 1.4 m (with 5–10% safety margin).

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Payload Requirement:**

Assuming the end-effector is 2 kg and the fixture is 1 kg:

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 5 + 2 + 1 = 8\,\text{kg}
$$

The robot must be rated for **at least 10 kg** (with 20% safety margin).

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Repeatability:**

The required repeatability is 0.05 mm, which is well within the capability of **SCARA or Cartesian robots** (typical industrial repeatability 0.02–0.05 mm). A standard articulated arm (typical 0.1 mm) is **insufficient**.

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Operating Environment:**

Wet and dusty conditions require an **IP65 or higher** rated robot, with sealed joints and corrosion-resistant surfaces.

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Cycle Time:**

A 6-second cycle is moderate. Both PTP and CP control can achieve this.

**Step 6 — Select the Configuration:**

| Criterion | Selected | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Reach ≥ 1.4 m | SCARA, Cartesian, or Articulated | All feasible |
| Payload ≥ 10 kg | Any industrial robot | Achievable |
| Repeatability ≤ 0.05 mm | **SCARA or Cartesian** | Articulated is borderline |
| IP65 rating | Required | Stainless option needed |
| Cycle ≤ 6 s | Achievable | Standard industrial spec |

**Decision:** A **4-axis SCARA robot** with **IP65 rating** and a **10 kg payload** is the optimal choice. The SCARA offers:
- High speed (ideal for cycle-time-critical pick-and-place).
- Excellent repeatability in the vertical plane.
- Lower cost than an articulated arm.
- Easy sealing for wet/dusty environments.

$$
\boxed{\text{Recommended: 4-axis SCARA robot, IP65, 10 kg payload, reach ≥ 1.4 m}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Consideration

| Application Domain | Primary Considerations | Typical Configuration |
|---|---|---|
| **Spot welding** | Reach, payload, PTP | Articulated 6-DOF |
| **Arc welding** | Reach, CP control, accuracy | Articulated 6-DOF |
| **Painting** | Reach, CP control, hazardous env. | Articulated 6-DOF (explosion-proof) |
| **Pick & place** | Speed, repeatability, cycle time | SCARA (4-DOF) or Cartesian |
| **Palletizing** | Reach, payload, PTP | Articulated 4–6 DOF |
| **Assembly** | Repeatability, accuracy, resolution | SCARA or Cartesian |
| **Machine tending** | Reach, payload, IP rating | Articulated 6-DOF |
| **Surgery** | Accuracy, precision, cleanroom | Custom 7-DOF |
| **Semiconductor** | Cleanroom, precision, vibration | Cartesian with air bearings |
| **Inspection** | Reach, accuracy, vision integration | Articulated 6-DOF |

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the terms "Accuracy" and "Repeatability" of a robot. How are they related mathematically?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Accuracy** is the ability of a robot to move to a commanded (target) position in absolute space. Mathematically, it is the deviation between the commanded position and the mean of the actually attained positions over many trials.

**Repeatability** is the ability of the robot to return to a previously taught position when commanded repeatedly from the same location, under identical conditions. Statistically, it is the radius of a sphere (typically ±3σ) within which the robot returns to the taught point 99.7% of the time.

**Mathematical Relationship:**

$$
\text{Accuracy} \;\geq\; \text{Repeatability}
$$

A robot with high repeatability can still have low accuracy, requiring calibration. The relationship is fundamental to all robot selection decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):**  
> (1 mark) Accuracy definition with formula.  
> (1 mark) Repeatability definition with formula.  
> (1 mark) The inequality and its engineering significance.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Point-to-Point (PTP) control and Continuous Path (CP) control. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | PTP Control | CP Control |
|---|---|---|
| Path Concern | Only start and end positions matter | Entire path is the product |
| Data Density | Few waypoints (sparse) | Many waypoints (dense) |
| Cycle Time | Faster | Slower (path must be tracked) |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Use Case | Pick-and-place, spot welding | Arc welding, painting |

**Examples:**  
- **PTP:** Pick-and-place of biscuits on a conveyor; spot welding of sheet metal.  
- **CP:** Arc welding of a car chassis; spray painting of a car body.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):**  
> (1.5 marks) Tabular differentiation. (1.5 marks) Correct examples.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Robot Selection with Justification

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]**

A company wants to automate a spot-welding operation on a car body. The workpieces are 1.5 m long, 0.8 m wide, and 0.4 m high, located on a horizontal fixture. The fixture is 1.2 m from the robot's base. The cycle time required is 8 seconds, and the operational environment is a standard automotive assembly floor with some oil mist.

**(a)** Recommend a suitable robot configuration for this application. Justify your choice based on the *number of axes, work volume, capacity, and path control*. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the importance of *operating environment* and *repeatability* specifications for this robot. If the robot manufacturer quotes a repeatability of ±0.05 mm, what does this number actually mean? (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Robot Configuration Selection (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Number of Axes:**  
Spot welding requires the robot to position a heavy weld gun at many discrete points across a 3D surface and approach each point at a specific angle. This requires 5–6 DOF. A **6-DOF articulated robot** is the industry standard for spot welding.

**Step 2 — Work Volume:**  
The required reach is approximately:

$$
R = 1.2\,\text{m (horizontal)} + \text{half diagonal of workpiece} = 1.2 + 0.5 \times \sqrt{1.5^2 + 0.8^2} \approx 1.96\,\text{m}
$$

A robot with reach ≥ **2.0 m** is needed. Most industrial 6-axis robots have reaches of 1.5–3.0 m, satisfying this.

**Step 3 — Capacity:**  
A typical spot weld gun weighs 30–80 kg. So the robot's payload must be **at least 100 kg** to handle the gun and any cabling. Industrial spot-welding robots (e.g., FANUC R-2000iC) are typically rated 100–270 kg.

**Step 4 — Path Control:**  
Spot welding is a discrete operation. The robot must move from point to point; the path between spots is irrelevant. So **PTP control** is sufficient and is faster.

**Step 5 — Final Selection:**  
**A 6-DOF articulated robot with reach ≥ 2.0 m, payload ≥ 100 kg, IP54 rating (for oil mist), and PTP control.** Examples: FANUC R-2000iC/210F, ABB IRB 6700, KUKA KR 210 R3100.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):**  
> (1.5 marks) DOF justification. (1.5 marks) Work volume calculation. (1.5 marks) Capacity justification. (1.5 marks) Path control choice. (1 mark) Final model selection.

---

### Part (b) — Environment and Repeatability (7 marks)

**Operating Environment:**  
The automotive assembly floor has **oil mist** (small airborne oil droplets) and possibly weld spatter. The robot must be **sealed to at least IP54** (dust-protected and splash-resistant) to prevent ingress. The wrist and arm joints must use sealed bearings. Critical electronics should be in NEMA-rated enclosures.

**Repeatability Discussion:**  
Spot welding creates physical penetration and metal flow at the weld point. The electrode must contact the metal within tight tolerance to ensure weld quality. If the electrode is misaligned by even 0.5 mm, the weld nugget may not form correctly.

A repeatability of **±0.05 mm** means that when the robot returns to a taught welding position, it will fall within a sphere of radius 0.05 mm, 99.7% of the time. This is more than adequate for spot welding, where the typical tolerance requirement is ±0.1–0.5 mm.

**Statistical meaning:** With k = 3 in $R_p = \pm 3\sigma$:

$$
\sigma = \frac{0.05}{3} \approx 0.0167\,\text{mm}
$$

So the standard deviation of the position cluster is about 17 µm. This is a high-precision industrial standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):**  
> (2 marks) Environment analysis. (2 marks) Repeatability interpretation. (3 marks) Statistical meaning with calculation.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Theoretical Comparison

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified]**

**(a)** A 6-axis articulated robot has the following joint parameters: shoulder link length 0.5 m, upper arm 0.7 m, forearm 0.6 m, and wrist length 0.2 m. Compute the **maximum reach** of the robot. If the wrist can roll by ±180°, what is the **maximum swept volume** approximately? (7 marks)

**(b)** A SCARA robot is rated for a payload of 5 kg, but the application requires carrying a part (2 kg), a gripper (1.5 kg), and a vacuum sensor (0.3 kg). Discuss whether the rated payload is sufficient, and explain the concept of **payload-speed derating**. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Reach and Swept Volume (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Maximum Reach:**

The maximum reach is the sum of the link lengths from the base to the end-effector mounting flange:

$$
R = a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + a_4 = 0.5 + 0.7 + 0.6 + 0.2 = 2.0\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\max} = 2.0\,\text{m}}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Swept Volume (Spherical Approximation):**

For a 6-DOF articulated robot, the swept work volume can be approximated as a sphere of radius equal to the reach. However, the wrist roll (±180°) and other joint limits reduce this. A common engineering approximation is:

$$
V_{\text{swept}} \approx \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \times \text{(coverage factor)}
$$

Using the full sphere approximation as an upper bound:

$$
V_{\text{swept}} \approx \frac{4}{3}\pi (2.0)^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi \times 8 = \frac{32\pi}{3} \approx 33.51\,\text{m}^3
$$

Accounting for typical joint limits (each ±90° to ±180°), a coverage factor of ~0.6 is realistic:

$$
V_{\text{swept}} \approx 0.6 \times 33.51 \approx 20.1\,\text{m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{swept}} \approx 20\,\text{m}^3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):**  
> (3 marks) Reach calculation. (2 marks) Volume formula selection. (2 marks) Numerical evaluation with coverage factor.

---

### Part (b) — Payload Sufficiency and Derating (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Total Payload Calculation:**

$$
P_{\text{total}} = m_{\text{part}} + m_{\text{gripper}} + m_{\text{sensor}} = 2.0 + 1.5 + 0.3 = 3.8\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Comparison with Rated Capacity:**

The SCARA is rated for 5 kg. The actual load is 3.8 kg, which is **76% of the rated capacity**. This is acceptable, but a **20% safety margin** is recommended. The rated 5 kg capacity is sufficient, but operating at near-maximum reduces cycle life and increases heat generation.

**Engineering Recommendation:** Use a robot with **6 kg rated capacity** to maintain 20% margin.

**Step 3 — Payload-Speed Derating Discussion:**

Industrial robots cannot maintain maximum speed at maximum payload. Manufacturers publish a **payload-speed derating curve**. A typical linear approximation is:

$$
v_{\text{actual}} = v_{\max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\max}}\right)
$$

If the SCARA's $v_{\max} = 8\,\text{m/s}$ and $P_{\max} = 5\,\text{kg}$:

$$
v_{\text{actual}} = 8 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{3.8}{5}\right) = 8 \times 0.24 = 1.92\,\text{m/s}
$$

So the robot will run at only **1.92 m/s** with the current payload, not the full 8 m/s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derating is the most-overlooked parameter in robot selection.** Engineers often buy a robot for its top speed, forgetting that payload reduces speed significantly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):**  
> (2 marks) Total payload calculation. (2 marks) Comparison and recommendation. (3 marks) Derating concept with numerical example.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating Accuracy and Repeatability:** Most KTU students lose 1–2 marks by using the two terms interchangeably. Remember: **Repeatability = returning to a known point; Accuracy = reaching a commanded point.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the End-Effector Weight:** Many students compute payload as just the part mass. Always include **gripper + fixture + tooling** in the total payload.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring Derating:** Specifying a robot by its max speed and max payload independently is a classic mistake. Always apply the **payload-speed derating** relationship.
> 
> 4. **Wrong Work Volume Formula:** Students often apply the spherical formula to a Cartesian robot (or vice versa). Match the formula to the **robot configuration**.
> 
> 5. **Confusing PTP and CP:** Many students pick PTP for arc welding (wrong!) or CP for spot welding (suboptimal). Use the **"stop mid-motion" test**.
> 
> 6. **Resolution Bit-Depth Confusion:** The encoder bit-depth `n` gives $2^n$ counts per **full revolution** of the encoder, not of the joint (for geared joints, multiply by the gear ratio in the denominator).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Number of Axes = DOF.** Full spatial mobility requires 6 DOF. Redundant manipulators have 7+ DOF.
- [ ] **Work Volume** depends on configuration: Cartesian (cuboid), Cylindrical (cylinder), Spherical (sphere), Articulated (irregular torus).
- [ ] **Capacity (Payload)** includes the part + end-effector + fixture + tooling.
- [ ] **Speed** is derated by payload. Always check the manufacturer's curve.
- [ ] **Reach** = sum of link lengths for serial linkage. **Stroke** = range of one prismatic joint.
- [ ] **Repeatability** = ±3σ (typically) of returning to a taught point. It is **statistical**, not a fixed error.
- [ ] **Accuracy** = distance from commanded point to mean of achieved points.
- [ ] **Resolution** = smallest commandable step = $\theta_{\max} / (2^n \cdot G)$.
- [ ] **Master Inequality:** Accuracy ≥ Repeatability ≥ Resolution.
- [ ] **Operating Environment** dictates IP rating, ATEX certification, and cleanroom class.
- [ ] **PTP control** = discrete waypoints, path irrelevant. **CP control** = dense waypoints, path is the product.
- [ ] **PTP Test:** Can the robot stop mid-motion without ruining the task? If yes → PTP.
- [ ] **Selection Workflow:** Define task → Choose control type → Choose DOF → Match work volume → Check payload/speed → Verify environment → Validate repeatability.
- [ ] **Formulas to memorize (KTU favorites):** $V_{\text{cylindrical}} = \pi R^2 H$, $V_{\text{sphere}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3$, $R_p = \pm 3\sigma$, $\Delta\theta = \theta_{\max}/(2^n G)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors and Actuators - Sensor Classification: Touch, Force, Proximity, Vision Sensors. Internal Sensors: Position Sensors, Velocity Sensors, Acceleration Sensors, Force Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET831 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors and Actuators - Sensor Classification: Touch, Force, Proximity, Vision Sensors. Internal Sensors: Position Sensors, Velocity Sensors, Acceleration Sensors, Force Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensors** are electro-mechanical or electro-optical devices that detect physical, chemical, or environmental changes in the robot's workspace or its own internal state, and convert these into measurable electrical signals (analog or digital) for the robot controller to interpret and act upon. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, sensors are broadly bifurcated into:
>
> 1. **External (Exteroceptive) Sensors** — perceive the external environment (touch, force, proximity, vision).
> 2. **Internal (Proprioceptive) Sensors** — monitor the robot's own internal state (position, velocity, acceleration, internal force/torque).

**Actuators**, in contrast, are the *output* devices that convert electrical energy (commands from the controller) into mechanical motion, force, or torque — i.e., they *act* on the environment based on sensor feedback.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

Imagine a human being. Your **skin** is a *touch sensor*, your **eyes** are a *vision sensor*, and your **inner ear + muscles** contain *position and acceleration sensors* (proprioception). When you pick up a fragile glass:

- Your **skin (touch sensor)** detects contact.
- Your **muscle spindles (position sensor)** tell your brain where your arm is.
- Your **vestibular system (acceleration sensor)** senses motion.
- Your **muscles (actuators)** apply just the right *force* — not too much, not too little.

A robot mimics this exact hierarchy. Without sensors, a robot is **"blind and numb"** — it cannot know *where* it is, *what* it is touching, or *how hard* it is pushing.

### 1.3 Hierarchical Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Classification Framework:**

| Class | Type | Examples | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **External** | Touch | Resistive, Capacitive, Piezoelectric skin | Detect contact / collision |
| **External** | Force | Strain gauge fingertips, tactile arrays | Measure contact force |
| **External** | Proximity | Inductive, Capacitive, Optical, Ultrasonic | Detect nearby objects without contact |
| **External** | Vision | CCD/CMOS cameras, depth sensors | Object recognition, navigation |
| **Internal** | Position | Encoders, Potentiometers, LVDT, Resolvers | Joint/link angle feedback |
| **Internal** | Velocity | DC Tachogenerator, Encoder-derivative | Speed of joints |
| **Internal** | Acceleration | Piezoelectric, MEMS accelerometers | Vibration, dynamic control |
| **Internal** | Force | Joint torque sensors, wrist F/T sensors | Force feedback in joints |

### 1.4 Fundamental Sensor Performance Metrics

Every KTU question on sensors implicitly tests these performance terms — **memorize them**:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Accuracy** — Closeness of measured value to true value.
> - **Precision / Repeatability** — Ability to reproduce same reading under identical conditions.
> - **Resolution** — Smallest detectable change in input.
> - **Sensitivity** — Ratio of output signal to input change (e.g., **V/mm**).
> - **Linearity** — Output proportional to input (deviation = *non-linearity error*).
> - **Range / Span** — Min to max measurable input.
> - **Bandwidth / Dynamic Response** — Frequency range over which sensor works accurately.
> - **Hysteresis** — Output difference for increasing vs. decreasing input at the same point.

### 1.5 Signal Conditioning

Raw sensor outputs are rarely directly usable. They pass through:

1. **Amplification** (signal level matching)
2. **Filtering** (noise removal — low-pass, high-pass)
3. **Linearization** (correcting non-linear curves)
4. **A/D Conversion** (analog → digital for microcontroller)
5. **Calibration** (offset/gain correction)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 A. Touch Sensors (Tactile Sensors)

Touch sensors detect **physical contact** and its location. Two main categories:

#### 2.1.1 Resistive (Conductive) Touch Sensor
- Working principle: Contact between two conductive layers changes **electrical resistance**.
- When pressure is applied, contact area ↑ → resistance ↓.
- Used in **robot fingertips**, simple bump sensors.

#### 2.1.2 Capacitive Touch Sensor
- Working principle: Contact changes the **capacitance** $C$ of a parallel-plate arrangement.
- $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$
- As $d$ ↓ (finger approaches) or $A$ ↑ (finger contact spreads), $C$ changes.
- Very sensitive; used in modern touchscreens and artificial skin.

#### 2.1.3 Piezoelectric Touch Sensor
- Working principle: Mechanical stress generates a **charge** across the crystal.
- $Q = d \cdot F$, where $d$ = piezoelectric charge constant (C/N), $F$ = force (N).
- Detects **dynamic touch** (vibration, slip) — not static pressure (charge leaks away).

### 2.2 B. Force Sensors (External)

#### 2.2.1 Strain Gauge (Most Important — KTU Hot Topic)
- A strain gauge is a thin resistive foil whose resistance changes with strain.
- $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ (basic resistance of a conductor)
- Under axial strain $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$:
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = GF \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$
- **Gauge Factor (GF)** = sensitivity, typically **2.0 to 2.2** for constantan.
- $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$
- Arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** for temperature compensation and amplification.

#### 2.2.2 Tactile Sensor Arrays
- A 2D grid (e.g., 16×16) of taxels gives **pressure distribution** over a contact patch — needed for grasping fragile/irregular objects.

### 2.3 C. Proximity Sensors

Detect the **presence** of an object *without physical contact*. Range typically **few mm to several meters**.

| Type | Detects | Range | Works on |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inductive** | Metallic objects only | 1–50 mm | Eddy currents in metal |
| **Capacitive** | Metals, plastics, liquids | 1–40 mm | Change in capacitance |
| **Optical (IR)** | Any opaque object | 10 mm–50 m | Light beam break/reflection |
| **Ultrasonic** | Any material | 50 mm–10 m | Sound wave echo (time-of-flight) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Inductive Proximity Sensor** uses a high-frequency electromagnetic field. When a metal target enters the field, **eddy currents** are induced, which dampen the oscillator. The damping is detected electronically and triggers the output.
>
> **Capacitive Proximity Sensor** detects any material with a dielectric constant different from air. Both target and sensor face act as plates of a capacitor. The change in capacitance at the rated distance triggers the output.

### 2.4 D. Vision Sensors

- A **camera** (CCD or CMOS) captures 2D images.
- Image processing extracts features: edges, blobs, colors.
- **Stereo vision** uses two cameras for depth (disparity).
- Structured light and Time-of-Flight (ToF) cameras give 3D directly.
- Resolution, frame rate, focal length are key specs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Vision Equation — Pinhole Camera Model:**
> $$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v}$$
> where $f$ = focal length, $u$ = object distance, $v$ = image distance. For a robot, knowing $f$ and pixel size lets you compute real-world dimensions of an object.

### 2.5 E. Internal Position Sensors (KTU Favourite)

#### 2.5.1 Potentiometer
- A variable resistor with a wiper that moves with the joint.
- Output voltage $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$
- Simple, cheap, but suffers from **wear, noise, and limited resolution**.

#### 2.5.2 Optical Encoder
- A disk with slots rotates with the joint. An LED + photodetector counts pulses.
- **Incremental encoder**: Outputs pulses; **needs homing on power-up**.
  - Resolution: $N = \dfrac{360°}{ppr}$ per quarter cycle, where ppr = pulses per revolution.
- **Absolute encoder**: Outputs a unique binary Gray code for every angle; **no homing needed**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution of an Encoder:** If the disk has $N$ slots, one full turn gives $N$ pulses. Minimum detectable angle:
> $$\theta_{min} = \frac{360°}{N} = \frac{2\pi}{N} \text{ rad}$$

#### 2.5.3 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)
- A transformer with one primary and two secondaries sharing a movable core.
- As the core moves, the coupling changes → differential voltage $V_{out} = V_{s2} - V_{s1}$ is linear with displacement.
- $V_{out} = k \cdot x$ (for $|x|$ within linear range)
- **Excellent linearity, infinite resolution, no contact** — gold standard for linear position.

#### 2.5.4 Resolver
- A rotating transformer (rotor + stator) giving analog sine/cosine outputs.
- $V_{sin} = V_0 \sin(\omega t) \sin(\theta)$
- $V_{cos} = V_0 \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta)$
- $\theta$ = rotor angle. Demodulated in resolver-to-digital converter (RDC).
- **Rugged, used in harsh environments and military robots**.

### 2.6 F. Velocity Sensors

#### 2.6.1 DC Tachogenerator
- A small DC generator coupled to the joint.
- Output EMF $E = K \cdot \omega$, where $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $K$ = generator constant.
- Output polarity reverses with rotation direction.

#### 2.6.2 Encoder-based Velocity (Computed)
- Velocity = differentiation of position (digital or analog).
- $\omega = \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t}$
- Susceptible to noise (numerical differentiation amplifies high-frequency noise) — usually filtered.

### 2.7 G. Acceleration Sensors (Accelerometers)

#### 2.7.1 Working Principle — Mass-Spring System
- A **proof mass** $m$ is suspended by a spring of stiffness $k$ inside the sensor housing.
- When the housing accelerates with $a$, the mass experiences an inertial force $F = ma$.
- The mass deflects by $x = \dfrac{ma}{k}$.
- This deflection is measured (capacitively, piezoresistively, or piezoelectrically).

#### 2.7.2 Natural Frequency
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$
- For accurate measurement, operating frequency should be $\ll \omega_n$.

#### 2.7.3 Piezoelectric Accelerometer
- Uses a piezoelectric crystal (e.g., quartz) as the spring.
- Output charge: $Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a$
- High bandwidth, ideal for vibration measurement.

#### 2.7.4 MEMS Accelerometer
- Micromachined silicon structure. Capacitive or piezoresistive readout.
- Used in smartphones, drones, IMUs.

### 2.8 H. Internal Force/Torque Sensors

- **Joint torque sensor** mounted on the motor shaft — measures torque from motor current or strain on the shaft.
- **Wrist Force/Torque Sensor (F/T Sensor)** — mounted between the robot's wrist and end-effector. Uses strain gauges in a cross or octagonal pattern to resolve 6 components: $F_x, F_y, F_z, \tau_x, \tau_y, \tau_z$.
- Essential for **force-controlled assembly, deburring, polishing**.

### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet (Comprehensive)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations — Memorize for Part A & Part B:**

| # | Quantity | Equation | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resistance of conductor | $R = \rho L / A$ | $\rho$ = resistivity ($\Omega \cdot m$) |
| 2 | Strain gauge | $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | $GF$ ≈ **2.0** (dimensionless) |
| 3 | Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Dimensionless (m/m) |
| 4 | Stress (Hooke) | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa) |
| 5 | Capacitance | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | Farads |
| 6 | Piezo charge | $Q = d \cdot F$ | $d$ = charge constant (C/N) |
| 7 | Encoder resolution | $\theta_{min} = 360° / N$ | $N$ = pulses per revolution |
| 8 | Tachogenerator | $E = K \cdot \omega$ | $\omega$ = rad/s |
| 9 | Accelerometer deflection | $x = m a / k$ | m, m/s², N/m |
| 10 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s |
| 11 | Newton's 2nd law | $F = ma$ | N, kg, m/s² |
| 12 | Potentiometer | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \theta/\theta_{max}$ | Volts |
| 13 | LVDT output | $V_{out} = k \cdot x$ | Volts, metres |
| 14 | Resolver signals | $V_{sin} = V_0 \sin(\omega t)\sin\theta$ ; $V_{cos} = V_0 \sin(\omega t)\cos\theta$ | Volts |
| 15 | Pinhole camera | $1/f = 1/u + 1/v$ | metres |
| 16 | Ultrasonic distance | $d = (v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo})/2$ | m (typically $v_{sound}$ ≈ **343 m/s**) |
| 17 | Ultrasonic time-of-flight | $t = 2d / v_{sound}$ | seconds |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL UNIT REMINDERS:**
> - $\omega$ in **rad/s** for equations involving $\sin/\cos$.
> - Convert **rpm to rad/s**: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N_{rpm}}{60}$.
> - **1 rev = 360° = $2\pi$ rad**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Strain Gauge Resistance Change

**Starting point — basic resistance formula:**

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

**Step 1 — Apply small perturbations** (length increases, area decreases due to Poisson effect):

$$R + \Delta R = \frac{(\rho + \Delta\rho)(L + \Delta L)}{A - \Delta A}$$

**Step 2 — Neglect higher-order small terms** (Poisson ratio $\nu$ for circular wire: $\Delta A / A = -2\nu \cdot \Delta L / L$):

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} \approx \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} + \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute area change via Poisson ratio:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Define Gauge Factor (combines both geometric and piezoresistive effects):**

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 5 — Final form used in KTU problems:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = GF \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L}}$$

> For constantan: $GF \approx 2.0$ → roughly half from geometry $(1 + 2\nu)$, half from piezoresistivity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Accelerometer Working (Mass-Spring-Damper)

**Step 1 — Inertial frame coordinates:** The housing accelerates at $a(t)$. The proof mass $m$ has displacement $y_m$ in the inertial frame.

**Step 2 — Forces on mass in housing frame:** Spring force $-k \cdot x$ and damper force $-c \cdot \dot x$ where $x = y_m - y_{housing}$ = relative displacement.

**Step 3 — Equation of motion:**

$$m \ddot x + c \dot x + k x = -m \cdot a_{housing}(t)$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state (low-frequency) regime** where $\omega \ll \omega_n$ and damping negligible:

$$k x \approx -m a \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = -\frac{m a}{k}$$

**Step 5 — Natural (resonant) frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Step 6 — Sensitivity of accelerometer:**

$$S = \frac{x}{a} = \frac{m}{k} = \frac{1}{\omega_n^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** A *stiffer* spring (larger $k$) → smaller deflection, *lower* sensitivity. A *lighter* mass (smaller $m$) → smaller deflection, *lower* sensitivity. Therefore, high sensitivity requires *low* $k$ and *high* $m$ — but that *lowers* the natural frequency (bandwidth). This is the fundamental **sensitivity–bandwidth trade-off** in accelerometers.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Ultrasonic Distance Sensor — Time of Flight

**Step 1 — Sensor transmits an ultrasonic pulse** at time $t_0$ in air. Sound speed in air at 20°C is **$v_s \approx 343$ m/s**.

**Step 2 — Pulse travels to target** (distance $d$ away) → time taken $= d / v_s$.

**Step 3 — Pulse reflects from target**, returns to sensor → another $d / v_s$.

**Step 4 — Total echo time:**

$$t_{echo} = \frac{2d}{v_s}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for distance:**

$$d = \frac{v_s \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example:** If $t_{echo} = 6$ ms, then
$$d = \frac{343 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.058}{2} = 1.029 \text{ m}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Encoder Angular Resolution

**Step 1 — Encoder disk has $N$ equally spaced slots** around its circumference.

**Step 2 — One full rotation** ($360°$ or $2\pi$ rad) produces $N$ pulses.

**Step 3 — Minimum angle per pulse (resolution):**

$$\theta_{min} = \frac{360°}{N} = \frac{2\pi}{N} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Quadrature decoding** (using two photodetectors 90° out of phase) multiplies resolution by 4:

$$\theta_{min,quad} = \frac{360°}{4N}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example:** $N = 1000$ slots → basic resolution = 0.36°; quadrature resolution = **0.09°**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Wheatstone Bridge Output for Strain Gauge

A single strain gauge is temperature-sensitive. KTU requires a **Wheatstone bridge** arrangement.

**Step 1 — Bridge equation:** For a bridge with arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ and excitation $V_{ex}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Balanced condition:** $V_{out} = 0$ when $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$.

**Step 3 — Quarter-bridge with one active gauge** (only $R_1$ changes by $\Delta R$):

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Full-bridge (4 active gauges, all in same strain orientation):**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

> Hence a full bridge gives **4× the sensitivity** of a quarter bridge — a favourite numerical question in KTU.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Selection

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix in viva and design questions:**

| Requirement | Best Sensor | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Joint angle (rotary) | Optical encoder (incremental) | High resolution, digital output |
| Joint angle with absolute position on power-up | Absolute encoder / Resolver | No homing needed |
| Linear displacement, high accuracy | LVDT | Excellent linearity, no contact |
| Detect metallic object proximity | Inductive proximity | Immune to non-metals, robust |
| Detect plastic/wood/liquid | Capacitive proximity | Dielectric-sensitive |
| Distance to soft/irregular object | Ultrasonic | Works on any material |
| Static contact force on fingertip | Capacitive tactile array | Holds static pressure |
| Dynamic vibration/impact | Piezoelectric sensor | High bandwidth, no drift |
| Slow tilt / orientation | MEMS accelerometer (DC mode) | Measures static $g$ |
| Object recognition & navigation | Vision (CCD/CMOS camera) | Rich information |
| Joint torque feedback | Strain-gauge torque sensor on shaft | Direct, accurate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sensor Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ROBOT SENSORS] --> B[EXTERNAL EXTEROCEPTIVE]
    A --> C[INTERNAL PROPRIOCEPTIVE]
    B --> B1[Touch]
    B --> B2[Force]
    B --> B3[Proximity]
    B --> B4[Vision]
    C --> C1[Position]
    C --> C2[Velocity]
    C --> C3[Acceleration]
    C --> C4[Internal Force Torque]
    B1 --> B1a[Resistive]
    B1 --> B1b[Capacitive]
    B1 --> B1c[Piezoelectric]
    B2 --> B2a[Strain Gauge]
    B2 --> B2b[Tactile Array]
    B3 --> B3a[Inductive]
    B3 --> B3b[Capacitive]
    B3 --> B3c[Optical IR]
    B3 --> B3d[Ultrasonic]
    B4 --> B4a[CCD Camera]
    B4 --> B4b[CMOS Camera]
    B4 --> B4c[Stereo Vision]
    B4 --> B4d[Depth ToF]
    C1 --> C1a[Potentiometer]
    C1 --> C1b[Incremental Encoder]
    C1 --> C1c[Absolute Encoder]
    C1 --> C1d[LVDT]
    C1 --> C1e[Resolver]
    C2 --> C2a[DC Tachogenerator]
    C2 --> C2b[Encoder Differentiation]
    C3 --> C3a[Piezoelectric Accelerometer]
    C3 --> C3b[MEMS Accelerometer]
    C3 --> C3c[Strain Gauge Accelerometer]
    C4 --> C4a[Joint Torque Sensor]
    C4 --> C4b[Wrist F T Sensor]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Sensor Signal Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Physical Quantity] --> S2[Sensor Element]
    S2 --> S3[Signal Conditioning]
    S3 --> S4[Amplifier]
    S4 --> S5[Filter]
    S5 --> S6[ADC]
    S6 --> S7[Microcontroller]
    S7 --> S8[Robot Controller]
    S8 --> S9[Actuator Command]
    S9 --> S10[Robot Motion]
    S10 -. feedback .-> S1
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Strain Gauge Force Sensor (Wheatstone Bridge)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F[Applied Force F] --> D[Deflection of Cantilever Beam]
    D --> ST[Strain epsilon in Beam]
    ST --> SG[Strain Gauge Resistance Change]
    SG --> WB[Wheatstone Bridge]
    WB --> AMP[Differential Amplifier]
    AMP --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> MC[Microcontroller]
    MC --> DSP[Compute F from Voltage]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TR[Trigger Pulse] --> TX[Transmitter 40 kHz Burst]
    TX --> AIR[Travel Through Air]
    AIR --> TG[Target Object]
    TG --> REF[Reflected Echo]
    REF --> RX[Receiver]
    RX --> AMP[Amplify Echo]
    AMP --> DET[Detect Envelope]
    DET --> TIM[Measure Time of Flight t]
    TIM --> CAL[Compute d = v x t over 2]
    CAL --> OUT[Distance Output]
```

### 4.5 Functional Architecture — Optical Incremental Encoder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SH[Rotating Shaft] --> DK[Slotted Disk]
    DK --> LED1[LED Light Source]
    LED1 --> PD1[Photodetector A]
    LED1 --> PD2[Photodetector B 90 degrees shifted]
    PD1 --> SQ1[Squarer Schmitt Trigger A]
    PD2 --> SQ2[Squarer Schmitt Trigger B]
    SQ1 --> CTR[Up Down Counter]
    SQ2 --> CTR
    CTR --> ANG[Joint Angle Output]
```

### 4.6 Functional Architecture — Piezoelectric Accelerometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ACC[Base Acceleration a] --> HS[Housing Moves]
    HS --> PM[Proof Mass m Inertial]
    PM --> SP[Spring Crystal]
    SP --> DEF[Deflection x = m a over k]
    DEF --> PZ[Piezoelectric Charge Q = d F]
    PZ --> CHG[Charge Amplifier]
    CHG --> V[V proportional to a]
    V --> OUT[Acceleration Output]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definitions)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors in robotics. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Proprioceptive (Internal) | Exteroceptive (External) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Sense the robot's *own* state | Sense the *external environment* |
| Measured quantity | Joint angle, joint velocity, motor current | Object presence, shape, distance, force |
| Feedback purpose | Control the robot's motion | Interact with surroundings |
| Example 1 | Optical encoder at joint | Vision (CCD) camera |
| Example 2 | DC tachogenerator | Ultrasonic proximity sensor |

*Self-explanatory, 2-line answer acceptable in KTU valuation.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Gauge Factor of a strain gauge. What is its typical value for a metallic foil gauge?**

**Model Answer:**

Gauge Factor (GF) is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in resistance to the fractional change in length (i.e., strain):

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L}$$

Typical value for a **constantan metallic foil gauge: GF ≈ 2.0**. For semiconductor (piezoresistive) gauges, GF can be 50–200.

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Strain Gauge & Force Sensor in Robotics*

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the governing equation of a strain gauge, $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$, starting from $R = \rho L / A$. Clearly state the meaning of Gauge Factor. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A strain gauge with $GF = 2.1$ and $R = 120 \, \Omega$ is bonded to a steel beam ($E = 200$ GPa). When a force is applied, the gauge resistance changes by $\Delta R = 0.24 \, \Omega$. Calculate:
  (i) The strain $\varepsilon$ in the beam.
  (ii) The stress $\sigma$ in the beam.
  (iii) The force $F$ if the cross-sectional area of the beam is $A = 25 \text{ mm}^2$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation** (full version reproduced from §3.1 above):
Start with $R = \rho L/A$. Under small strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$, with area change $\Delta A/A = -2\nu \varepsilon$ and piezoresistive change $\Delta \rho/\rho$:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Gauge Factor** $GF = (1 + 2\nu) + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ — combines geometric (Poisson) and piezoresistive effects. For constantan, $GF \approx 2.0$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Strain:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta R / R}{GF} = \frac{0.24 / 120}{2.1} = \frac{0.002}{2.1} = 9.524 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(ii) Stress (Hooke's Law):**
$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = (200 \times 10^{9}) \times (9.524 \times 10^{-4}) = 1.905 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx 190.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Force from stress:**
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \sigma \cdot A = (1.905 \times 10^{8}) \times (25 \times 10^{-6}) = 4761.9 \text{ N} \approx 4.76 \text{ kN}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Sensors & Accelerometer*

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a piezoelectric accelerometer with a neat block diagram. Derive the relationship $x = m a / k$ and natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An accelerometer has a proof mass $m = 0.005$ kg and spring stiffness $k = 2000$ N/m. It is mounted on a robot link vibrating at 25 Hz.
  (i) Find the natural frequency $\omega_n$ and check if it is suitable for measuring this vibration.
  (ii) Find the steady-state deflection of the mass when the vibration peak acceleration is $a = 9.81$ m/s².
  (iii) If the same accelerometer is to measure a 200 Hz vibration, is it suitable? Justify. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Working principle: A proof mass $m$ is suspended inside the housing by a spring of stiffness $k$. When the housing accelerates at $a(t)$, the inertial force $F = ma$ deflects the mass. Equation of motion:

$$m\ddot x + c\dot x + kx = -m a(t)$$

In the quasi-static limit ($\omega \ll \omega_n$, damping negligible): $kx = ma$, so $x = ma/k$.

Piezoelectric element senses this deflection and produces a charge $Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot ma$, which is converted to a voltage by a charge amplifier → output voltage ∝ acceleration.

Natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ is the upper usable limit.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Natural frequency:**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2000}{0.005}} = \sqrt{400000} = 632.46 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{632.46}{2\pi} = 100.66 \text{ Hz}$$

Operating frequency 25 Hz $\ll f_n$ = 100.66 Hz. Since 25 < 100.66, the operating frequency is well within the flat (usable) region of the response. **Accelerometer is suitable** ✓

**(ii) Deflection at peak acceleration:**
$$x = \frac{ma}{k} = \frac{0.005 \times 9.81}{2000} = 2.4525 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 24.5 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**(iii) For 200 Hz vibration:** $f_{input} = 200$ Hz $> f_n = 100.66$ Hz. The accelerometer is operating *above* resonance, where the response is **highly attenuated and non-linear**. **Not suitable** — it will give a falsely low reading (or even output zero) and may resonate destructively.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in sensor questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units**: Strain is dimensionless; stress in Pa (or MPa); force in N.
> 2. **Sign errors in Wheatstone bridge** derivations — carefully track which arm changes.
> 3. **Mixing up rad/s and Hz** in accelerometer problems: $\omega_n = 2\pi f_n$.
> 4. **Stating "strain gauge measures force"** — it measures *strain*; force is *computed* using Hooke's law and the geometry of the host structure.
> 5. **Conflating "absolute" and "incremental" encoders** — absolute gives position on power-up; incremental needs homing.
> 6. **Saying "ultrasonic = speed of light"** — wrong! Sound in air ≈ **343 m/s**; do not use $3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
> 7. **Skipping the constant-of-proportionality derivation** in piezo problems — must show $Q = dF$, not just state it.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Sensors):**

- [x] **Sensors are the robot's "senses"**; classified as *exteroceptive* (touch, force, proximity, vision) and *proprioceptive* (position, velocity, acceleration, internal force).
- [x] **Strain gauge equation** — must-know: $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$, with **GF ≈ 2.0** for metallic gauges.
- [x] **Piezoelectric sensors** — generate charge on deformation: $Q = dF$. **Cannot measure static quantities** (charge leaks).
- [x] **Wheatstone bridge** is *always* used with strain gauges for temperature compensation; full bridge = 4× output of quarter bridge.
- [x] **Inductive proximity** → only metals; **Capacitive** → metals + non-metals (dielectric sensing); **Ultrasonic** → all materials; **Optical** → opaque objects.
- [x] **Ultrasonic distance** — $d = v_s t_{echo} / 2$, with $v_s \approx 343$ m/s in air.
- [x] **Incremental encoder** needs homing; **absolute encoder** does not. Resolution = $360°/N$, with quadrature = $360°/(4N)$.
- [x] **LVDT** → linear position (no contact, infinite resolution, excellent linearity).
- [x] **Resolver** → rugged rotary position (sine/cosine outputs).
- [x] **Tachogenerator** → $E = K\omega$ (rotary velocity, no electronics needed).
- [x] **Accelerometer** → mass-spring-damper: $x = ma/k$, $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$. Operating frequency must be $\ll \omega_n$ for accurate measurement.
- [x] **MEMS accelerometers** = tiny silicon chip with capacitive/piezoresistive readout (used in phones, drones, robot IMUs).
- [x] **Wrist F/T sensor** → 6-component force/torque measurement (used in force-controlled assembly and polishing).
- [x] **Performance metrics** — accuracy, precision, resolution, sensitivity, linearity, bandwidth, hysteresis.
- [x] **Pinhole camera model** — $1/f = 1/u + 1/v$ — basis of vision-based distance/measurement in robotics.
- [x] **Touch sensors** → resistive (pressure → area → R↓), capacitive (pressure → C↑), piezoelectric (dynamic only).
- [x] **DC motor current ∝ torque** — basis of current-based torque estimation in robot joints.
- [x] **Always include units** in the final numerical answer; show full derivation for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External Sensors: Contact Type, Non-contact Type. Actuators for Robots - Classification: Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET831 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** External Sensors: Contact Type, Non-contact Type. Actuators for Robots - Classification: Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 External Sensors in Robotics

### Formal Definition
**External sensors** in robotics are measurement devices mounted on the manipulator (end-effector) or within the robot's working environment that gather information about the **interaction between the robot and its surroundings**. Unlike internal (proprioceptive) sensors that report the robot's own state (joint angles, motor speeds), external (exteroceptive) sensors perceive external stimuli such as **proximity, contact, force, vision, and range**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** External sensors are categorized primarily by their **physical contact mechanism** with the target:
> - **Contact Type** — Require physical touch with the object/environment.
> - **Non-Contact Type** — Sense the object without physical touch, using fields, waves, or optical means.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Robot's Five Senses"
Imagine a blind person navigating a room. They use a **white cane** (contact sensor — feels the obstacle) and listen to **echoes** (non-contact — like ultrasonic sensing). A robot does the same:

- The **cane** ≈ **Contact Sensor** (tactile, limit switch, force sensor)
- The **echo** ≈ **Non-Contact Sensor** (ultrasonic, infrared, vision)
- The **eyes** ≈ **Vision/Proximity sensors**
- The **fingertip pressure** ≈ **Force/Torque sensor**

This is exactly how humans combine multiple sensory channels — a robot uses sensor **fusion** to achieve robust perception.

## 1.2 Actuators for Robots

### Formal Definition
An **actuator** is an energy-conversion device that transforms input energy (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, thermal) into **mechanical motion** (linear or rotary) to drive the robot's joints and links. Actuators are the "muscles" of a robot, providing the **torque/force** necessary to move the manipulator, hold loads, and execute tasks.

### The Three Principal Classifications (KTU Mandatory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Actuators (Must-Memorize for KTU):**
> 1. **Electric Actuators** — Use electrical energy → mechanical motion (DC motors, stepper motors, servo motors, solenoids).
> 2. **Hydraulic Actuators** — Use incompressible fluid (oil) under high pressure → mechanical motion.
> 3. **Pneumatic Actuators** — Use compressible gas (air) under moderate pressure → mechanical motion.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Three Ways to Drive a Car"
- **Electric Actuator** = An **electric car** — quiet, precise, easy to control (good for clean rooms, precision assembly).
- **Hydraulic Actuator** = A **heavy bulldozer** — extremely strong, slow but powerful (good for heavy payloads, mining).
- **Pneumatic Actuator** = A **pneumatic drill** — fast, lightweight, but less precise (good for pick-and-place, packaging).

## 1.3 Performance Metrics for Sensors (KTU Standard Terminology)

| Metric | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensitivity** | Ratio of output change to input change | V/unit |
| **Range** | Min–max measurable input | m, N, °C |
| **Resolution** | Smallest detectable increment | unit |
| **Accuracy** | Closeness to true value | % |
| **Repeatability** | Consistency of repeated measurements | % |
| **Bandwidth** | Frequency range of operation | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Static vs Dynamic Characteristics:** A KTU favourite question — **Static** characteristics describe steady-state behavior (accuracy, linearity, drift). **Dynamic** characteristics describe time-response (rise time, settling time, bandwidth).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Contact-Type External Sensors

### 2.1.1 Limit Switches (Microswitches)
The simplest and most robust contact sensor. A mechanical lever/button physically contacts an object; the contact triggers an electrical SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw) switching action.

- **Operating Principle:** Mechanical deflection → snap-action contacts open/close.
- **Output:** Binary (0 or 1) digital signal.
- **Application:** End-of-travel limits, gripper closure detection, safety interlocks.

### 2.1.2 Tactile Sensors
Matrix of pressure-sensitive elements (taxels) distributed over a surface. They mimic human skin.

- **Types:**
  - **Resistive (FSR — Force Sensing Resistor):** Resistance decreases as force increases.
  - **Capacitive:** Capacitance changes with applied pressure.
  - **Piezoelectric:** Voltage generated upon mechanical stress.
  - **Optical:** Light intensity changes with deformation.

### 2.1.3 Force / Torque Sensors
Mounted at the **wrist** (between the end-effector and the last link) to measure interaction forces during assembly, polishing, deburring.

- **Working principle:** Strain gauges bonded to a deformable element (flexure).
- **Wrench measurement:** 3 forces ($F_x, F_y, F_z$) + 3 torques ($M_x, M_y, M_z$) → 6-axis force-torque sensor.

### 2.1.4 Touch / Slip Sensors
Detect **incipient slip** of a gripped object by sensing micro-vibrations on the finger surface. Critical for **dexterous grasping**.

## 2.2 Non-Contact-Type External Sensors

### 2.2.1 Proximity Sensors
Detect presence without physical contact.

- **Inductive:** Detects metallic objects via eddy-current loss in an LC oscillator.
- **Capacitive:** Detects any material by change in capacitance.
- **Magnetic (Hall effect):** Detects magnetic fields.
- **Range:** typically **1–15 mm**.

### 2.2.2 Range / Distance Sensors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield — Three Non-Contact Ranging Principles:**

**(a) Triangulation**
A laser spot is projected; a position-sensitive detector (PSD) measures the reflected angle. Distance $d$ is computed geometrically:

$$d = \frac{b \cdot f}{x}$$

where $b$ = baseline, $f$ = focal length, $x$ = spot position on PSD.

**(b) Time-of-Flight (ToF)**
Distance computed from time taken for an emitted wave to return:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

where $c$ = speed of wave ($3 \times 10^8$ m/s for light, $\approx 343$ m/s for sound).

**(c) Phase-Shift Detection**
Measures the phase difference $\phi$ between emitted and received modulated wave:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4\pi f_{mod}}$$

### 2.2.3 Vision Systems (Machine Vision)
A **CCD/CMOS camera** captures a 2D (or reconstructed 3D) image; image-processing algorithms extract features (edges, blobs, corners).

- **Components:** Lighting, lens, image sensor, frame grabber, processing unit.
- **Tasks:** Object recognition, pose estimation, visual servoing.

### 2.2.4 Other Non-Contact Sensors
- **Infrared (IR) sensors:** Detect heat signatures / proximity.
- **Ultrasonic sensors:** Sound-based ranging (ToF principle), robust to dust/optical interference.
- **Laser scanners (LIDAR):** 2D/3D mapping via rotating laser beam.

## 2.3 Actuator Theory — Detailed Classification

### 2.3.1 Electric Actuators

**(a) DC Servo Motors**
Provide continuous rotation; torque proportional to armature current.

$$T_m = K_t \cdot I_a$$

$$V_a = I_a R_a + K_b \omega$$

where $K_t$ = torque constant, $K_b$ = back-emf constant, $I_a$ = armature current, $\omega$ = angular speed.

**(b) Stepper Motors**
Rotate in discrete **steps** (typically 1.8° per step = 200 steps/rev). Open-loop position control.

$$\theta = N \cdot \alpha_{step}$$

where $N$ = number of pulses, $\alpha_{step}$ = step angle.

**(c) AC Servo / Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors**
Used in modern industrial robots; high torque-to-inertia ratio, low maintenance.

**(d) Solenoids**
Linear motion via electromagnetic force on a plunger; used in valves and simple grippers.

### 2.3.2 Hydraulic Actuators

- **Working fluid:** Mineral oil (incompressible).
- **Operating pressure:** 50–200 bar (very high).
- **Power density:** Extremely high.

**Force generated by linear hydraulic cylinder:**

$$F = P \cdot A = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Flow rate–velocity relation:**

$$Q = A \cdot v$$

**Hydraulic power:**

$$P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v$$

**Rotary hydraulic motor torque:**

$$T = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi}$$

where $D_m$ = motor displacement (m³/rad).

### 2.3.3 Pneumatic Actuators

- **Working fluid:** Compressed air (compressible).
- **Operating pressure:** 4–8 bar (low compared to hydraulic).
- **Power density:** Lower, but cheap and clean.

**Force generated:**

$$F = P \cdot A - F_{friction} - F_{back}$$

**Air consumption (for a cylinder stroke $L$):**

$$V_{air} = A \cdot L \cdot \frac{P_{supply}}{P_{atm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Compressibility is the key difference:** Hydraulic systems are **stiff and precise** (incompressible oil). Pneumatic systems are **compliant and cushioning** (compressed air absorbs shock — excellent for delicate pick-and-place).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DC Motor Torque | $T_m = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Torque ∝ armature current |
| 2 | DC Motor Back-EMF | $V_a = I_a R_a + K_b \omega$ | KVL on armature circuit |
| 3 | Stepper Angle | $\theta = N \cdot \alpha_{step}$ | Discrete rotation |
| 4 | Hydraulic Force | $F = P \cdot A$ | $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ |
| 5 | Hydraulic Power | $P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v$ | Power balance |
| 6 | Flow–Velocity | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Continuity equation |
| 7 | Pneumatic Force | $F = P_{gauge} \cdot A$ | Net effective force |
| 8 | ToF Distance | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Ultrasonic / laser ToF |
| 9 | Triangulation | $d = \frac{b \cdot f}{x}$ | Optical triangulation |
| 10 | Phase-Shift Range | $d = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4\pi f_{mod}}$ | Modulated wave ranging |

> **Engineering Utility:** Electric actuators dominate **precision assembly and clean-room robotics** (SCARA, 6-axis articulated). Hydraulic actuators dominate **heavy-payload and outdoor** applications (mining, construction, military). Pneumatic actuators dominate **high-speed pick-and-place** and **food/pharmaceutical** industries where cleanliness, low cost, and simplicity are paramount.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DC Servo Motor Steady-State Speed Equation

We start from the **armature circuit equation** (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law):

$$V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$$

**Step 1 — Assume steady-state operation.**
In steady state, $I_a$ is constant, so $\dfrac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$ and the inductive term vanishes:

$$V_a = I_a R_a + E_b$$

**Step 2 — Express back-EMF in terms of angular velocity.**
The back-EMF is proportional to the motor's angular velocity:

$$E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$V_a = I_a R_a + K_b \omega$$

**Step 4 — Solve for armature current:**

$$I_a = \frac{V_a - K_b \omega}{R_a}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the torque-current relationship:**
The electromagnetic torque is proportional to armature current:

$$T_m = K_t \cdot I_a$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $I_a$:**

$$T_m = \frac{K_t \left(V_a - K_b \omega\right)}{R_a}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the no-load speed** ($\omega_{NL}$, when $T_m = 0$):

$$\omega_{NL} = \frac{V_a}{K_b}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for the stall torque** ($T_{stall}$, when $\omega = 0$):

$$T_{stall} = \frac{K_t V_a}{R_a}$$

> **Interpretation:** The torque–speed characteristic is a straight line from $(0, T_{stall})$ to $(\omega_{NL}, 0)$. The operating point is found by intersecting this line with the load line.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hydraulic Cylinder Force and Power

**Step 1 — Pressure definition.** Pressure is force per unit area:

$$P = \frac{F}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = P \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Cylinder cross-section area** (piston of diameter $D$):

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Therefore the thrust force:**

$$F = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Volume flow rate into the cylinder:**

$$Q = A \cdot v \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

**Step 5 — Hydraulic power (two equivalent forms):**

$$P_{hyd} = F \cdot v = (P \cdot A) \cdot \left(\frac{Q}{A}\right) = P \cdot Q$$

So the **power balance identity** is:

$$P_{hyd} = F \cdot v = P \cdot Q$$

> **Units check:** $[P] = \text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$; $[Q] = \text{m}^3/\text{s}$; $P \cdot Q$ has units of $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}/\text{s} = \text{W}$. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: Time-of-Flight Distance

**Step 1 — Wave travels from sensor to target and back.** Total path length = $2d$.

**Step 2 — Speed of wave** $c$ is constant. Time elapsed:

$$t = \frac{2d}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for distance:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.** For an ultrasonic sensor ($c_{sound} \approx 343$ m/s at 20°C) measuring a round-trip time of $t = 6$ ms:

$$d = \frac{343 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.058}{2} = 1.029 \text{ m}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Actuator Selection

| Parameter | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Source** | Electrical energy | Pressurized oil | Compressed air |
| **Power-to-Weight Ratio** | Medium | **Very High** | Low |
| **Precision** | **Very High** | High | Low |
| **Speed of Response** | Fast | Medium | **Very Fast** |
| **Stiffness / Position Holding** | High | **Very High** | Low (compressible) |
| **Cleanliness** | **Clean** | Risk of oil leak | **Very Clean** |
| **Operating Pressure** | N/A | 50–200 bar | 4–8 bar |
| **Typical Application** | Assembly, welding | Heavy lifting, forging | Packaging, pick-and-place |
| **Cost** | Medium–High | **High (initial)** | **Low** |
| **Maintenance** | Low | High (seals, filters) | Low |
| **Example Robot** | SCARA, PUMA | Cincinnati T3, Caterpillar | Pick-and-place, food industry |

## 3.5 Sensor Selection Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Contact Type | Non-Contact Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Response Time** | Slower (mechanical) | **Faster** |
| **Wear & Tear** | Yes (limited life) | **No contact wear** |
| **Detection Range** | Touch only | **mm to meters** |
| **Suitable for Soft/Delicate Objects** | **Yes** (controlled force) | Yes (no contact) |
| **Cost** | Low (limit switch) | Medium–High (vision/LIDAR) |
| **Examples** | Limit switch, FSR, tactile array | IR, ultrasonic, LIDAR, camera |
| **Robustness to Environment** | Moderate | **High** (sealed sensors) |
| **Hysteresis** | Often present | Minimal (in some types) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — External Sensor Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Sensors] --> B[Contact Type]
    A --> C[Non-Contact Type]
    B --> B1[Limit Switch]
    B --> B2[Tactile Sensor]
    B --> B3[Force Torque Sensor]
    B --> B4[Touch Slip Sensor]
    B1 --> B1a[Mechanical SPDT]
    B2 --> B2a[Resistive FSR]
    B2 --> B2b[Capacitive Array]
    B2 --> B2c[Piezoelectric]
    B3 --> B3a[Wrist Mounted 6 Axis]
    B3 --> B3b[Strain Gauge Based]
    C --> C1[Proximity Sensor]
    C --> C2[Range Sensor]
    C --> C3[Vision System]
    C --> C4[Ultrasonic Sensor]
    C1 --> C1a[Inductive]
    C1 --> C1b[Capacitive]
    C1 --> C1c[Hall Effect]
    C2 --> C2a[Laser Triangulation]
    C2 --> C2b[Time of Flight ToF]
    C2 --> C2c[LIDAR]
    C3 --> C3a[2D CCD Camera]
    C3 --> C3b[Stereo Vision]
    C3 --> C3c[Structured Light]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Actuator Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Actuators] --> B[Electric]
    A --> C[Hydraulic]
    A --> D[Pneumatic]
    B --> B1[DC Servo Motor]
    B --> B2[Stepper Motor]
    B --> B3[AC BLDC Servo]
    B --> B4[Solenoid]
    C --> C1[Linear Cylinder]
    C --> C2[Rotary Motor]
    C --> C3[Servo Valve Controlled]
    C1 --> C1a[Single Acting]
    C1 --> C1b[Double Acting]
    D --> D1[Linear Cylinder]
    D --> D2[Rotary Motor]
    D --> D3[Muscle Air Bag]
    D1 --> D1a[Single Acting]
    D1 --> D1b[Double Acting]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Force/Torque Sensor Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Force F] --> B[Flexure Element Deformation]
    B --> C[Strain Gauges Bridge]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioner Amplifier]
    D --> E[ADC Converter]
    E --> F[Microprocessor]
    F --> G[Robot Controller]
    G --> H[Actuator Command]
    H --> I[Motor / Hydraulic Servo]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Time-of-Flight Distance Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Controller Trigger] --> B[Pulse Generator]
    B --> C[Emitter LED Laser Ultrasonic]
    C --> D[Target Object]
    D --> E[Reflected Wave]
    E --> F[Receiver Detector]
    F --> G[Time Measurement Circuit]
    G --> H[Computation d = c t / 2]
    H --> I[Distance Output to Robot]
```

## 4.5 Decision Flow — Actuator Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Selection] --> Q1{Payload greater than 100 kg?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Environment Outdoor Dirty?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Precision required less than 0.1 mm?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> HYD[Choose Hydraulic Actuator]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Clean room required?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ELE[Choose Electric Servo Stepper]
    Q3 -- No --> Q5{Cycle time less than 1 second?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> ELE
    Q4 -- No --> HYD
    Q5 -- Yes --> PNE[Choose Pneumatic Actuator]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Cost sensitive low budget?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> PNE
    Q6 -- No --> ELE
```

> **Reading the diagrams:** The flowcharts act as a **mental decision tree**. In the KTU viva, you can redraw this with chalk to show a recruiter/examiner that you understand *when* to choose which technology.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1]**

**Q1. Differentiate between contact and non-contact type sensors used in robotics. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Contact Type | Non-Contact Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Requires physical touch with object | Senses object without touch |
| **Working Principle** | Mechanical deflection, strain, pressure | Electromagnetic, optical, acoustic waves |
| **Examples** | Limit switch, Force/Torque sensor, Tactile sensor | Proximity sensor, Ultrasonic, Vision, IR |
| **Wear & Tear** | Yes (limited life) | No contact wear |
| **Range** | At point of contact | mm to meters |

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2]**

**Q2. List the three classifications of robot actuators. State one advantage and one disadvantage of hydraulic actuators.**

**Model Answer:**

The three classifications of robot actuators are:
1. **Electric actuators** (DC, stepper, servo, BLDC)
2. **Hydraulic actuators** (using pressurized oil)
3. **Pneumatic actuators** (using compressed air)

**Hydraulic actuators:**
- **Advantage:** Very high power-to-weight ratio; capable of lifting very heavy payloads (several hundred kg to tonnes).
- **Disadvantage:** Risk of oil leakage, requires pumps and filters, higher maintenance cost, not suitable for clean environments.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT DO THESE:**
> 1. **Don't confuse "contact" with "internal" sensors.** Limit switch is *contact-external*; encoder is *internal-proprioceptive*.
> 2. **Don't write "hydraulic and pneumatic are the same."** Pneumatic uses compressible air (low pressure, fast, compliant); hydraulic uses incompressible oil (high pressure, slow, stiff). Marks are routinely lost here.
> 3. **In the formula $F = P \cdot A$, always specify units:** P in Pa (or N/m²), A in m², F in N.
> 4. **For stepper motors, mention "open-loop control" explicitly** — this is the key KTU buzzword.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *(Optional Choice 1)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Q A]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the working principle and types of **contact-type external sensors** used in robotics. Discuss the construction of a **6-axis force/torque sensor** with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** With a functional block diagram, explain the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle of non-contact distance measurement. A robot's ultrasonic sensor measures a round-trip time of **4 ms**. Compute the distance to the object. Assume speed of sound $c = 340$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Working Principle of Contact Sensors:**
Contact-type external sensors require the robot's end-effector (or a probe) to physically touch the object/environment. The contact produces a measurable physical change — mechanical displacement, strain, or pressure — that is transduced into an electrical signal.

**Types of Contact Sensors:**

1. **Limit Switches (Microswitches):**
   - A spring-loaded lever/plunger is deflected by the object.
   - Snap-action SPDT contacts open/close a circuit → binary output.
   - Application: End-of-travel limits, gripper closure detection.

2. **Tactile Sensors:**
   - Array of pressure-sensitive "taxels" arranged in a matrix.
   - **Resistive (FSR):** Resistance $\downarrow$ as force $\uparrow$.
   - **Capacitive:** Capacitance changes with compression.
   - **Piezoelectric:** Voltage generated by mechanical stress.
   - **Optical:** Light coupling changes with deformation.

3. **Force/Torque Sensors:** *(Discussed below)*

4. **Touch / Slip Sensors:** Detect micro-vibrations for incipient slip detection.

**6-Axis Force/Torque Sensor:**

- **Mounting:** Located at the **wrist** between the last link and the end-effector.
- **Construction:** A machined **flexure element** (cross-shaped or Maltese-cross) with strain gauges bonded on its deformable beams. Typically 4 beams, each instrumented with multiple strain gauges.
- **Working:** External forces $(F_x, F_y, F_z)$ and moments $(M_x, M_y, M_z)$ deform the flexure. Strain gauges in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration produce voltage outputs proportional to the deformation.
- **Decoding:** A microprocessor applies the calibration matrix to extract the **6-component wrench** from the strain gauge readings.

**Block Diagram:** (As shown in Section 4.3 — Flexure → Strain Gauges → Amplifier → ADC → Processor → Controller → Actuator)

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Time-of-Flight (ToF) Principle:**

The sensor emits a wave pulse (ultrasonic sound / laser light / IR). The wave propagates, strikes the target, and is reflected back to a receiver. The total round-trip time $t$ is measured. Distance is computed as:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Block Diagram:** (As shown in Section 4.4 — Trigger → Pulse Generator → Emitter → Target → Reflected → Receiver → Timer → Computation → Output)

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $t = 4 \text{ ms} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$, $c = 340$ m/s.

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{340 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{1.36}{2}$$

$$\boxed{d = 0.68 \text{ m}}$$

The object is located **0.68 m** away from the sensor.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *(Optional Choice 2)*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Q B]**

> **(a)** Classify **robot actuators** with a neat block diagram. Compare electric, hydraulic, and pneumatic actuators on the basis of: power source, power-to-weight ratio, precision, and typical application. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A **double-acting hydraulic cylinder** has a piston diameter $D = 50$ mm and a rod diameter $d = 25$ mm. The supply pressure is $P = 100$ bar. The cylinder extends against a load of $F_L = 8$ kN. Determine:
>   (i) The piston area during extension and retraction.
>   (ii) The effective thrust force during extension and retraction.
>   (iii) Whether the cylinder can move the load.
>   **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Classification of Robot Actuators:**

Robot actuators are classified based on the energy source used to produce mechanical motion:

1. **Electric Actuators:** Electrical energy → mechanical motion.
   - DC servo motors, stepper motors, AC/BLDC servo motors, solenoids.
2. **Hydraulic Actuators:** Pressurized oil (incompressible fluid) → mechanical motion.
   - Linear hydraulic cylinders, rotary hydraulic motors, servo valves.
3. **Pneumatic Actuators:** Compressed air (compressible gas) → mechanical motion.
   - Pneumatic cylinders, air motors, muscle (McKibben) actuators.

**Block Diagram:** (As shown in Section 4.2 — Robot Actuators → Electric / Hydraulic / Pneumatic → Sub-classes)

**Comparison Table:**

| Basis | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Source** | Electricity | Pressurized oil (50–200 bar) | Compressed air (4–8 bar) |
| **Power-to-Weight Ratio** | Medium | **Very High** | Low |
| **Precision** | **Very High** (sub-mm) | High | Low (compressible) |
| **Typical Application** | SCARA, articulated, assembly robots | Heavy-payload, mining, military | Pick-and-place, packaging, food |

**Key takeaway:** Electric actuators dominate *precision robotics*; hydraulic dominates *heavy-duty*; pneumatic dominates *high-speed low-cost* automation.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Piston diameter: $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- Rod diameter: $d = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m
- Supply pressure: $P = 100$ bar $= 100 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 10^7$ Pa
- Load force: $F_L = 8$ kN $= 8000$ N

**(i) Piston Areas:**

Full (annular) area for **extension** (pressure acts on the full piston face):

$$A_e = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4}$$

$$A_e = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

Annular area for **retraction** (pressure acts on the rod-side, minus the rod cross-section):

$$A_r = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05^2 - 0.025^2)}{4}$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi (0.0025 - 0.000625)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.001875}{4}$$

$$A_r = 1.4726 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 1472.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

**(ii) Effective Thrust Forces:**

During **extension:**

$$F_e = P \cdot A_e = 10^7 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_e = 19635 \text{ N} \approx 19.635 \text{ kN}$$

During **retraction:**

$$F_r = P \cdot A_r = 10^7 \times 1.4726 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_r = 14726 \text{ N} \approx 14.726 \text{ kN}$$

**(iii) Can the cylinder move the load?**

During extension: $F_e = 19.635$ kN $> F_L = 8$ kN  ✓
During retraction: $F_r = 14.726$ kN $> F_L = 8$ kN  ✓

$$\boxed{\text{Yes, the cylinder can move the load in both directions.}}$$

The cylinder has a comfortable **force margin** of more than $80\%$ in both directions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔖 Must-Memorize Bullet Points for KTU Module 2 — Sensors & Actuators:**

### Sensors
- ✅ **Contact sensors** = physical touch required; **Non-contact** = no touch (EM wave, sound, light, magnetic).
- ✅ **Limit switch** is the simplest contact sensor — SPDT, binary output.
